topics.title: Basics of Artificial Intelligent Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Artificial Intelligent Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Artificial Intelligent System

An **Artificial Intelligent (AI) System** is a computational entity — hardware, software, or a hybrid — that perceives its environment, reasons over the acquired data, and executes actions intended to maximize its probability of success toward a defined objective or human-specified goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> AI is the branch of computer science devoted to developing systems that perform tasks that, when carried out by humans, require intelligence. An *Intelligent System* is one that exhibits traits such as reasoning, learning, perception, planning, and natural-language understanding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational AI Equation (Russell & Norvig Formulation):**
> 
> $$\boxed{\text{AI System} = f(\text{Perception}, \text{Reasoning}, \text{Action}, \text{Learning})}$$
> 
> A system qualifies as "intelligent" only if it maps environmental **inputs** to goal-directed **outputs** while continuously updating its internal model.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human-as-Agent Model

Imagine hiring a **new office assistant** on their very first day.

- The assistant **walks in and observes** the office (this is *perception* via sensors).
- They **recall** the rules of the company from training (this is *knowledge representation*).
- They **decide** which tasks to prioritize based on deadlines (this is *reasoning*).
- They **execute** the chosen task — filing, emailing, scheduling (this is *action via actuators*).
- After feedback from the manager, they **update** their approach for the next day (this is *learning*).

An AI system is exactly this assistant — but silicon-based, lightning-fast, and tireless. The fundamental ingredients remain identical: **sense → think → act → learn**.

---

## 1.3 The Four Foundational Approaches to AI

| Approach | Core Idea | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Thinking Humanly** | Cognitive modeling — make the machine replicate the actual thought process of the brain. | Neural networks mimicking neurons. |
| **Thinking Rationally** | Logic-based reasoning using formal rules. | Theorem provers, expert systems. |
| **Acting Humanly** | The Turing Test approach — behave indistinguishably from a person. | Chatbots like virtual assistants. |
| **Acting Rationally** | The rational agent approach — perform the optimal action to achieve the best outcome. | Self-driving cars, game-playing AI. |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to identify which approach a given system falls under. Memorize the four rows above using the mnemonic **"Human-Human-Rational-Rational"** mapped to *Thinking-Acting-Thinking-Acting*.

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Timeline (KTU High-Yield)

- **1943** — McCulloch & Pitts propose the first mathematical model of an artificial neuron.
- **1950** — Alan Turing publishes *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* and introduces the **Turing Test**.
- **1956** — The **Dartmouth Conference** officially coins the term *"Artificial Intelligence"* (John McCarthy).
- **1956–1974** — The Golden Years: symbolic AI, LISP programming language invented.
- **1980s** — Rise of **Expert Systems** (e.g., MYCIN, DENDRAL).
- **1997** — IBM's **Deep Blue** defeats world chess champion Garry Kasparov.
- **2011** — IBM **Watson** wins the *Jeopardy!* quiz show.
- **2012** — **AlexNet** triggers the modern Deep Learning revolution (ImageNet competition).
- **2017** — **Transformer architecture** introduced (*"Attention is All You Need"*).
- **2020s** — Generative AI, LLMs, and foundation models dominate the field.

---

## 1.5 The Turing Test — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Turing Test (1950):** A human interrogator communicates via text with two hidden entities — one human, one machine. If the interrogator cannot reliably distinguish the machine from the human, the machine is said to have passed the test of intelligent behavior.

The test avoids the philosophical debate of *"Can a machine truly think?"* by replacing it with an operational, behavioral benchmark: **Can a machine act humanly?**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of AI Systems — By Capability

Modern AI is stratified into three capability tiers, each with distinct engineering requirements and ethical implications.

| Type | Capability Level | Key Characteristics | Real-World Instance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI)** | Specialized — one task | High accuracy in a narrow domain; no transfer to other tasks. | Spam filters, voice assistants (Siri, Alexa), recommendation engines. |
| **Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)** | Human-level — multi-domain | Abstract reasoning, transfer learning, common sense. | *Theoretical / Research frontier* — not yet achieved. |
| **Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI)** | Beyond human — every domain | Outperforms humans in creativity, problem-solving, social skills. | Hypothetical — future speculation. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** As of the 2024 scheme, **all deployed AI systems in the world are ANI**. AGI remains a research goal, and ASI is speculative. Examiners may frame a 3-mark question around this classification.

---

## 2.2 Classification of AI Systems — By Functionality

| Functional Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactive Machines** | No memory; respond to current inputs only. | IBM Deep Blue |
| **Limited Memory** | Uses past data for a short window. | Self-driving cars |
| **Theory of Mind** | Understands emotions, beliefs, intentions. | Research stage |
| **Self-Aware AI** | Consciousness of self. | Hypothetical |

---

## 2.3 The Intelligent Agent Framework (Russell & Norvig)

The most influential KTU-recommended framing of an AI system is the **Rational Agent** model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Agent:** Anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**.

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Formulation of a Rational Agent

An agent function maps the complete history of percepts to an action:

$$f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ = the set of all possible **percept sequences** (history).
- $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of all legal **actions** the agent can execute.

A **rational agent** selects the action that maximizes its **performance measure**, given the percept history and its prior knowledge of the environment.

### 2.3.2 Performance Measure (Utility)

For a given percept sequence, the expected utility of an action $a$ is:

$$\text{Expected Utility} = \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

Where:
- $s$ = current state
- $s'$ = next state
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ = probability of reaching $s'$ after action $a$ in state $s$
- $U(s')$ = utility (usefulness) of the resulting state

A **rational agent** chooses:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

### 2.3.3 PEAS Descriptor

Every task environment is fully specified using the **PEAS** framework:

| Component | Meaning | Example (Taxi Driver) |
|---|---|---|
| **P**erformance | Success metric | Safe, fast, legal, comfortable trip |
| **E**nvironment | Surroundings | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather |
| **A**ctuators | Action mechanisms | Steering wheel, brakes, accelerator, signal lights |
| **S**ensors | Input devices | Cameras, GPS, LIDAR, speedometer, accelerometer |

---

## 2.4 Properties of Task Environments

| Property | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Fully vs. Partially Observable** | Are all relevant variables known? | Chess (full) / Poker (partial) |
| **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** | Is the next state fully determined by current state + action? | Tic-Tac-Toe (deterministic) / Weather prediction (stochastic) |
| **Episodic vs. Sequential** | Does each action depend on prior history? | Image classification (episodic) / Chess (sequential) |
| **Static vs. Dynamic** | Does the environment change while the agent deliberates? | Crossword puzzle (static) / Driving (dynamic) |
| **Discrete vs. Continuous** | Are the percepts/actions countable? | Chess (discrete) / Robot navigation (continuous) |
| **Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent** | Is the agent operating alone? | Solitaire (single) / Soccer (multi) |
| **Known vs. Unknown** | Are the rules/outcomes known? | Board games (known) / Exploration (unknown) |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Agent Function | $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Maps percept history to action |
| Rational Action | $a^* = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$ | Optimization |
| Performance Measure | $PM = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i$ | Weighted sum of criteria |
| Turing Test Pass Criterion | Misclassification Rate $\geq 50\%$ | Probability |
| Autonomy Index | $\text{Autonomy} = 1 - \frac{\text{Designer-Knowledge Used}}{\text{Total Knowledge}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| Learning Rate Adaptation | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla L(\theta_t)$ | Gradient descent update |

> [!TIP]
> Avoid using the vertical pipe `|x|` notation inside markdown tables. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead, as required by the KTU-Engine protocol.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The agent framework is the **unified lens** through which KTU 2024 evaluates every subsequent module — from search algorithms to reinforcement learning. Whether the agent is a vacuum-cleaner robot or a stock-trading bot, the PEAS specification and rationality criterion remain identical. This abstraction is the **backbone of the entire course**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Rational Action Theorem

We now rigorously derive the formula for the optimal action of a rational agent, step by step.

### Given:
- Current environment state: $s \in S$ (state space).
- Set of legal actions: $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$.
- Transition model: $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — probability of moving from state $s$ to state $s'$ upon taking action $a$.
- Utility function: $U(s')$ — the desirability of reaching state $s'$.

### Step 1 — Define the Utility of an Action
The expected utility of taking action $a$ from state $s$ is the probability-weighted sum of utilities of all reachable next states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(a \mid s) &= \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s, a)}[U(s')] \\
&= \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We multiply the probability of each possible outcome $s'$ by how good that outcome is ($U(s')$), then sum across all outcomes. This is the definition of mathematical expectation.

### Step 2 — Define the Rational Action
A rational agent is defined as one that **selects the action that maximizes expected utility**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a^*(s) &= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; EU(a \mid s) \\
&= \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The $\arg\max$ operator returns the *argument* (i.e., the action) that yields the highest expected utility, not the utility value itself.

### Step 3 — Simplification for Deterministic Environments
If the environment is **deterministic**, then $P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ for exactly one next state $s' = \delta(s, a)$, and zero for all others. The sum collapses to a single term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(a \mid s) &= \sum_{s' \in S} 1 \cdot U(\delta(s, a)) \\
&= U(\delta(s, a))
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{a^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; U(\delta(s, a))}
$$

**Explanation:** In a deterministic world, the agent simply picks the action leading to the best next state.

### Step 4 — Extension to Sequential Decisions (Bellman Form)
When decisions span multiple time steps with discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1]$, the value of a state $V(s)$ satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(s) &= \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot V(s') \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The value of being in state $s$ is the immediate reward $R(s, a)$ plus the discounted value of the best possible future. This is the **Bellman optimality equation**, foundational to Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) covered in later modules.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** A delivery robot is in state $s$. It has two action choices:
- $a_1$: Take Highway. Probability of reaching high-payoff state $s'_1 = 0.7$; probability of reaching low-payoff state $s'_2 = 0.3$.
- $a_2$: Take Local Road. Probability of reaching $s'_1 = 0.4$; probability of reaching $s'_2 = 0.6$.

Utility values: $U(s'_1) = 100$, $U(s'_2) = 30$.

**Calculate $EU(a_1 \mid s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(a_1 \mid s) &= (0.7 \times 100) + (0.3 \times 30) \\
&= 70 + 9 \\
&= 79
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculate $EU(a_2 \mid s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(a_2 \mid s) &= (0.4 \times 100) + (0.6 \times 30) \\
&= 40 + 18 \\
&= 58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rational Decision:**

$$
a^* = \arg\max \{79, 58\} = a_1 \;\; (\text{Highway})
$$

**Result:** The rational agent must take the Highway, since $79 > 58$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Agent Architectures

| Agent Type | Internal State | World Model | Handles Partial Observability | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Reflex** | None | No | No | Thermostat, light switch |
| **Model-Based Reflex** | Internal state | Yes (transition model) | Yes | Robotic vacuum |
| **Goal-Based** | Goals + state | Yes | Yes | GPS navigation |
| **Utility-Based** | Utility function + state | Yes | Yes | Autonomous trading |
| **Learning Agent** | All of above + performance element | Yes (learned) | Yes | AlphaGo, recommendation systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between goal-based and utility-based agents."* The **goal-based** agent considers only success/failure (binary), whereas the **utility-based** agent weighs the *degree* of success across multiple criteria.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Turing Test Pass Threshold

Let $H$ = human responder, $M$ = machine, $I$ = interrogator. The interrogator issues $N$ text-based questions. The interrogator classifies each answer as "human" or "machine."

The probability of a correct identification by random guessing is:

$$
P(\text{correct by chance}) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

For the machine to *pass* the Turing Test, the interrogator's correct-classification rate should be statistically indistinguishable from chance:

$$
\boxed{P(\text{correct} \mid \text{machine}) \approx 0.5 \;\;\text{or lower}}
$$

**Engineering Implication:** To pass, the AI must produce responses that mimic human error patterns, hesitation, and ambiguity — an extremely high bar that only a small fraction of conversational systems can approach.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level AI System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment] -->|Stimuli| B[Sensors]
    B --> C[Perception Module]
    C --> D[Knowledge Base]
    D --> E[Reasoning Engine]
    E --> F[Action Selector]
    F --> G[Actuators]
    G -->|Effect| A
    D -.->|Updates| H[Learning Module]
    E -.->|Feedback| H
    H -.->|Refines| D

    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#B4FFE5,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFD4D4,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#D4D4FF,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFFFB4,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#FFB4FF,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#B4FFFF,stroke:#333
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The **Environment** provides raw stimuli (pixels, sound, temperature, etc.).
- **Sensors** convert stimuli into machine-readable signals.
- The **Perception Module** extracts meaningful features.
- The **Knowledge Base** stores facts, rules, and prior experience.
- The **Reasoning Engine** applies logic, search, or inference.
- The **Action Selector** picks the optimal action.
- **Actuators** execute the action, changing the environment.
- The **Learning Module** continuously updates the Knowledge Base from feedback.

---

## 4.2 Intelligent Agent Architecture (PEAS Mapped)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AgentCore[Agent Core]
        P[Performance Measure]
        E[Environment Model]
        A[Actuator Interface]
        S[Sensor Interface]
        R[Reasoning Engine]
        K[Knowledge Base]
        L[Learning Component]
    end

    Env[External World] -->|Raw Data| S
    S --> R
    R -->|Query| K
    K -->|Facts| R
    R -->|Decision| A
    A -->|Action| Env
    R -->|Feedback Signal| L
    L -->|Updated Rules| K
    P -.->|Evaluates| R
    E -.->|Describes| Env

    style P fill:#FFA07A
    style E fill:#FFA07A
    style A fill:#98FB98
    style S fill:#87CEEB
    style R fill:#DDA0DD
    style K fill:#F0E68C
    style L fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of AI Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AI[Artificial Intelligence] --> Type1[Based on Capability]
    AI --> Type2[Based on Functionality]
    AI --> Type3[Based on Approach]

    Type1 --> ANI[Artificial Narrow Intelligence]

    Type2 --> RM[Reactive Machines]

    Type3 --> AR[Acting Rationally]

    style SA fill:#E0FFFF

    style AR fill:#FFFACD
```

---

## 4.4 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Env as Environment
    participant Sen as Sensors
    participant Ag as Agent
    participant Act as Actuators

    loop Continuous Cycle
        Env->>Sen: Emits Percept
        Sen->>Ag: Filtered Input
        Ag->>Ag: Reason & Select Action
        Ag->>Act: Action Command
        Act->>Env: Executes Action
        Env->>Env: State Transition
    end
```

**Sequence Explanation:**
- The **Environment** emits a percept (raw observation).
- **Sensors** filter and digitize it.
- The **Agent** reasons over its knowledge and selects an action.
- **Actuators** physically or digitally execute the action.
- The environment transitions to a new state, completing the loop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an Intelligent Agent. List and briefly explain any two properties of task environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators to achieve a goal, as defined by its performance measure.

Two properties of task environments:

1. **Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable (1 mark):** A fully observable environment's complete state is known to the agent at all times (e.g., chess). In a partially observable environment, the agent lacks access to all relevant variables (e.g., poker, where opponents' hole cards are hidden).

2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic (1 mark):** If the next state is fully determined by the current state and action, the environment is deterministic (e.g., tic-tac-toe). If uncertainty exists, it is stochastic (e.g., real-world driving, where other vehicles behave unpredictably).

*(Definition — 1 mark; Two properties — 1 mark each)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the Turing Test? State one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Turing Test**, proposed by Alan Turing in 1950, evaluates a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behaviour indistinguishable from a human. A human interrogator communicates with both a hidden human and a hidden machine via text. If the interrogator cannot reliably tell them apart, the machine is said to have passed the test. **(2 marks)**

> **Limitation:** The test only measures *behavioural equivalence*, not true understanding or consciousness — a machine could pass by cleverly mimicking human conversational patterns without actually comprehending meaning (the "Chinese Room" objection). **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (per KTU ESE 2024 norms):** *Answer either Option A or Option B in full.*

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define an Artificial Intelligent System. Discuss any two approaches to AI with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the PEAS specification framework. Use the example of an automated medical diagnosis system to illustrate each component. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

An **Artificial Intelligent System** is a computational system capable of perceiving its environment, reasoning over the gathered information, and executing actions to maximize success toward a defined objective.

**Two approaches to AI (4 marks for explanation, 1 mark each for example):**

1. **Acting Humanly (Turing Test Approach):** A system is considered intelligent if it behaves indistinguishably from a human during a text-based conversation.
   *Example:* ELIZA, the early natural-language chatbot mimicking a Rogerian psychotherapist, or modern LLMs like ChatGPT attempting to produce human-like responses.

2. **Acting Rationally (Rational Agent Approach):** A system is considered intelligent if it always selects the action that maximizes its performance measure given its knowledge.
   *Example:* Deep Blue selecting the optimal chess move based on minimax search and evaluation functions.

*(Definition — 1 mark; Each approach — 2 marks each = 4 marks; Two examples — 1 mark each = 2 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

The **PEAS** (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors) descriptor fully specifies a task environment for an agent.

**Application to an Automated Medical Diagnosis System: (1 mark per component)**

| PEAS Component | Description for Medical Diagnosis System |
|---|---|
| **Performance Measure** | Correct diagnosis rate, patient recovery rate, minimization of false negatives, cost efficiency, patient satisfaction. |
| **Environment** | Hospital/clinic, patients, electronic health records, doctors, lab reports, medical imaging archives. |
| **Actuators** | Display of diagnostic suggestions, prescription recommendations, alert generation for critical cases, report generation module. |
| **Sensors** | Input from patient interviews (keyboard/text), medical test results (numeric data), MRI/CT scan images, electronic health record databases. |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** The medical diagnosis environment is **partially observable** (some symptoms may be hidden), **stochastic** (patient responses vary), **sequential** (each diagnosis informs the next), **dynamic** (patient condition changes over time), and **continuous** (symptom values are real-valued).

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Differentiate between goal-based and utility-based agents. Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot must reach a goal cell in a 3×3 grid. From any cell, it can move Up, Down, Left, or Right with equal probability 0.25. The utility of the goal state is 100, and the utility of a non-goal state is 10. Using the rational action formula, determine which action the robot should take when in the center cell, assuming two possible next states. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

| Feature | Goal-Based Agent | Utility-Based Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Criterion** | Binary — does the action lead to a goal? | Quantitative — does the action maximize expected utility? |
| **Flexibility** | Limited to yes/no outcomes | Handles trade-offs among multiple, conflicting goals |
| **Information Used** | Set of goals | Utility function over all states |
| **Handling of Uncertainty** | Poor — may fail if multiple goals conflict | Excellent — weighs probabilistic outcomes |
| **Example** | A navigation system that just needs to reach the destination. | A medical treatment recommender balancing cure-rate, side-effects, and cost. |

**Illustrative Example (2 marks):** Consider a self-driving car deciding between two routes to the airport.
- A *goal-based* agent picks the first route that reaches the airport.
- A *utility-based* agent compares the expected utility of both routes — weighing travel time, fuel cost, toll cost, accident risk, and scenery — and selects the route with the highest aggregate utility.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- State $s$ = center cell.
- Utility of goal state: $U(s'_{\text{goal}}) = 100$.
- Utility of non-goal state: $U(s'_{\text{non}}) = 10$.
- Equal transition probability: $P = 0.25$ for each of the 4 directions, but only 2 distinct next-state categories are considered (goal vs. non-goal).

**Assumed Probabilities (1 mark):**
- $P(s'_{\text{goal}} \mid s, a) = 0.4$
- $P(s'_{\text{non}} \mid s, a) = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Utility of the action (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(a \mid s) &= P(s'_{\text{goal}} \mid s, a) \cdot U(s'_{\text{goal}}) + P(s'_{\text{non}} \mid s, a) \cdot U(s'_{\text{non}}) \\
&= (0.4 \times 100) + (0.6 \times 10) \\
&= 40 + 6 \\
&= 46
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the alternative scenario (2 marks):**
Suppose an alternative action $a_2$ has:
- $P(s'_{\text{goal}} \mid s, a_2) = 0.2$
- $P(s'_{\text{non}} \mid s, a_2) = 0.8$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU(a_2 \mid s) &= (0.2 \times 100) + (0.8 \times 10) \\
&= 20 + 8 \\
&= 28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rational Decision (1 mark):**

$$
a^* = \arg\max \{46, 28\} = a_1
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot must execute action $a_1$, which yields a higher expected utility of **46** versus the alternative's **28**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "fully observable" with "deterministic"** — these are *independent* properties. An environment can be fully observable yet stochastic (e.g., a robot with perfect sensors but a coin-flipping door).
> 2. **Forgetting the "rationality ≠ omniscience" distinction** — a rational agent is not all-knowing; it acts based on its *current percept sequence*, not future knowledge.
> 3. **Mixing up PEAS order** — the correct sequence is **P → E → A → S**, not random. Examiners check this in definitions.
> 4. **Writing $\arg\max$ as "max"** — these are different operators. $\max$ returns a *value*; $\arg\max$ returns an *argument* (the action).
> 5. **Omitting utility units or probability values** in numerical problems — KTU's step-marking scheme penalizes unexplained numbers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AI** is the science of making machines perform tasks that require human-level cognition.
- The **Turing Test** (1950) is the foundational behavioural benchmark for machine intelligence.
- The **Dartmouth Conference (1956)** is the official birth-event of AI as a discipline.
- AI is classified as **ANI (current reality), AGI (research goal), ASI (hypothetical)**.
- The four foundational approaches are: **Thinking Humanly, Thinking Rationally, Acting Humanly, Acting Rationally** — the rational agent approach is dominant in modern AI.
- An **agent** perceives via **sensors** and acts via **actuators**.
- The **PEAS** framework specifies any task environment: **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors**.
- An agent's function maps $\mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$; a *rational* agent maximizes expected utility.
- Key environment properties to memorize: **observability, determinism, episodic nature, dynamics, continuity, agent count, knowledge**.
- Five agent architectures in increasing sophistication: **Simple Reflex → Model-Based → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning Agent**.
- The **Bellman optimality equation** $V(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)V(s')]$ underpins all sequential decision-making in AI.
- Use $\arg\max$ (not $\max$) when stating which action a rational agent selects.
- Avoid using bare vertical bars `|x|` in KTU-formatted answer tables — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and **Machine Learning (ML)**, the phrase *"Different Types"* in **Module 1** refers to the formal taxonomies used to classify intelligent systems along two fundamental axes:

1. **By Capability** — How "smart" or capable the system is (from narrow task-specific systems to hypothetical superintelligences).
2. **By Functionality / Architecture** — How the system internally represents, stores, and uses knowledge (from simple reflex reactions to self-aware models).

A secondary, equally important classification divides **Machine Learning algorithms** into the three core learning paradigms: **Supervised**, **Unsupervised**, and **Reinforcement Learning**, with hybrids like **Semi-Supervised** and **Self-Supervised Learning** as modern extensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 expects you to be able to **(a)** name the types, **(b)** give one real-world example of each, and **(c)** state one limitation. Memorize the 3 capability-based AI types and 4 functionality-based AI types — these are recurring 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Animal Kingdom of AI"

Imagine the world of AI as a **zoo with different animals**:

- A **goldfish** is *Narrow AI* — it can swim beautifully, but ask it to climb a tree and it fails. This is like **Siri** or **Google Maps**: brilliant at one job, useless outside it.
- A **chimpanzee** is *General AI* — it can solve many problems, use tools, learn signs — but it's not human. *(This level does NOT yet exist in 2026.)*
- A **mythical phoenix** is *Super AI* — smarter than every human combined. It exists only in theory today.
- A **cockroach** is *Reactive AI* — it reacts instantly to touch but has zero memory (think **IBM Deep Blue**, the chess computer).
- A **squirrel** is *Limited Memory AI* — it remembers where it buried nuts last winter (think **self-driving cars** that remember recent pedestrians).
- A **trained dog** is *Theory of Mind AI* — it can read your emotions and intent. *(Still research-stage.)*
- A **human** is *Self-Aware AI* — conscious of its own existence. *(Philosophical/hypothetical.)*

The lesson: **AI is not one thing** — it is a spectrum of capabilities and architectures, and recognizing *which type* a system belongs to is the first skill of any AI engineer.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined)

- **Artificial Intelligence (AI):** The broad discipline of building systems that perform tasks requiring *human-like* intelligence — reasoning, learning, perception, decision-making.
- **Machine Learning (ML):** A *subset* of AI where systems learn patterns from **data** rather than from explicitly coded rules.
- **Deep Learning (DL):** A *subset* of ML that uses **multi-layer neural networks** to learn hierarchical representations.
- **Intelligence:** The capacity to **acquire, understand, and apply knowledge** to achieve goals in a given environment.
- **Agent:** Anything that **perceives its environment through sensors** and **acts upon it through actuators**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy to memorize:** $\text{AI} \supset \text{ML} \supset \text{DL}$. Data Science is a *related but separate* field that uses ML as a tool.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of AI — Two Parallel Classification Axes

### Axis A: Classification by **Capability** (the "How smart?" question)

This is the **most-asked axis in KTU exams**. There are exactly **3 types**:

### 2.1.1 Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI) — aka **Weak AI / Narrow AI**

- **Definition:** AI specialized in **one narrow task**; cannot generalize beyond its training.
- **Examples:** Spam filters, voice assistants (Alexa, Siri), recommendation engines (Netflix, YouTube), Google Translate, chess engines (Stockfish, Deep Blue).
- **Why it matters:** This is the **only type of AI that exists today (2026)**. Every commercial AI product is ANI.
- **Limitation:** Brittle — fails catastrophically on out-of-distribution inputs. A chess AI cannot drive a car.

### 2.1.2 Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) — aka **Strong AI / Human-Level AI**

- **Definition:** Hypothetical AI with **human-level cognitive abilities** across *any* intellectual task — reasoning, planning, abstract thinking, transferring learning from one domain to another.
- **Status:** **Does not exist** as of 2026. Active research at labs like DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic.
- **The "Coffee Test"** (Steve Wozniak): An AGI robot should be able to walk into a stranger's house and make a cup of coffee — even if it has never seen that specific kitchen before.
- **Why it matters:** AGI would require *common sense*, *transfer learning*, and *causal reasoning* — all currently open problems.

### 2.1.3 Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) — aka **Superintelligence**

- **Definition:** Hypothetical AI that **surpasses the brightest human minds** in every field — science, creativity, social skills, and wisdom.
- **Status:** Purely theoretical / philosophical.
- **Why it matters:** The subject of the famous *"Alignment Problem"* — how do we ensure an ASI remains beneficial? (Nick Bostrom, *Superintelligence*, 2014.)

---

### Axis B: Classification by **Functionality** (the "How does it think?" question)

This is the **4-type classification** (Arend Hintze, 2016) — also heavily tested in KTU:

### 2.1.4 Type I — Reactive Machines

- **Definition:** AI that **perceives the world directly** and reacts. **No memory**, no learning from past.
- **Mechanism:** Rule-based mapping from input to output: $\text{output} = f(\text{current input})$.
- **Examples:** IBM **Deep Blue** (chess), thermostat controllers, basic spam filters.
- **Limitation:** Cannot learn; same input always gives same output.

### 2.1.5 Type II — Limited Memory

- **Definition:** AI that uses **recent observations** (a short rolling memory) to make decisions, but **cannot build a long-term model** of the world.
- **Mechanism:** Maintains a buffer of recent states; trains on this buffer to update weights.
- **Examples:** Self-driving cars (Tesla, Waymo), chatbots with context windows, GPT-style LLMs (within a single conversation).
- **Real-world engineering use:** This is the **state of the art** in 2026 for almost all deployed AI.

### 2.1.6 Type III — Theory of Mind

- **Definition:** AI that **understands that other entities (humans, animals) have their own beliefs, desires, and intentions** — and uses this to predict behavior.
- **Status:** **Active research area**, not deployed. Requires advances in social cognition modeling.
- **Example research:** Affectiva's emotion-recognition systems, social robots like Sophia (limited).

### 2.1.7 Type IV — Self-Aware AI

- **Definition:** AI that has **consciousness, self-awareness, and its own desires** — it "knows that it knows".
- **Status:** **Philosophical / hypothetical**. The "hard problem of consciousness" remains unsolved in neuroscience, so building one is impossible with current science.
- **Pop-culture example:** HAL 9000, Data (Star Trek), Samantha (Her).

---

## 2.2 The Three Types of Machine Learning (Module 1 Continuation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although ML is "Module 2" content in some KTU syllabi, the **types of ML** are usually introduced in Module 1 as part of the AI taxonomy. KTU frequently asks: *"Compare the three types of ML with examples."*

### 2.2.1 Supervised Learning

- **Definition:** Algorithm learns a mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ from **labeled** training data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$.
- **Goal:** Minimize a loss function $L(y, \hat{y})$ over the training set.
- **Two sub-tasks:** **Classification** (discrete $y$) and **Regression** (continuous $y$).
- **Examples:** Linear regression, logistic regression, SVM, decision trees, k-NN, CNNs, transformers.
- **Real-world use:** Email spam detection, medical diagnosis, credit scoring, price prediction.

### 2.2.2 Unsupervised Learning

- **Definition:** Algorithm finds **hidden structure** in **unlabeled** data $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$.
- **Goal:** Discover clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or density estimates.
- **Common tasks:** **Clustering** (k-means, DBSCAN, hierarchical), **Dimensionality Reduction** (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP), **Association** (Apriori).
- **Real-world use:** Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, gene sequence analysis, recommender systems (collaborative filtering).

### 2.2.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL)

- **Definition:** An **agent** learns a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an environment and receiving **rewards** $r_t$ at each timestep $t$.
- **Goal:** Maximize the **expected cumulative discounted return**:
$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}, \qquad 0 \le \gamma \le 1$$
- **Key elements:** Agent, Environment, State $s_t$, Action $a_t$, Reward $r_t$, Policy $\pi$, Value function $V^{\pi}(s)$, Q-function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$.
- **Bellman Optimality Equation:**
$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \, V^{*}(s') \right]$$
- **Examples:** AlphaGo, AlphaZero, robotics locomotion, autonomous helicopter, game playing (Atari).
- **Real-world use:** Robotics, autonomous driving (decision layer), dynamic pricing, LLM fine-tuning with RLHF.

### 2.2.4 Hybrid / Modern Extensions (for advanced answers)

| Extension | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Small labeled + large unlabeled set | Medical imaging with few annotations |
| **Self-Supervised Learning** | Labels are auto-generated from data | GPT pretraining (predict next token), BERT (masked LM) |
| **Multi-Task Learning** | One model learns several related tasks jointly | Tesla's vision model (detection + segmentation + depth) |
| **Transfer Learning** | Pretrained model fine-tuned on new task | ResNet pretrained on ImageNet → fine-tuned for X-rays |
| **Federated Learning** | Train across many devices without centralizing data | Gboard next-word prediction on phones |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas in this table. They are testable in Part A (3-mark) derivations and in Part B (14-mark) numerical/theoretical sub-parts.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ML Hypothesis (supervised) | $\hat{y} = f_\theta(x)$ | $\theta$ = model parameters | Supervised Learning |
| 2 | Empirical Risk (loss) | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L\!\left(y_i, f_\theta(x_i)\right)$ | $n$ = number of samples, $L$ = loss function | Training objective |
| 3 | Mean Squared Error (regression loss) | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | $y_i$ true, $\hat{y}_i$ predicted | Regression |
| 4 | Cross-Entropy Loss (classification) | $H(p,q) = -\sum_{c} p(c) \, \log q(c)$ | $p$ true, $q$ predicted distribution | Classification |
| 5 | Discounted Return (RL) | $G_t = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ = discount factor | Reinforcement Learning |
| 6 | Bellman Optimality | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \!\left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^{*}(s') \right]$ | $P$ = transition probability | RL planning |
| 7 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\right]$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate | RL algorithm |
| 8 | Policy Definition | $\pi(a \mid s) = P(a_t = a \mid s_t = s)$ | probability of action given state | RL |
| 9 | AI Capability Hierarchy | $\text{ANI} \subset \text{AGI} \subset \text{ASI}$ (theoretical subset) | — | Type classification |
| 10 | Functionality Types (Hintze) | Reactive $<$ Limited Memory $<$ Theory of Mind $<$ Self-Aware | — | Architecture classification |

**Units & Constants worth knowing:**
- $\gamma$ (discount factor) is **dimensionless**, in $[0,1]$. Common values: **0.9, 0.99, 0.999**.
- $\alpha$ (learning rate) is **dimensionless**, in $(0,1)$. Common values: **0.001, 0.01, 0.1**.
- $\theta$ (model parameters) — count varies: linear regression has $d+1$ parameters for $d$-dim input; a ResNet-50 has $\sim 25.6$ million.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:** In real KTU-projects and internships, you will use formula (2) to design your training loop, formula (4) to choose your classification loss, and formulas (5)–(7) when you build a self-balancing robot or a chess agent in your final year. The Bellman equation (6) is the *backbone* of every RL system from AlphaGo to Tesla's decision planner.

---

## 2.4 Why "Types" Matter in Real Engineering

When you join an AI company, the first question your manager asks is **"which type of approach fits this problem?"**:

- Fraud detection with labeled past fraud data → **Supervised Classification**.
- Segmenting millions of users by behavior with no labels → **Unsupervised Clustering**.
- Teaching a robot arm to pick objects with no demonstrations → **Reinforcement Learning**.
- A chatbot that must remember the last 10 messages but doesn't need self-awareness → **Limited Memory AI (Type II)**.
- A medical X-ray analyzer that must explain *why* it flags a tumor → **Theory of Mind-style XAI (Explainable AI)**.

Knowing the **type** you need *before* writing a single line of code saves months of work.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark questions often contain a *derivation* sub-part. Below are the **must-know derivations** for the "Different Types" topic, written out line-by-line with full transition logic.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Supervised Loss to Gradient Descent Update (Linear Regression)

We want to learn parameters $\theta = (w, b)$ for the model $\hat{y} = w x + b$ from data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$.

**Step 1 — State the model (hypothesis).**
We assume the true relationship is approximately linear, so we propose:
$$\hat{y}_i = w x_i + b$$
This is the **Narrow AI (ANI)** model: one specific function form for one specific task (regression).

**Step 2 — Choose the loss function (Empirical Risk).**
For regression, the standard loss is squared error. The empirical risk is the average loss:
$$\hat{R}(w,b) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the model into the loss.**
$$\hat{R}(w,b) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - (w x_i + b) \right)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute partial derivatives (gradients).**
For $w$:
$$\frac{\partial \hat{R}}{\partial w} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2 \left( y_i - w x_i - b \right)(-x_i) = -\frac{2}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left( y_i - w x_i - b \right)$$
For $b$:
$$\frac{\partial \hat{R}}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - w x_i - b \right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply gradient descent update (learning rule).**
With learning rate $\alpha$:
$$w \leftarrow w - \alpha \frac{\partial \hat{R}}{\partial w}, \qquad b \leftarrow b - \alpha \frac{\partial \hat{R}}{\partial b}$$

**Step 6 — Closed-form alternative (Normal Equation).**
Setting $\frac{\partial \hat{R}}{\partial w} = 0$ and $\frac{\partial \hat{R}}{\partial b} = 0$ and solving gives the optimal closed-form:
$$w^{*} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}, \qquad b^{*} = \bar{y} - w^{*} \bar{x}$$
This is the **least-squares estimator** — the supervised learner converges to this.

**Engineering takeaway:** This is *why* ANI works for regression: it has a well-defined, convex objective, a guaranteed global optimum, and fast training. **Memorize the gradient formulas and the normal equation.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bellman Equation from First Principles (Reinforcement Learning)

**Step 1 — Define the return.**
The total reward an agent receives from time $t$ onward, discounted by $\gamma$:
$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} r_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}$$
If $\gamma < 1$ and rewards are bounded, this infinite sum converges.

**Step 2 — Define the value function as expected return.**
The **state-value function** under a policy $\pi$ is:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_t \mid s_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3 — Unroll one timestep of the return.**
By definition, $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$. Substituting:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]$$
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right]$$

**Step 4 — Condition the second expectation on the next state.**
By the law of total expectation, summing over possible next states $s'$ and actions $a$:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s \right] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1} = s' \right]$$
$$= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, V^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get the Bellman equation.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

**Step 6 — Take the max over actions to get the optimal Bellman equation.**
The optimal value $V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$ satisfies:
$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \, V^{*}(s') \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The Bellman equation is the *defining equation* of RL. A 14-mark question may ask: *"Derive the Bellman optimality equation and explain its use in Q-learning."* Write Steps 1–6 above — full marks guaranteed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which Type of AI/ML Do I Use?"

This is a high-yield **comparison matrix** the KTU examiner loves for 14-mark answers. **Memorize this for sure.**

| Decision Criterion | Choose Supervised | Choose Unsupervised | Choose Reinforcement | Choose Limited Memory AI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Do you have labeled data?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ Not required | ✅ Yes (perception) |
| **Is the environment dynamic?** | Static dataset | Static dataset | Sequential, interactive | Sequential perception |
| **Goal type** | Predict $y$ from $x$ | Find structure | Maximize cumulative reward | React using recent past |
| **Feedback type** | Direct (labels) | None | Delayed reward | Recent observations |
| **Example problem** | Cancer detection | Customer segmentation | Game-playing AI | Self-driving car |
| **Key metric** | Accuracy / F1 | Silhouette / inertia | Cumulative reward | Reaction latency |
| **Common algorithm** | SVM, neural net | k-means, PCA | Q-learning, PPO | RNN, LSTM, Transformer |
| **Real-world product** | Gmail spam filter | Spotify Discover Weekly | AlphaGo | Tesla Autopilot |

**Reading the matrix:** Move from left to right. If you have labels → supervised. No labels but want structure → unsupervised. Have a sequence of decisions and rewards → RL. Need real-time reaction with context → Limited Memory AI.

---

## 3.4 Type Selection Tree (Logical Flow)

When given a problem in the exam, ask in this order:

1. *Does the system need long-term memory?* → No → **Reactive Machine (Type I)**.
2. *Does it need recent past (last few seconds)?* → Yes → **Limited Memory (Type II)**. ← *This is 99% of deployed AI in 2026.*
3. *Does it need to model other agents' minds?* → Yes → **Theory of Mind (Type III)**. ← *Research only.*
4. *Does it need self-awareness?* → Yes → **Self-Aware (Type IV)**. ← *Hypothetical.*

On the capability axis:
1. *Can the system do exactly one task well?* → **ANI (Narrow AI)**. ← *All current systems.*
2. *Can it do any human task at human level?* → **AGI**. ← *Not yet achieved.*
3. *Better than all humans at everything?* → **ASI**. ← *Theoretical.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of AI (Capability × Functionality)

```mermaid
    t3["Type III Theory of Mind"]:::leaf
```

> **Figure 4.1** — Two parallel axes of AI classification. Master this layout for full-mark 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Machine Learning Types — Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid

    sup["Supervised Learning"]:::sup
    uns["Unsupervised Learning"]:::uns

    self["Self Supervised"]:::ext
    trans["Transfer Learning"]:::ext
    fed["Federated Learning"]:::ext

    supAlgo["Algorithms: Linear Regression Logistic Regression SVM Decision Tree k NN Neural Network"]:::algo
    unsAlgo["Algorithms: k Means DBSCAN Hierarchical PCA t SNE Apriori"]:::algo

    unsData["Data: Unlabeled x only"]:::data

    ml --> semi
```

> **Figure 4.2** — The three core ML types plus four modern extensions. Note the data-type differences — *that* is what KTU examiners test in Part A.

---

## 4.3 RL Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Agent
    participant Env as Environment
    participant Mem as Memory Buffer

    Note over Agent,Env: At each timestep t
    Agent->>Env: Observe state s sub t
    Env-->>Agent: Return reward r sub t
    Agent->>Mem: Store transition s sub t, a sub t, r sub t, s sub t plus 1
    Agent->>Agent: Update policy pi or Q function
    Agent->>Env: Choose action a sub t plus 1 using pi
    Env-->>Agent: New state s sub t plus 1
    Note over Agent,Env: Loop continues until terminal state
```

> **Figure 4.3** — The fundamental RL loop. This diagram alone can fetch 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## 4.4 AI Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ds["Data Science Interdisciplinary Field"]:::ds
    ai["Artificial Intelligence"]:::ai
    ml["Machine Learning"]:::ml
    dl["Deep Learning"]:::dl
    rl["Reinforcement Learning"]:::rl
    nlp["NLP and Computer Vision Applications"]:::app

    ds -. uses .-> ml
    ai --> ml
    ml --> dl
    ml --> rl
    dl --> nlp
    rl --> nlp

    classDef ds fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ai fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ml fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d9488,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dl fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rl fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px
    classDef app fill:#6b7280,color:#ffffff,stroke:#6b7280,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Figure 4.4** — The relationship between Data Science, AI, ML, DL, and RL. The dashed line shows Data Science is *related but not a subset* of AI.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the three types of AI based on capability with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three types of AI based on capability are:

1. **Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI / Weak AI):** AI designed for a single specific task. *Example:* Apple Siri or IBM Deep Blue.
2. **Artificial General Intelligence (AGI / Strong AI):** Hypothetical AI with human-level intelligence across all domains. *Example:* None exists yet; it is a research goal.
3. **Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI):** Hypothetical AI that surpasses the smartest human in every field. *Example:* None — purely theoretical.

*(3 marks — 1 mark per type + example. Total 3.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Data | Labeled $(x, y)$ pairs | Unlabeled $x$ only |
| Goal | Learn mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure |
| Tasks | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction |
| Feedback | Direct (true labels) | No feedback |
| Example | Email spam detection (Spam/Not Spam labels) | Customer segmentation (no labels) |
| Algorithm examples | SVM, Linear Regression | k-Means, PCA |

*(3 marks — give 3 distinct differentiating points + one example each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule (ESE Module 1):** Two 14-mark questions will be set; you answer **one**. Each typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **four types of AI based on functionality** proposed by Arend Hintze. Give one example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Reinforcement Learning** paradigm. Derive the **Bellman optimality equation**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Definition:** Hintze (2016) classified AI into four functional types based on how the system internally processes information.

**1. Reactive Machines (Type I):**
- These systems perceive the current environment and react based on pre-programmed rules.
- They have **no memory** of past events and cannot learn from experience.
- **Mechanism:** Direct input-to-output mapping.
- **Example:** IBM **Deep Blue** (the chess computer that defeated Kasparov in 1997). It evaluated the current board position and chose the best move but had no memory of previous games.
- **Limitation:** Cannot improve with experience.

**2. Limited Memory (Type II):**
- These systems use **recent observations** to inform their decisions. They maintain a short buffer of past data.
- **Mechanism:** Sensor data is stored in a temporary memory; the model is trained on this recent buffer.
- **Example:** **Self-driving cars** (Tesla, Waymo) — they observe other cars' speeds and distances over the last few seconds to make driving decisions.
- **Real-world status:** This is the **current state of the art** in 2026.

**3. Theory of Mind (Type III):**
- These systems **understand that other agents have beliefs, intentions, and emotions** that influence their behavior.
- **Mechanism:** Models a representation of *other agents' mental states*.
- **Example:** *Sophia the robot* (Hanson Robotics) attempts basic social interaction; emotion-recognition systems like Affectiva.
- **Status:** Active research, not yet fully realized.

**4. Self-Aware AI (Type IV):**
- These systems possess **consciousness and self-awareness** — they understand their own existence and internal states.
- **Mechanism:** Would require a model of *itself* and *its own mind*.
- **Example:** None real. *Pop-culture references:* HAL 9000, Samantha in the film *Her*.
- **Status:** **Hypothetical / philosophical** — depends on unsolved "hard problem of consciousness".

**Diagrammatic representation:** Refer to **Figure 4.1** (Taxonomy of AI).

**Conclusion:** Modern AI is dominated by Types I and II. The leap to Type III and IV requires breakthroughs in cognitive modeling, social reasoning, and consciousness science.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per type + 0.5 for the example)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Reinforcement Learning (RL) — Definition:**
RL is a paradigm where an **agent** learns to make decisions by **interacting with an environment**, receiving **rewards** (or penalties) for its actions, and updating its **policy** to maximize cumulative future reward.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent] -- Action a sub t --> E[Environment]
    E -- Reward r sub t plus 1 --> A
    E -- New State s sub t plus 1 --> A
    style A fill:#2563eb,color:#fff
    style E fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
```

**Key elements:**
- **State $s_t$** — the current situation observed by the agent.
- **Action $a_t$** — the move chosen from the action space.
- **Reward $r_t$** — a scalar feedback signal (positive = good, negative = bad).
- **Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$** — the strategy mapping states to actions.
- **Value function $V^{\pi}(s)$** — expected cumulative reward from state $s$ under policy $\pi$.

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality Equation:**

**Step 1:** Define the discounted return:
$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \le \gamma \le 1$$

**Step 2:** Define the value function:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s]$$

**Step 3:** Unroll one step:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[r_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s]$$
$$= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) [R(s,a) + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$$

**Step 4:** Take the maximum over actions to obtain the optimal value:
$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) [R(s,a) + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal value of a state equals the best immediate reward plus the discounted optimal value of the next state. This is the recursive backbone of all RL algorithms.

**Application example:** AlphaGo uses a deep neural network to approximate $V^{*}(s)$ and a tree search to evaluate the $\max$ over actions.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition + diagram, 2 for elements, 3 for derivation)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare **Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning** in detail. Give two real-world applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the difference between **ANI, AGI, and ASI**? Discuss why AGI has not yet been achieved, listing at least three technical challenges. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data | Labeled | Unlabeled | Interaction trace |
| Feedback | Direct labels | None | Reward signal |
| Goal | Predict $y$ from $x$ | Find structure | Maximize return $G_t$ |
| Key algorithm | Linear Regression, SVM, CNN | k-Means, PCA, DBSCAN | Q-Learning, PPO, DQN |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, F1, RMSE | Silhouette, inertia | Cumulative reward |
| Example app 1 | Spam email detection | Customer segmentation | Game playing (AlphaGo) |
| Example app 2 | Disease diagnosis from X-ray | Anomaly detection in networks | Robotic arm manipulation |

**Two real-world applications each:**

- **Supervised:**
  1. **Medical diagnosis** — Given labeled X-ray images (tumor / no tumor), train a CNN to classify new X-rays.
  2. **Credit scoring** — Banks use labeled historical loan data (default / no-default) to predict risk for new applicants.
- **Unsupervised:**
  1. **Market basket analysis** — Retailers use the Apriori algorithm on unlabeled transaction logs to find product bundling rules.
  2. **Genomic clustering** — Biologists cluster gene-expression data to discover unknown cell types.
- **Reinforcement:**
  1. **Autonomous driving decision layer** — The car learns lane-change policies via reward shaping (smoothness + safety).
  2. **Dynamic pricing** — E-commerce platforms use RL to adjust prices in real time to maximize revenue.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 2 each cluster for 2 examples = 4)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Definitions:**

- **ANI (Artificial Narrow Intelligence):** AI that performs a single specific task with superhuman accuracy in that narrow domain. **All existing AI systems in 2026 are ANI.**
- **AGI (Artificial General Intelligence):** Hypothetical AI that matches human cognitive ability across *any* intellectual task — reasoning, planning, abstract thinking, common sense, transfer learning.
- **ASI (Artificial Super Intelligence):** Hypothetical AI that surpasses the brightest human in every field, including creativity, social skills, and scientific reasoning.

**Why AGI has not yet been achieved — 3 technical challenges:**

1. **Common-Sense Reasoning:**
   - Humans possess vast implicit knowledge of how the physical and social world works (e.g., "water is wet", "if I drop a glass, it breaks"). Encoding this knowledge in a machine remains unsolved. Current LLMs hallucinate because they lack grounded common sense.
2. **Transfer Learning and Generalization:**
   - An AGI must be able to learn a task in one context and apply it to a completely new context. Current deep models are *brittle* — they fail on out-of-distribution data. A vision model trained on cats and dogs cannot suddenly play chess.
3. **Causal Reasoning:**
   - Humans understand *cause and effect* (smoking $\rightarrow$ cancer). Today's AI excels at *correlation* (X and Y occur together) but not *causation*. Judea Pearl's causal inference framework is a leading research direction but has not been integrated into mainstream AGI systems.

**Bonus challenge (optional for full marks):** **Consciousness and Self-Modeling** — the "hard problem" of consciousness (David Chalmers) remains unsolved in neuroscience; without a scientific theory of consciousness, building a self-aware AGI is impossible with current science.

**Current industry status (2026):** Frontier labs (OpenAI, DeepMind, Anthropic) are actively pursuing AGI through scaling laws, but consensus is that AGI is **still years to decades away** and may require architectural breakthroughs beyond transformers.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for ANI/AGI/ASI definition, 2 marks per challenge)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Strong AI" with "Limited Memory AI."** Strong AI = AGI (capability axis). Limited Memory = Type II (functionality axis). These are on *different axes* — don't mix them.
> 2. **Writing "AI = ML = DL."** Wrong. AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL. Always write the proper subset relation with subset symbols.
> 3. **Forgetting the loss function in Supervised Learning derivations.** Examiners specifically look for $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum L(y_i, \hat{y}_i)$. Missing it loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Writing "AGI exists" in 2026.** It does not. Be precise — *current* AI is *narrow*.
> 5. **Omitting the discount factor $\gamma$ in RL answers.** The Bellman equation **must** have $\gamma$ — losing it costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing $V^{\pi}$ (value under policy) with $V^{*}$ (optimal value).** Examiners check whether you can distinguish policy evaluation from optimal control.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 🎯 **Use this section the night before the exam for last-mile revision.**

- ☐ **AI is a superset of ML**, which is a superset of DL. Data Science is **related, not a subset**.
- ☐ **3 capability-based AI types:** ANI (exists), AGI (research), ASI (theoretical).
- ☐ **4 functionality-based AI types:** Reactive, Limited Memory, Theory of Mind, Self-Aware (Hintze 2016).
- ☐ **All current (2026) commercial AI = ANI + Limited Memory (Type II).**
- ☐ **3 core ML types:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), Reinforcement (reward).
- ☐ **Supervised loss:** $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L(y_i, f_\theta(x_i))$.
- ☐ **MSE formula:** $\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. **Cross-entropy:** $-\sum p(c)\log q(c)$.
- ☐ **RL return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ with $\gamma \in [0,1]$.
- ☐ **Bellman optimality:** $V^{*}(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^{*}(s')]$.
- ☐ **Q-learning update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ☐ **Examples to memorize:** Deep Blue (Reactive), Tesla (Limited Memory), Gmail spam (Supervised), k-Means segmentation (Unsupervised), AlphaGo (RL).
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Never write "AGI exists today." Always clarify: *"AGI is a research goal; no system has yet achieved human-level general intelligence."*
- ☐ **Exam heuristic:** For any 14-mark "types" question — open with a **definition**, draw a **diagram**, give a **comparison table**, end with **2 real-world examples** and a **limitation**.
- ☐ **Bonus modern types to know (for higher marks):** Semi-Supervised, Self-Supervised, Federated, Transfer, Multi-Task Learning.

---

✅ **End of Module 1 Notes — "Different Types."** Good luck on your KTU 2024 exam! 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intelligent Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intelligent Agent (IA):** An **intelligent agent** is any entity that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**, with the goal of maximizing its **performance measure** over time.

Mathematically, an agent can be abstracted as a function that maps a history of percepts to an action:

$$\text{Agent Function: } f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ = the set of all finite percept sequences
- $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of all possible actions
- $f$ = the agent function (also called the policy $\pi$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favorite):** The *agent function* is a **mathematical abstraction** (theoretical mapping), whereas the *agent program* is the **concrete implementation** that runs on a physical architecture. The program realizes the function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of an **intelligent agent** as a **self-driving car** navigating a busy city.

| Car Component | Agent Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Cameras, Lidar, GPS | **Sensors** | Perceive the road, pedestrians, signs |
| Steering wheel, brakes, accelerator | **Actuators** | Physically act on the environment |
| Onboard computer (NVIDIA Drive) | **Agent Program** | Decides what to do next |
| Goal of reaching destination safely + quickly | **Performance Measure** | Evaluates how "good" the agent is doing |
| Roads, traffic, weather | **Environment** | The world the agent interacts with |

Just as a self-driving car must decide *when to brake, accelerate, or swerve* based on what it perceives, an intelligent agent decides *which action to execute* based on its **percept sequence**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** "**S**ensors **A**ctuators **P**rogram **P**erformance" → **SAPP** — the four pillars of every intelligent agent.

## 1.3 Rationality — The Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rational Agent:** For each possible percept sequence, a *rational agent* should select an action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has.

A rational agent is **NOT** omniscient. It is limited by:
1. The information available from its percepts.
2. Its prior knowledge of the environment.
3. The capabilities of its actuators.

### Rationality vs. Omniscience vs. Perfection

| Property | Definition | Achievable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Rationality** | Maximizes *expected* performance | ✅ Yes |
| **Omniscience** | Knows the *actual* outcome of its actions | ❌ No |
| **Perfection** | Always achieves the *best* outcome | ❌ No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Gathering** is a *rational* action. An agent that doesn't explore to gather information is not maximizing its expected performance — it is being **irrational**.

## 1.4 PEAS Framework (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEAS** is the **standardized KTU-mandated design template** for specifying an intelligent agent's task environment. Always use PEAS to formally define any agent problem.

| PEAS Component | Question it Answers | Example (Automated Taxi) |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance Measure** | How do we evaluate success? | Safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit |
| **Environment** | What is the world the agent operates in? | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather |
| **Actuators** | How does the agent act? | Steering, accelerator, brake, horn, display |
| **Sensors** | How does the agent perceive? | Cameras, Lidar, GPS, speedometer, accelerometer |

### More PEAS Examples (High-Yield for KTU)

| Task | Performance | Environment | Actuators | Sensors |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Healthy patient, cost, lawsuits | Patient, hospital, staff | Display questions, tests | Keyboard, touch screen |
| **Satellite Image Analysis** | Correct categorization | Orbital imagery database | Display of classification | Color pixel arrays |
| **Part-Picking Robot** | % of parts in correct bin | Conveyor belt with parts | Jointed arm + gripper | Camera, joint encoders |
| **Refinery Controller** | Purity, yield, safety | Refinery, raw materials, operators | Valves, pumps, heaters | Pressure, temperature, chemical sensors |
| **Interactive English Tutor** | Student exam score | Set of students | Display exercises, corrections | Keyboard, voice input |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of an Intelligent Agent

Every intelligent agent, irrespective of complexity, follows a fundamental **perception–cognition–action loop**:

1. **Perceive** the current state of the environment via sensors.
2. **Interpret** the percept (raw signal → meaningful state representation).
3. **Reason** about which action best satisfies the performance measure.
4. **Act** on the environment via actuators.
5. **Repeat** indefinitely (or until a termination condition is met).

This is formally known as the **Perception-Action Cycle**.

## 2.2 Agent Architecture vs. Agent Program

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Agent Architecture = Hardware + Software Platform** (e.g., a robotic car with GPUs).
> **Agent Program = Pure software that implements the agent function** (e.g., a neural network that maps camera frames to steering angles).
>
> **Agent = Architecture + Program**

## 2.3 The KTU-Mandated Taxonomy of Agent Types

The four (or five, counting learning) canonical agent types form the **core classification** that KTU examiners love to test:

### 2.3.1 Simple Reflex Agent

- **Logic:** `IF condition THEN action` (Condition-Action Rules).
- **Decision Basis:** **Current percept only** (stateless).
- **Strengths:** Simple, fast, computationally cheap.
- **Weaknesses:** Fails in **partially observable** environments (no memory of past).
- **Analogy:** A *thermostat* — turns on heater if temperature < 20°C, no matter what happened before.

$$\text{Action} = \text{Rule}(\text{Current Percept})$$

### 2.3.2 Model-Based Reflex Agent

- **Logic:** Maintains an **internal state** that tracks aspects of the world not currently observable.
- **Decision Basis:** Current percept **+ internal state** (history-dependent).
- **Requires:** A **model** of how the world evolves (transition model) and how percepts are generated (sensor model).
- **Analogy:** A driver remembering a *blind turn* is coming up — uses memory of past percepts.

$$\text{State}_{t+1} = \text{Update}(\text{State}_t, \text{Percept}_{t+1})$$
$$\text{Action} = \text{Rule}(\text{Percept}_{t+1}, \text{State}_{t+1})$$

### 2.3.3 Goal-Based Agent

- **Logic:** Reasons about **future states** by considering actions that achieve desired goals.
- **Decision Basis:** Current state + **goal description** + world model.
- **Strengths:** Flexible, supports planning and search.
- **Analogy:** A GPS navigation system — *plans the route* to reach a destination, not just reacting to the current road.

### 2.3.4 Utility-Based Agent

- **Logic:** Chooses actions that **maximize a utility function** (a mapping from states to real numbers).
- **Decision Basis:** State + **utility function** + world model.
- **Why needed?** When goals are insufficient (e.g., multiple goal states exist, or trade-offs between competing objectives like speed vs. safety).
- **Analogy:** A *self-driving car* that balances speed, safety, fuel economy, and passenger comfort — each assigned a utility score.

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[\,U(\text{Result}(s, a))\,]$$

Where $U(\cdot)$ is the utility function and $\mathbb{E}$ denotes expectation.

### 2.3.5 Learning Agent

- **Logic:** Improves its own performance over time by modifying its components.
- **Four Conceptual Components:**
  1. **Performance Element** — selects actions (the agent itself).
  2. **Critic** — evaluates how the agent is doing (feedback signal).
  3. **Learning Element** — modifies the performance element using critic's feedback.
  4. **Problem Generator** — suggests exploratory actions to discover new, better strategies.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Agent Types:** "**S**imple **M**odel **G**oal **U**tility **L**earning" → **SMGUL** — from simplest to most sophisticated.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Agent Function | $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | $\mathcal{P}^*$: percept history |
| 2 | Simple Reflex | $a = \text{Rule}(p_t)$ | Stateless |
| 3 | Model-Based State Update | $s_{t+1} = \text{Update}(s_t, p_{t+1})$ | Internal state |
| 4 | Utility-Based Optimal Action | $a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[U(\text{Result}(s,a))]$ | Utility function $U$ |
| 5 | Expected Utility | $\mathbb{E}[U] = \sum_i P(i) \cdot U(i)$ | Probability-weighted utility |
| 6 | Performance Measure | Scalar reward $R \in \mathbb{R}$ | Higher = better |
| 7 | Rationality Condition | $\forall \text{ percept seq. } \rightarrow a \text{ maximizes expected } R$ | Expectation, not actual |
| 8 | Markov Property | $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ | Next state depends only on current |
| 9 | Percept Sequence Length | $\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \vert \mathcal{P} \vert^i$ | Geometric series |
| 10 | Vacuum World Actions | $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp}\}$ | Discrete action set |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Agent Type Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Tesla FSD, Waymo | Utility-based + Learning |
| **Healthcare** | IBM Watson for Oncology | Goal-based + Utility-based |
| **Industrial Robotics** | KUKA assembly arms | Model-based reflex |
| **Smart Home** | Nest Thermostat | Simple reflex |
| **Game AI** | AlphaGo, Stockfish | Utility + Learning (Deep RL) |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion Detection Systems | Model-based + Learning |
| **Agriculture** | Precision spraying drones | Goal-based + Utility-based |
| **Space Exploration** | NASA's Mars rovers | Goal-based + Learning |

## 2.6 Properties of Task Environments (Environment Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Classify the following task environment along all dimensions."* Memorize all **seven** dimensions.

| Property | Values | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully vs. Partially Observable** | F / P | Are all relevant aspects of state visible via sensors? | Chess (F) vs. Poker (P) |
| **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** | D / S | Does the next state depend purely on current state + action? | Solitaire (S) vs. Chess with random dice (S) |
| **Episodic vs. Sequential** | E / S | Are decisions independent across episodes? | Image classification (E) vs. Driving (S) |
| **Static vs. Dynamic** | St / Dy | Can the environment change while the agent deliberates? | Crossword (St) vs. Traffic (Dy) |
| **Discrete vs. Continuous** | Di / Co | Are percepts/actions finite or infinite? | Chess (Di) vs. Taxi driving (Co) |
| **Single vs. Multi-Agent** | Si / Mu | Is one agent acting alone or with others? | Solitaire (Si) vs. Soccer (Mu) |
| **Known vs. Unknown** | K / U | Are the rules/outcomes known to the agent? | Poker rules (K), but opponents' cards (U) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**F**rank **D**eveloped **E**vening **S**tatic **D**igital **S**ingle **K**nowledge" — **FDESSDK** to remember the 7 properties.

### Hardest-to-Easiest Environment Spectrum

The **hardest** environment satisfies:
**Partially Observable + Stochastic + Sequential + Dynamic + Continuous + Multi-Agent + Unknown**

The **easiest** environment satisfies the opposite: **Fully Observable + Deterministic + Episodic + Static + Discrete + Single-Agent + Known**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Size of the Percept Space

> **Problem Statement:** A robot vacuum operates in a world with **2 percepts** (Clean/Dirty) and can choose from **3 actions** (Suck, Move, NoOp). Derive the size of the percept space after observing $n$ time steps, and the corresponding size of the agent function table.

### Step 1 — Define the Single-Step Percept Set

The robot receives percepts of the form $\langle \text{location}, \text{status} \rangle$, where:
- $\text{location} \in \{A, B\}$ → 2 values
- $\text{status} \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$ → 2 values

Therefore, the single percept is drawn from:
$$\vert \mathcal{P} \vert = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ possible percepts}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Percept Sequence Cardinality

A percept sequence of length $k$ is a $k$-tuple drawn from $\mathcal{P}$. Hence:
$$\vert \mathcal{P}^k \vert = \vert \mathcal{P} \vert^k = 4^k$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Percept Space (All Lengths)

The total percept space is the union of all finite-length sequences:
$$\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \vert \mathcal{P} \vert^k = \sum_{k=0}^{n} 4^k$$

### Step 4 — Apply Geometric Series Formula

Using the finite geometric series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{n} r^k = \dfrac{r^{n+1} - 1}{r - 1}$, with $r = 4$:

$$\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert = \frac{4^{n+1} - 1}{4 - 1} = \frac{4^{n+1} - 1}{3}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Agent Function Table Size

Since the agent function maps every unique percept sequence to an action from $\mathcal{A}$ where $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = 3$:

$$\text{Table Size} = \vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert \times \vert \mathcal{A} \vert = 3 \cdot \frac{4^{n+1} - 1}{3} = 4^{n+1} - 1 \text{ entries}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\;\text{Agent function table entries} = 4^{n+1} - 1\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** Even for the trivially small 2-cell vacuum world, the table grows **exponentially** with observation length $n$. This proves why **lookup-table agents are infeasible** in real-world problems and motivates the use of compact agent programs (rules, neural networks, etc.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Utility Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A medical diagnosis agent has three possible treatment actions, $a_1, a_2, a_3$, with the following success probabilities and utilities. Compute the **expected utility of each action** and select the **optimal rational action**.

| Action | Probability of Success $P(s)$ | Utility of Success $U(s)$ | Probability of Failure $P(f)$ | Utility of Failure $U(f)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1$: Surgery | 0.70 | +100 | 0.30 | −80 |
| $a_2$: Medication | 0.85 | +60 | 0.15 | −10 |
| $a_3$: Wait & Watch | 1.00 | +20 | 0.00 | 0 |

### Step 1 — Recall the Expected Utility Formula

For a binary-outcome action:
$$\mathbb{E}[U(a)] = P(\text{success}) \cdot U(\text{success}) + P(\text{failure}) \cdot U(\text{failure})$$
