### Step 2 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[U(a_1)]$ for Surgery

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_1)] = (0.70)(+100) + (0.30)(-80)$$

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_1)] = 70.0 - 24.0 = +46.0$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[U(a_2)]$ for Medication

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_2)] = (0.85)(+60) + (0.15)(-10)$$

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_2)] = 51.0 - 1.5 = +49.5$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\mathbb{E}[U(a_3)]$ for Wait & Watch

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_3)] = (1.00)(+20) + (0.00)(0)$$

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_3)] = 20.0 + 0.0 = +20.0$$

### Step 5 — Compare and Select the Optimal Action

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a_1)] = +46.0, \quad \mathbb{E}[U(a_2)] = +49.5, \quad \mathbb{E}[U(a_3)] = +20.0$$

The rational action is:
$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \{a_1, a_2, a_3\}} \mathbb{E}[U(a)]$$

$$\boxed{\;a^* = a_2 \text{ (Medication) with } \mathbb{E}[U] = +49.5\;}$$

### Step 6 — Insight

> **Why not Surgery ($a_1$) despite high success utility?** Because the *cost of failure* (−80) is so severe that the expected value drops below Medication. The rational agent **balances risk and reward** using the expectation operator, not raw peak values.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Agent Type

| Scenario | Recommended Agent Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Thermostat controlling room temperature | **Simple Reflex** | Stateless, no need for memory |
| Self-driving car in a busy city | **Model-Based + Utility-Based + Learning** | Partially observable, continuous, multi-agent |
| Vacuum robot in a 2-room house | **Model-Based Reflex** | Needs to remember which rooms were cleaned |
| Chess-playing AI | **Goal-Based + Utility-Based** | Clear goal (checkmate), evaluates board states |
| Spam email classifier | **Simple Reflex (with learning)** | Stateless classification of each email |
| AlphaGo | **Utility-Based + Learning** | Learns from self-play, uses deep value networks |
| Mars rover Curiosity | **Goal-Based + Learning** | Long-horizon goals, learns from terrain |
| Intrusion detection system | **Model-Based + Learning** | Tracks attack patterns, learns novel threats |
| Industrial robotic arm picking parts | **Model-Based Reflex** | Predicts part positions, fast reactions |
| ChatGPT (conversational agent) | **Utility-Based + Learning** | Generates max-utility text, fine-tuned from feedback |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — PEAS for an Automated Taxi

> **Problem (from KTU-style Module 1 Past Papers):** *Design a PEAS specification for an automated taxi driver. Then classify its environment along all 7 dimensions.*

### PEAS Specification

$$\text{PEAS} = \begin{cases} \text{Performance:} & \text{Safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit, fuel efficiency} \\ \text{Environment:} & \text{Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, road signs, other vehicles} \\ \text{Actuators:} & \text{Steering, accelerator, brake, gearshift, horn, signal, display} \\ \text{Sensors:} & \text{Cameras, Lidar, Radar, GPS, accelerometer, speedometer, microphone} \end{cases}$$

### 7-Dimensional Environment Classification

| Dimension | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Observable | **Partially Observable** | Cannot see around corners or anticipate all pedestrian actions |
| Determinism | **Stochastic** | Traffic behavior, mechanical failures introduce randomness |
| Episodic | **Sequential** | Current action affects all future states (turning affects route) |
| Static/Dynamic | **Dynamic** | Other cars, pedestrians move while the agent deliberates |
| Discrete/Continuous | **Continuous** | Steering angles, velocities, distances are continuous |
| Single/Multi-Agent | **Multi-Agent** | Must coordinate with other drivers and pedestrians |
| Known/Unknown | **Known** | Traffic rules are standardized and known in advance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Intelligent Agent Perception–Action Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment] -->|provides stimuli| SENS[Sensors]
    SENS -->|raw percept| PERC[Perception Module]
    PERC -->|interpreted state| REAS[Reasoning / Decision Module]
    REAS -->|selected action| ACT[Actuators]
    ACT -->|action effects| ENV
    REAS -->|updates| MEM[Internal State / Memory]
    MEM -->|feeds| REAS
```

> **Diagram Note:** This block-level flow shows the closed perception–action loop. The agent continuously cycles through **Perceive → Reason → Act → Update State**, with memory feeding back into reasoning.

## 4.2 The Four Canonical Agent Architectures (Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SRA[Simple Reflex Agent]
        S1[Current Percept] --> S2[Condition-Action Rules]
        S2 --> S3[Action]
    end

    subgraph MRA[Model-Based Reflex Agent]
        M1[Current Percept] --> M2[State Update Module]
        M4[World Model] --> M2
        M2 --> M3[Internal State]
        M1 --> M5[Condition-Action Rules]
        M3 --> M5
        M5 --> M6[Action]
    end

    subgraph GBA[Goal-Based Agent]
        G1[Current State] --> G2[World Model + Goal]
        G2 --> G3[Search / Planning]
        G3 --> G4[Action]
    end

    subgraph UBA[Utility-Based Agent]
        U1[Current State] --> U2[World Model + Utility Function]
        U2 --> U3[Expected Utility Computation]
        U3 --> U4[Best Action]
    end
```

## 4.3 Learning Agent — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LA[Learning Agent]
        PE[Performance Element<br>selects actions]
        LE[Learning Element<br>improves performance]
        CR[Critic<br>evaluates performance]
        PG[Problem Generator<br>suggests explorations]
    end

    ENV[Environment] -->|percepts| PE
    PE -->|actions| ENV
    ENV -->|rewards / outcomes| CR
    CR -->|feedback signal| LE
    LE -->|updated knowledge| PE
    PG -->|exploratory actions| PE
    PE -->|new experiences| LE
```

> **Diagram Note:** The four components of a learning agent work in a closed feedback loop. The **critic** provides a performance feedback signal (analogous to a coach), the **learning element** uses this to modify the **performance element** (the agent's "brain"), and the **problem generator** injects exploratory actions to discover better strategies.

## 4.4 Agent Type Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Agent Problem] --> B{Need memory of<br>past percepts?}
    B -- No --> C{Is the environment<br>fully observable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Simple Reflex Agent]
    C -- No --> E{Does the agent need<br>to plan ahead?}
    B -- Yes --> E
    E -- No --> F[Model-Based Reflex Agent]
    E -- Yes --> G{Are multiple goals<br>or trade-offs involved?}
    G -- No --> H[Goal-Based Agent]
    G -- Yes --> I{Does the agent need to<br>improve over time?}
    I -- No --> J[Utility-Based Agent]
    I -- Yes --> K[Learning Agent]
```

## 4.5 Environment Properties Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Task Environment] --> B[Observable]
    A --> C[Deterministic]
    A --> D[Episodic / Sequential]
    A --> E[Static / Dynamic]
    A --> F[Discrete / Continuous]
    A --> G[Single / Multi-Agent]
    A --> H[Known / Unknown]

    B --> B1[Fully Observable<br>e.g. Chess]
    B --> B2[Partially Observable<br>e.g. Poker]

    C --> C1[Deterministic<br>e.g. 8-puzzle]
    C --> C2[Stochastic<br>e.g. Backgammon]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **Define an Intelligent Agent. Explain the term "rationality" with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer:

An **intelligent agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through **sensors** and acts upon it through **actuators** to achieve a **performance measure**.

A **rational agent** selects, for every possible percept sequence, the action that **maximizes the expected value of its performance measure**, given the percept history and prior knowledge.

**Example:** A medical diagnosis system. Given symptoms (percepts) as input, the rational action is the diagnosis/treatment (action) that maximizes the probability of patient recovery (performance measure) given the available test results.

*(Word count: ~70 words — appropriate for a 3-mark answer.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **What is the PEAS framework? Specify the PEAS for a Vacuum Cleaner Agent.**

#### Model Answer:

**PEAS** stands for **Performance Measure, Environment, Actuators, and Sensors** — the standardized template for specifying any intelligent agent's task environment.

**PEAS for Vacuum Cleaner Agent:**

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Cleanliness, time taken, electricity consumed |
| **Environment** | Room, dirt, furniture, floor type |
| **Actuators** | Wheels, brushes, vacuum motor, dust bag |
| **Sensors** | Dirt sensor, bumper sensor, cliff sensor, camera |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **(a)** Explain the four main types of agents in detail with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define rational agent. How does rationality differ from omniscience? Discuss with an example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Four Main Agent Types (7 Marks)

**1. Simple Reflex Agent (2 Marks)**

These agents select actions based **only on the current percept**, ignoring the rest of the percept history. They operate using **condition-action rules** of the form:

$$\text{IF } \text{percept} = c \text{ THEN } \text{action} = a$$

- **Example:** A vacuum cleaner that activates the brush motor only when the dirt sensor reads "Dirty."
- **Limitation:** Will fail in partially observable environments (e.g., a reflex that only knows the *current* square cannot determine if the *adjacent* square is dirty).

**2. Model-Based Reflex Agent (2 Marks)**

These agents maintain an **internal state** that captures the unobserved aspects of the world, using a **world model** (transition model + sensor model).

$$\text{State}_{t+1} = \text{Update}(\text{State}_t, \text{Percept}_{t+1}, \text{WorldModel})$$

- **Example:** A self-driving car that tracks the *expected position* of a pedestrian who stepped behind a wall.
- **Advantage:** Handles partial observability.

**3. Goal-Based Agent (1.5 Marks)**

These agents reason about **future states** and choose actions that lead to a **desired goal state**. They use **search and planning** algorithms.

- **Example:** A GPS navigation system that plans a route to reach a destination.
- **Advantage:** Flexible — the same goal-based engine can be reused for different goals.

**4. Utility-Based Agent (1.5 Marks)**

These agents use a **utility function** $U: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ to evaluate the *desirability* of states, choosing actions that maximize **expected utility**.

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[U(\text{Result}(s, a))]$$

- **Example:** An autonomous taxi that balances speed, safety, fuel economy, and passenger comfort.
- **Advantage:** Handles conflicting objectives and uncertain outcomes.

*(Simple block diagrams of each agent type to be drawn in the exam — see SECTION 4.)*

#### Part (b) — Rational vs. Omniscience (7 Marks)

**Definition of Rational Agent (2 Marks):**

A **rational agent** is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that **maximizes the expected value of the performance measure**, based on:
1. The percept sequence so far.
2. The agent's prior knowledge.
3. The actions the agent can perform.

**Definition of Omniscience (2 Marks):**

An **omniscient agent** knows the *true* outcome of its actions and the *complete* state of the environment at all times. Omniscience is **not achievable** in the real world.

**Key Differences (2 Marks):**

| Property | Rational | Omniscient |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of decision | Expected outcome | Actual outcome |
| Achievable? | Yes | No |
| Information source | Percepts + prior knowledge | All knowledge |

**Example (1 Mark):**

A self-driving car that sees a clear road and accelerates. A child darts out from behind a parked truck. The car cannot possibly have *seen* the child beforehand — so the action was *rational* (maximized expected performance). However, the car is **not omniscient** (it couldn't know about the child). After braking, the rational agent **learns** and updates its internal model to be more cautious at blind spots in the future.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **(a)** Describe the Learning Agent architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Explain the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the various properties used to classify task environments. Classify the task environment of an Automated Medical Diagnosis System along all the properties. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Learning Agent Architecture (7 Marks)

A **learning agent** is one that improves its performance over time by observing its own actions and the outcomes. It has four conceptual components:

**1. Performance Element (2 Marks)**

The core "decision-maker" of the agent — it takes percepts as input and produces actions. This is essentially the agent itself (reflex, model-based, goal-based, or utility-based).

**2. Critic (2 Marks)**

The critic evaluates the agent's performance by comparing the actual outcomes against a fixed **performance standard**. It generates a **feedback signal** (e.g., reward/punishment). The critic is necessary because the percepts alone do *not* tell the agent how well it is doing.

**3. Learning Element (2 Marks)**

Uses the feedback from the critic (and possibly prior knowledge) to **modify the performance element** so that future actions lead to better outcomes. This is the part that truly "learns."

**4. Problem Generator (1 Mark)**

Suggests **exploratory actions** that may lead to new, informative experiences. Without it, the agent would never discover better strategies and would get stuck in suboptimal local behaviors.

**Diagram (to be drawn — refer to Section 4.3):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment] -->|percepts| PE[Performance Element]
    PE -->|actions| ENV
    ENV -->|outcomes| CR[Critic]
    CR -->|feedback| LE[Learning Element]
    LE -->|updates| PE
    PG[Problem Generator] -->|exploratory actions| PE
```

#### Part (b) — Environment Classification (7 Marks)

**Seven Properties of Task Environments (4 Marks):**

1. **Fully vs. Partially Observable** — Whether the agent's sensors give it access to the complete state.
2. **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** — Whether the next state is fully determined by the current state and action.
3. **Episodic vs. Sequential** — Whether the agent's experience is divided into independent episodes.
4. **Static vs. Dynamic** — Whether the environment can change while the agent is deliberating.
5. **Discrete vs. Continuous** — Whether the percepts and actions are discrete or continuous.
6. **Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent** — Whether the agent operates alone or with other agents.
7. **Known vs. Unknown** — Whether the agent knows the rules and outcomes of the environment.

**Classification of Automated Medical Diagnosis System (3 Marks):**

| Property | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Observable | **Partially Observable** | Tests may be inconclusive; patient may withhold symptoms |
| Determinism | **Stochastic** | Diseases progress variably; patient responses are probabilistic |
| Episodic | **Episodic** | Each patient is a new, independent diagnosis episode |
| Static/Dynamic | **Dynamic** | Patient's condition can change during diagnosis (deteriorate/improve) |
| Discrete/Continuous | **Continuous** | Vital signs (BP, temperature, heart rate) are continuous values |
| Single/Multi-Agent | **Multi-Agent** | Patient, doctors, nurses, lab technicians all interact |
| Known/Unknown | **Known** | Medical protocols and disease knowledge are well-documented |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Rational" with "Successful."** A rational agent can still *fail* because the world is stochastic. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you equate rationality with omniscience or perfection.
>
> 2. **Skipping the PEAS Table.** Many students *describe* the four components in prose and lose half the marks. **Always present PEAS as a labeled table** — it is the only format that maps to the mark distribution.
>
> 3. **Wrong PEAS for Vacuum Cleaner.** A frequent error: listing "dirt" as an *actuator* or "wheels" as a *sensor*. **Mnemonic:** Actuators produce *motion/change*; Sensors detect *information*.
>
> 4. **Missing the 7th property (Known/Unknown).** Students typically list 6 and forget **Known vs. Unknown**. This is an easy 1-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the World Model in Model-Based Agents.** Examiners expect you to mention the **transition model** and **sensor model** explicitly. A bare mention of "internal state" loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing Episodic with Static.** Episodic means *decisions are independent* across tasks; Static means *environment doesn't change during deliberation*. They are orthogonal.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam, you MUST memorize the following:**

- [x] **Agent Definition:** Perceives via sensors, acts via actuators, maximizes performance measure.
- [x] **Agent Function:** $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ — maps percept history to action.
- [x] **Rational Agent:** Maximizes **expected** performance, not actual.
- [x] **Rational ≠ Omniscient ≠ Perfect.**
- [x] **PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — always present as a table.
- [x] **Four Agent Types (in order of complexity):** Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based.
- [x] **Fifth Type:** Learning Agent (Performance Element + Critic + Learning Element + Problem Generator).
- [x] **Simple Reflex:** Stateless, condition-action rules.
- [x] **Model-Based Reflex:** Internal state + world model.
- [x] **Goal-Based:** Plans to achieve a goal state.
- [x] **Utility-Based:** Maximizes expected utility function.
- [x] **Learning Agent:** Improves via feedback from critic.
- [x] **Seven Environment Properties:** Observable, Deterministic, Episodic, Static, Discrete, Single-Agent, Known. (Mnemonic: **FDESSDK**.)
- [x] **Hardest environment:** Partially Observable + Stochastic + Sequential + Dynamic + Continuous + Multi-Agent + Unknown.
- [x] **Example mappings:** Chess (F,D,S,St,Di,Si,K); Poker (P,S,S,Dy,Di,Mu,U); Taxi (P,S,S,Dy,Co,Mu,K).
- [x] **Agent = Architecture + Program.**
- [x] **Information gathering is a rational action.**

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "design an agent for X," always follow this three-step template:
> 1. Write the **PEAS table** (4 marks).
> 2. **Classify the environment** along all 7 dimensions (3 marks).
> 3. **Choose the appropriate agent type** with justification (remaining marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem Solving by Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving by Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PBRAT404 - Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning*, **Problem Solving by Search** is defined as the systematic process by which an **Intelligent Agent** transitions from a given *initial state* to a *goal state* by exploring a discrete **State Space** using a defined set of **Actions** (operators), evaluated by a **Goal Test** and a **Path Cost Function**.

Mathematically, a search problem is formulated as a tuple:

$$ P = (S, A, s_0, G, C) $$

Where:
- $S$ = Set of all possible states in the world
- $A$ = Set of available actions
- $s_0 \in S$ = Initial state
- $G \subseteq S$ = Set of goal states
- $C$ = Step-cost function $C(s, a, s') \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** A search problem is *fully observable*, *deterministic*, and *discrete*. If the environment were stochastic or partially observable, search alone would be insufficient — we would need **Adversarial Search** or **Probabilistic Reasoning** (covered in later modules).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigator" Intuition

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi** to **Trivandrum** with Google Maps. The problem is structured as:

- **State Space ($S$):** Every possible road intersection in Kerala.
- **Initial State ($s_0$):** Your current GPS location in Kochi.
- **Goal State ($G$):** The Trivandrum city center.
- **Actions ($A$):** Turn left, turn right, continue straight (3 possible exits per junction).
- **Goal Test:** "Is my current location Trivandrum?"
- **Path Cost ($C$):** Total kilometers traveled (or travel time in minutes).

The **Search Algorithm** is the underlying engine that decides *which road to take next* — like a BFS would explore the nearest junctions first, while a heuristic (A*) would always steer toward the highway. This is the essence of **Problem Solving by Search**.

### 1.3 Well-Defined Problem — Five Mandatory Elements

Per the standard KTU textbook reference (Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*), every problem-solver requires these five components:

| S.No | Component | Meaning | Kerala Analogy |
|------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Initial State** | Where the agent starts | Your house in Kottayam |
| 2 | **Actions** | Possible moves $Actions(s)$ | Bus routes available at your stop |
| 3 | **Transition Model** | $Result(s, a) \rightarrow s'$ | Next stop on the bus route |
| 4 | **Goal Test** | Determines if goal is reached | "Have I reached the college?" |
| 5 | **Path Cost** | Numerical cost of a path | Total fare paid in rupees |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Solution** is a sequence of actions leading from $s_0$ to a goal state. An **Optimal Solution** is the one with the *minimum path cost*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Search Tree

When an agent searches, it builds a **Search Tree** dynamically over the state space. The distinction is critical:

- **State Space:** The *complete* graph of all reachable states (often infinite or huge).
- **Search Tree:** The *actively explored* subset, where each **Node** represents a state with cumulative path information.

A **Node** in the search tree is a 5-tuple data structure:

$$ n = \langle s, parent(n), action(n), path\_cost(n), depth(n) \rangle $$

Where:
- $s$ = the state represented
- $parent(n)$ = back-pointer to the predecessor node
- $action(n)$ = the action taken from parent to reach $n$
- $path\_cost(n)$ = $g(n)$ = cumulative cost from root
- $depth(n)$ = number of actions from root

### 2.2 The Generic Search Algorithm — Conceptual Logic Steps

Every informed and uninformed search algorithm (BFS, DFS, A*, etc.) is just a specialization of this single generic procedure:

1. **Initialize** the frontier (open list) with the root node containing $s_0$.
2. **Loop** until goal is found or frontier is empty:
   - **Select** a node $n$ from the frontier (the *strategy* differs per algorithm).
   - **Goal Test:** If $n$ satisfies the goal condition → return solution.
   - **Expand** $n$: generate successors using $Actions(state(n))$.
   - **Add** each child node to the frontier (avoiding explored states if graph search is used).
3. **Failure Return:** If frontier empties, return "failure" — no solution exists.

The *only thing* that varies between algorithms is **how step 2(a) selects a node**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Search. Memorize it as a complete unit — questions worth 7–14 marks are often asked directly on this comparison.

| Algorithm | Frontier Strategy | Complete? | Optimal? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|-----------|------------------|-----------|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **BFS** | FIFO Queue | Yes (uniform cost) | Yes (unit cost) | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ |
| **DFS** | LIFO Stack | No (fails in infinite spaces) | No | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ |
| **DLS** (Depth-Limited) | LIFO + limit $\ell$ | No | No | $\mathcal{O}(b^\ell)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot \ell)$ |
| **IDS** (Iterative Deepening) | Repeated DLS, $\ell = 0,1,2,\dots$ | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ |
| **UCS** (Uniform Cost) | Priority queue by $g(n)$ | Yes | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| **Greedy Best-First** | Priority queue by $h(n)$ | No | No | Worst: $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | Worst: $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ |
| **A\* Search** | Priority queue by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | Yes (if $h$ admissible + consistent) | Exponential in worst case | Exponential in worst case |

Where the variables are defined as:
- $b$ = **branching factor** (max number of successors of any node)
- $d$ = **depth of the shallowest goal node**
- $m$ = **maximum depth of the state space** (may be $\infty$)
- $\ell$ = **depth limit**
- $C^*$ = **optimal path cost**
- $\epsilon$ = **minimum positive step cost**
- $g(n)$ = **cost from start to node $n$**
- $h(n)$ = **heuristic estimated cost from $n$ to goal**
- $f(n)$ = **estimated total cost through $n$**

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These complexity results directly govern the **deployment of search in robotics, route planning, and game AI**. BFS's $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ memory cost is why Google Maps cannot do BFS at a country scale — it uses **bidirectional A\*** with admissible heuristics (like Euclidean distance) to stay memory-efficient.

### 2.4 Heuristic Functions — The Heart of Informed Search

A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ estimates the cost of the cheapest path from the state at node $n$ to a goal state. Two properties are sacrosanct for KTU examinations:

**Property 1 — Admissibility:**
$$ h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n \in S $$

An admissible heuristic *never overestimates* the true cost to the goal. It is an *optimistic* estimate.

**Property 2 — Consistency (Monotonicity):**
$$ h(n) \leq C(n, a, n') + h(n') \quad \forall \text{ edges } (n, n') $$

The estimated cost from $n$ is no greater than the step cost to $n'$ plus the estimated cost from $n'$. Every consistent heuristic is admissible, but not vice versa.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "If $h$ is admissible, is A\* optimal?" → **Yes**, if the search is on a finite graph or with finite branching. Consistency adds optimality even when finding a goal at a non-optimal depth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why BFS Achieves Optimal Path Cost for Uniform Step Costs

**Given:** A search tree with branching factor $b$ and shallowest goal at depth $d$. All step costs are equal to 1.

**To Prove:** BFS returns the goal with minimum depth $d$, hence minimum cost.

**Step 1 — BFS expands nodes in non-decreasing order of depth.**
BFS uses a FIFO queue. All nodes at depth $k$ are dequeued before any node at depth $k+1$.

**Step 2 — Mathematical induction on depth.**
*Base case:* $k=0$ — only the root is at depth 0. BFS tests the root first.

*Inductive step:* Assume BFS has tested all nodes at depths $\leq k$. When BFS dequeues a node at depth $k$, all nodes at depth $k$ have already been dequeued (by the FIFO discipline). Therefore, no node at depth $k+1$ can be dequeued before all nodes at depth $k$ are processed.

**Step 3 — Termination at depth $d$.**
The first time a goal node is dequeued, every node at shallower depths $\{0, 1, \dots, d-1\}$ has already been dequeued. Since the goal is at depth $d$, no goal exists at a shallower level (by definition of $d$).

**Step 4 — Optimality conclusion.**
Path cost = number of edges $\times$ unit cost = $d \times 1 = d$. No other path can have fewer than $d$ edges, hence BFS is optimal. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: A* Optimality Under Admissible Heuristic

**Given:** A* expands nodes in order of $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, where $h$ is admissible.

**To Prove:** A* returns an optimal goal node.

**Step 1 — Lower bound on unexpanded goals.**
Let $G$ be any unexpanded goal node on the optimal path. Its actual cost is $g(G) = C^*$.
By admissibility: $h(G) = 0$ (heuristic at goal is zero by definition of admissible functions on goal states).
Therefore: $f(G) = g(G) + h(G) = C^* + 0 = C^*$.

**Step 2 — Upper bound on any node $n$ in the frontier.**
For any frontier node $n$, there exists some goal $G^*$ reachable through it. So:
$$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + h^*(n) = g(G^*) $$

**Step 3 — Bounding $f(n)$.**
Since $G^*$ is on *some* path, $g(G^*) \geq C^*$ (the optimal cost is the minimum).
Thus: $f(n) \leq g(G^*)$, but we need a tighter bound.

For the specific path through $n$ to its eventual goal: the cost is at least $g(n) + $ (true remaining cost) $\geq C^*$.

So: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + h^*(n) \leq C^*$.

**Step 4 — Termination of A*.**
A* terminates when the dequeued node $n$ satisfies the goal test. At this point, $f(n) \leq C^*$ (by the bound above). Since $f(n) = g(n)$ at a goal, $g(n) \leq C^*$. But $C^*$ is the minimum, so $g(n) = C^*$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: UCS Frontier Evolution

Consider a graph with the following directed edges and step costs:

| Edge | Step Cost |
|------|-----------|
| $S \rightarrow A$ | 1 |
| $S \rightarrow B$ | 5 |
| $A \rightarrow B$ | 2 |
| $A \rightarrow G$ | 8 |
| $B \rightarrow G$ | 3 |

Goal: Find the minimum-cost path from $S$ to $G$ using **Uniform Cost Search**.

**Iteration Trace:**

| Step | Frontier (min-heap by $g$) | Node Expanded | Resulting Children |
|------|----------------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| 0 | $\{(S, 0)\}$ | — | — |
| 1 | $\{(A, 1), (B, 5)\}$ | $S$ | $A, B$ |
| 2 | $\{(B, 3), (B, 5), (G, 9)\}$ | $A$ | $B'(g=3), G(g=9)$ |
| 3 | $\{(B, 5), (G, 9), (G, 6)\}$ | $B$ ($g=3$ wins) | $G(g=6)$ |
| 4 | $\{(G, 6), (G, 9)\}$ | $G$ ($g=6$) | **GOAL FOUND** |

**Optimal Path:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow G$ with cost $1 + 2 + 3 = 6$.

> Notice how the cheaper path to $B$ via $A$ ($g=3$) overwrites the direct $S \rightarrow B$ entry ($g=5$) in the frontier. This **path-maximization** in the frontier is a hallmark of graph search.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Algorithm

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Justification |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| Shallow tree, uniform costs | **BFS** | Optimal, complete, simple to implement |
| Deep tree, memory constrained | **DFS or DLS** | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ memory |
| Deep tree, solution depth unknown | **IDS** | Combines BFS optimality with DFS memory efficiency |
| Variable step costs | **UCS** | Dijkstra-like; optimal for any non-negative costs |
| Good domain knowledge available | **Greedy Best-First** | Fast, but not optimal |
| Need optimality + speed | **A\*** with admissible heuristic | Industry standard (Google Maps, robotics) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Search Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Problem Definition P] --> B1[Initialize Frontier with Start Node]
    B1 --> C1{Frontier Empty?}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Return Failure]
    C1 -- No --> E1[Select Node from Frontier]
    E1 --> F1{Goal Test Pass?}
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[Return Solution Path]
    F1 -- No --> H1[Expand Node Generate Successors]
    H1 --> I1{Graph Search?}
    I1 -- Yes --> J1[Filter via Explored Set]
    I1 -- No --> K1[Add All Successors]
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Update Frontier with Strategy]
    L1 --> C1
```

> **Reading Guide:** Every search algorithm (BFS, DFS, A*, etc.) plugs into node **L1** with its own insertion rule. BFS uses a FIFO queue, DFS uses a LIFO stack, A* uses a min-heap ordered by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

### 4.2 State Space Search Tree Expansion — Schematic (BFS vs DFS)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode((Start S)) --> n1[Node A depth 1]
    rootNode((Start S)) --> n2[Node B depth 1]
    rootNode((Start S)) --> n3[Node C depth 1]
    n1 --> n11[Node D depth 2]
    n1 --> n12[Node E depth 2]
    n2 --> n21[Node F depth 2]
    n2 --> n22[Node G depth 2 GOAL]
    n3 --> n31[Node H depth 2]
    n3 --> n32[Node I depth 2]
```

> **How to read:** In **BFS**, nodes are explored level by level: S → A, B, C → D, E, F, G (goal found here). In **DFS**, the order would be S → A → D → ... going deep along the leftmost branch first.

### 4.3 A* Search Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Initialize f = g + h] --> P1[Insert Start Node into Open List]
    P1 --> P2{Pick Lowest f Node}
    P2 --> P3{Is Goal Node?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Return Optimal Path]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Move to Closed List]
    P5 --> P6[Generate Successors]
    P6 --> P7[Compute g h f for Each]
    P7 --> P8{Better Path Found?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Update Parent and g value]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Discard]
    P9 --> P11[Insert into Open List]
    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P2
```

### 4.4 Search Strategy Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Search Strategies] --> S1[Uninformed Blind]
    S0 --> S2[Informed Heuristic]
    S1 --> S1a[BFS]
    S1 --> S1b[DFS]
    S1 --> S1c[UCS]
    S1 --> S1d[DLS IDS]
    S2 --> S2a[Greedy Best First]
    S2 --> S2b[A Star Search]
    S2 --> S2c[IDA Star]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define the following terms with respect to problem solving in AI: (i) State Space, (ii) Goal Test, (iii) Path Cost.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **State Space:** The complete set of all possible states reachable from the initial state by applying any sequence of valid actions. Formally, if $S$ is the state space, $S = \{s \mid s = Result^*(s_0, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n), a_i \in A\}$.

(ii) **Goal Test:** A Boolean function $GoalTest(s)$ that returns *True* if state $s$ satisfies the goal condition. It is evaluated on every node that is selected from the frontier for expansion.

(iii) **Path Cost:** A non-negative numerical function $C(s_0, a_1, s_1, a_2, \dots, s_n)$ that assigns a cost to a sequence of state transitions. The agent's objective is to find a solution path that *minimizes* this cost. *(3 marks — 1 mark per definition with example.)*

---

**Q2.** What is a heuristic function? State the two essential properties that make a heuristic suitable for A* search.

**Model Answer:**

A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ is a domain-specific estimate of the cost of the cheapest path from the state at node $n$ to a goal state. It is a "rule of thumb" that guides informed search.

**Two essential properties for A\*:**

1. **Admissibility:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$ — the heuristic never overestimates the true cost. This guarantees A\* returns an optimal solution.

2. **Consistency (Monotonicity):** $h(n) \leq C(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for all edges $(n, n')$ — the estimated cost is no greater than the step cost plus the successor's estimated cost. This guarantees $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path, allowing A\* to be optimal even with early termination. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for properties with equations.)*

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing search tree with state space** — examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify "graph search" vs "tree search"** when discussing explored sets.
> 3. **Using $h(n) \geq h^*(n)$ for admissible** (it is $\leq$, the inequality direction is reversed — a classic blunder).
> 4. **Not stating completeness/optimality conditions** for any algorithm you list.
> 5. **Skipping heuristic admissibility proof** when justifying A\* optimality — examiners expect 2–3 lines minimum.

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the generic search algorithm with a neat flowchart. Compare BFS and DFS in terms of completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **generic search algorithm** is the universal skeleton for all search strategies. The flowchart was shown in Section 4.1. Key elements:
- **Frontier (Open List):** Set of unexpanded nodes.
- **Explored Set (Closed List):** For graph search, stores already-expanded nodes to avoid loops.
- **Selection Rule:** The strategy-defining choice — FIFO (BFS), LIFO (DFS), priority (UCS, A*).

**BFS vs DFS Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | BFS | DFS |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Data structure | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack |
| Complete? | **Yes** (if $b$ finite) | **No** (fails in infinite/loopy spaces) |
| Optimal? | **Yes** (unit step cost) | **No** |
| Time | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ |
| Space | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(bm)$ |

**When to use BFS:** Shallow solutions, uniform costs, small branching factor.
**When to use DFS:** Deep trees, memory-constrained environments, many solutions exist.

---

**(b)** What is A* search? Explain the role of the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. With a suitable example, demonstrate how A* finds the optimal path. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**A\* Search Definition:** A* is a best-first search that uses an evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ to prioritize nodes in the frontier. It is a generalization of UCS (where $h(n) = 0$).

**Role of $f(n)$:**
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$ (known exactly).
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to goal (heuristic).
- $f(n)$ = estimated cost of the *cheapest solution passing through $n$*.

A* always expands the node with the **lowest $f$ value** in the frontier, ensuring that when a goal is selected, no other unexpanded node can yield a cheaper path (assuming $h$ is admissible).

**Numerical Example:** Consider the Romania-style graph:
- $S \rightarrow A$: cost 3, $h(A) = 4$
- $S \rightarrow B$: cost 2, $h(B) = 5$
- $A \rightarrow G$: cost 5, $h(G) = 0$
- $B \rightarrow G$: cost 7, $h(G) = 0$

**A* Trace:**

| Step | Frontier | Selected | f value |
|------|----------|----------|---------|
| 0 | $\{(S, 0+10)\}$ | $S$ | 10 |
| 1 | $\{(A, 3+4=7), (B, 2+5=7)\}$ | Tie | — |
| 2 | Expand $A$ → $\{(B, 7), (G, 8+0=8)\}$ | $B$ (f=7) | 7 |
| 3 | $\{(G, 8), (G, 9)\}$ | $G$ (f=8) | 8 |

**Optimal Path:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow G$ with cost $3 + 5 = 8$ (verified optimal by enumeration).

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Define heuristic function. Explain admissibility and consistency with examples. Why is consistency a stronger condition than admissibility? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Heuristic Function:** A heuristic $h: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ maps a state to an *estimated* cost of reaching the nearest goal.

**Admissibility:**
A heuristic is admissible if it never overestimates the true cost:
$$ h(n) \leq h^*(n) $$
*Example:* In route-finding, the **straight-line distance (SLD)** to the goal is admissible for road networks because the actual road distance is always $\geq$ the Euclidean distance.

**Consistency (Monotonicity):**
A heuristic is consistent if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$:
$$ h(n) \leq C(n, a, n') + h(n') $$
*Example:* In a tile-sliding puzzle (8-puzzle), the heuristic $h(n) = $ number of misplaced tiles is consistent because moving a tile can decrease the count by at most 1, matching the unit step cost.

**Why Consistency is Stronger:**
- Every consistent heuristic is admissible (proved by triangle-inequality-style induction along a path).
- But not every admissible heuristic is consistent.
- Consistency guarantees that $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path. This means A* can be implemented with a **graph search** (using an explored/closed set) without losing optimality, because once a node is expanded, it is guaranteed to be on the optimal path.
- An admissible-but-inconsistent heuristic may cause A* to re-expand nodes that were previously closed, since a later, better path may be discovered.

---

**(b)** Discuss Uniform Cost Search (UCS) with its algorithmic procedure. Compare UCS with BFS. Show UCS trace on a graph where step costs differ. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**UCS Definition:** Uniform Cost Search expands the node in the frontier with the **lowest path cost $g(n)$**, irrespective of depth. It is essentially Dijkstra's algorithm applied to goal-directed search.

**Procedure:**
1. Insert start node $S$ with $g(S) = 0$ into a priority queue (min-heap on $g$).
2. Dequeue the node with minimum $g$. Apply goal test.
3. If goal → return path. Else, expand and insert each child with $g(child) = g(n) + C(n, a, child)$.
4. If a child is already in the frontier with a higher $g$, replace it.

**Comparison with BFS:**

| Property | BFS | UCS |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Order | Depth, then $g$ | Strictly by $g$ |
| Step cost assumption | Uniform | Non-negative, can vary |
| Optimal? | Yes (if uniform) | Yes (if all $\geq 0$) |
| Equivalent to | UCS with $C=1$ | Dijkstra's algorithm |

**Trace Example:** Already worked out in Section 3.3 of these notes, showing the optimal path $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow G$ with cost 6. *(3 marks for the trace table reproduction.)*

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A search problem = 5-tuple $(S, A, s_0, G, C)$ — *memorize this exactly*.
- ✅ **BFS** = complete + optimal for uniform cost, but $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ memory.
- ✅ **DFS** = not complete in infinite spaces, not optimal, but $\mathcal{O}(bm)$ memory.
- ✅ **Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)** = best of both — complete, optimal, $\mathcal{O}(bd)$ memory.
- ✅ **Uniform Cost Search (UCS)** = expands by $g(n)$ only; optimal for non-negative costs.
- ✅ **A\* = UCS + heuristic**, $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- ✅ **Admissibility:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates.
- ✅ **Consistency:** $h(n) \leq C(n, a, n') + h(n')$ — stronger than admissibility.
- ✅ A* with **admissible** heuristic → **optimal**.
- ✅ A* with **consistent** heuristic → **optimal + no re-expansions** (graph search safe).
- ✅ Distinguish **search tree** (active exploration) from **state space** (full graph).
- ✅ **Heuristic examples:** 8-puzzle uses *misplaced tiles* or *Manhattan distance*; route-finding uses *Euclidean distance*.
- ✅ KTU exam tip: Always cite **completeness, optimality, time, space** for any algorithm you write.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** that enables computer systems to **learn patterns from data** and **improve their performance on a given task** over time *without being explicitly programmed*.

The two classical academic definitions of Machine Learning, frequently quoted in KTU examinations, are:

**Arthur Samuel (1959) – The Pioneer Definition:**
> *"Field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

**Tom M. Mitchell (1997) – The Formal Operational Definition (Highly Examinable):**
> *"A computer program is said to learn from **Experience (E)** with respect to some class of **Tasks (T)** and **Performance measure (P)**, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

The Mitchell definition is mathematically expressible as the triple $\langle E, T, P \rangle$ and is a guaranteed **high-yield KTU question**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – Teaching a Child to Recognise Fruits 🍎🍌**
>
> Imagine teaching a toddler to distinguish an *apple* from a *banana*:
>
> 1. **Data (Experience):** You show the child 100 labelled fruits — "This is an apple, this is a banana."
> 2. **Features (Inputs):** The child subconsciously learns features like *colour, shape, texture, size*.
> 3. **Model (Brain):** The child's brain forms an internal rule: *"Round + Red ≈ Apple, Long + Yellow ≈ Banana."*
> 4. **Testing (Generalisation):** You show a *new, unseen* fruit. The child correctly identifies it.
> 5. **Improvement (Learning):** Each mistake corrects the internal rule, improving future predictions.
>
> A Machine Learning algorithm does **exactly this** — it ingests data, identifies hidden patterns, builds a mathematical model, and generalises to unseen inputs.

---

## 1.3 Why Machine Learning? The Motivation

Traditional rule-based programming fails when:
- Rules are too **numerous** or **complex** (e.g., spam detection, autonomous driving).
- The environment **changes dynamically** (e.g., stock markets).
- Human expertise is **unavailable** (e.g., genomics, cosmic signal detection).

ML solves these by letting the **algorithm discover the rules** from data.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Buzzwords to Memorise:** *Data-driven, Pattern recognition, Generalisation, Inductive bias, Hypothesis space, Training, Inference.*

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology (Foundational Glossary)

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Dataset** | Collection of data instances used for learning | `iris.csv` |
| **Instance / Sample** | A single row of data | One flower record |
| **Feature (x)** | An input variable / attribute | `sepal_length` |
| **Label (y)** | The target output to be predicted | `species` |
| **Feature Space (X)** | The n-dimensional space of all features | $\mathbb{R}^{4}$ |
| **Hypothesis (h)** | The function the model learns to map $X \to Y$ | $h(x) = \text{sign}(w^Tx + b)$ |
| **Model** | The hypothesis + learned parameters | Linear classifier |
| **Training** | The process of learning parameters from data | Fitting weights |
| **Testing / Inference** | Using the trained model on unseen data | Predicting test set labels |
| **Generalisation** | Ability to perform well on **unseen** data | Low test error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:**
> **Generalisation** = the model's capacity to map inputs to outputs correctly on data *it has never encountered* during training. Poor generalisation is the **primary cause of model failure** in KTU practical examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Machine Learning

Machine Learning is broadly classified into **three primary paradigms**, each governed by the **nature of the learning signal** (feedback) available to the algorithm.

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning 📘

- **Definition:** The algorithm learns from a dataset that contains **input-output pairs** $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- **Goal:** Learn a mapping function $f: X \to Y$.
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Classification** — Discrete categorical outputs (e.g., spam / not-spam, disease / no-disease).
  - **Regression** — Continuous numerical outputs (e.g., house price, temperature).
- **Algorithms:** Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, k-NN, Neural Networks.
- **Real-world Applications:** Email filtering, credit scoring, medical diagnosis, weather forecasting.

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning 📙

- **Definition:** The algorithm learns from data containing **only inputs** $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with **no labels**.
- **Goal:** Discover hidden structure, patterns, or groupings within the data.
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **Clustering** — Grouping similar instances (e.g., customer segmentation).
  - **Dimensionality Reduction** — Compressing high-dimensional data (e.g., PCA, t-SNE).
  - **Association Rule Mining** — Finding co-occurrence rules (e.g., market-basket analysis).
- **Algorithms:** K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical Clustering, PCA, Autoencoders.
- **Real-world Applications:** Customer segmentation, anomaly detection, recommendation systems, gene expression analysis.

### 2.1.3 Reinforcement Learning 📕

- **Definition:** An **agent** learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an **environment**, receiving **rewards** or **penalties** for actions.
- **Goal:** Learn an optimal **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximises **cumulative reward**.
- **Key Elements:** Agent, Environment, State ($s$), Action ($a$), Reward ($r$), Policy ($\pi$), Value Function ($V(s)$).
- **Algorithms:** Q-Learning, SARSA, Deep Q-Networks (DQN), Policy Gradient, PPO.
- **Real-world Applications:** Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero), robotics, autonomous vehicles, trading bots.

### 2.1.4 (Optional / Advanced) Semi-Supervised & Self-Supervised Learning

- **Semi-Supervised:** A small labelled set + a large unlabelled set (e.g., medical imaging where labels are expensive).
- **Self-Supervised:** Labels are auto-generated from the data itself (e.g., BERT pretraining using masked tokens).

---

## 2.2 The Standard Machine Learning Workflow (Pipeline)

A typical KTU-practicals-compliant ML pipeline follows these six stages:

1. **Problem Definition** — Identify the task $T$ and metric $P$.
2. **Data Collection** — Gather the dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}$.
3. **Data Pre-processing** — Handle missing values, encode categorical variables, normalise features, split into **train / validation / test** sets (typical split: **70% / 15% / 15%**).
4. **Model Selection** — Choose an appropriate algorithm based on data size, linearity, and interpretability.
5. **Training** — Optimise model parameters by minimising a **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ on the training set.
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Measure performance using the chosen metric $P$ on the test set, then deploy.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all foundational mathematical relations** required at the KTU 2024 ESE level for Module 1.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Unit |
|---|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Mitchell's Learning Definition | Program improves $P$ on $T$ using $E$ | Triple $\langle E, T, P \rangle$ |
| 2 | Hypothesis Function | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | Linear hypothesis example |
| 3 | Empirical Risk (Training Error) | $R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(h_{\theta}(x_i), y_i)$ | Average loss over training set |
| 4 | Expected Risk (True Error) | $R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{data}} \left[ \mathcal{L}(h_{\theta}(x), y) \right]$ | True generalisation error |
| 5 | Mean Squared Error (Regression) | $MSE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Unit: square of output |
| 6 | Mean Absolute Error (Regression) | $MAE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Unit: same as output |
| 7 | 0-1 Loss (Classification) | $\mathcal{L}_{0\text{-}1} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}(\hat{y}_i \neq y_i)$ | Misclassification rate |
| 8 | Accuracy | $Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 9 | Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 10 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 11 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ |
| 12 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = Bias^2 + Variance + \sigma^2$ | Total expected error |
| 13 | Bias Term | $Bias^2 = ( \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x) )^2$ | Squared systematic error |
| 14 | Variance Term | $Variance = \mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$ | Sensitivity to training data |
| 15 | Irreducible Noise | $\sigma^2 = Var(\epsilon)$ | Inherent data randomness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute-value expressions (e.g., $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$) are rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` command instead of the vertical pipe symbol `\vert`, to preserve Markdown table integrity. Examiners should do the same in their answer scripts.

---

## 2.4 The Bias-Variance Trade-off (Conceptually Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bias-Variance Trade-off** is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU Module 1.

- **High Bias (Underfitting):** The model is **too simple** to capture the underlying pattern. Both training and test errors are **high**.
- **High Variance (Overfitting):** The model is **too complex** and memorises noise in training data. Training error is **low**, but test error is **high**.
- **The Goal:** Find the sweet spot that **minimises the total expected error** $Bias^2 + Variance + \sigma^2$.

| Model Complexity | Bias | Variance | Test Error |
|------------------|------|----------|------------|
| Very Low (e.g., constant model) | High | Low | High |
| Optimal (sweet spot) | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** |
| Very High (deep model) | Low | High | High |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM) Principle

The fundamental principle of supervised learning is the **Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM)** theorem, derived below in a fully expanded step-by-step format suitable for a 7-mark KTU sub-question.

### Step 1 — Define the True Risk Function

We begin with the population (true) risk, which is the expectation of the loss over the *true* but unknown data distribution $P_{data}(x, y)$:

$$
R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim P_{data}} \left[ \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_{\theta}(x),\, y\bigr) \right]
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* $R(\theta)$ is the *expected loss* the model will incur over the *entire infinite* data distribution — this is what we ultimately want to minimise.

### Step 2 — Expand the Expectation as an Integral

By the definition of expectation for a continuous distribution:

$$
R(\theta) = \int\int \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_{\theta}(x),\, y\bigr) \cdot P_{data}(x, y) \, dx \, dy
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* We integrate the loss function weighted by the joint probability density over the entire input-output space.

### Step 3 — Acknowledge the Practical Intractability

In real-world ML, $P_{data}(x, y)$ is **unknown** — we only observe a finite sample dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$. Therefore, computing the true risk $R(\theta)$ is **mathematically impossible**.

> *Textual interpretation:* This is the central dilemma of statistical learning: we cannot minimise what we cannot compute.

### Step 4 — Approximate the True Distribution with the Empirical Distribution

Replace the unknown $P_{data}(x, y)$ with the **empirical distribution** $P_{emp}(x, y)$, which places a point mass of $\frac{1}{N}$ on each observed sample:

$$
P_{emp}(x, y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta\bigl((x, y) - (x_i, y_i)\bigr)
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta function — it effectively "spreads" equal probability mass $1/N$ over each training sample.

### Step 5 — Substitute the Empirical Distribution into the Risk Integral

$$
R_{emp}(\theta) = \int\int \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_{\theta}(x),\, y\bigr) \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta\bigl((x, y) - (x_i, y_i)\bigr) \right] dx \, dy
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The double integral "picks out" the loss values at the exact training points because the delta function is non-zero only at the sample locations.

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Integral (Collapse via Sifting Property)

Applying the **sifting property of the Dirac delta**:

$$
R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_{\theta}(x_i),\, y_i\bigr)
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The empirical risk is simply the **average loss** over the $N$ training samples — a quantity we *can* compute.

### Step 7 — State the ERM Objective

The **Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM) Principle** is therefore:

$$
\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; R_{emp}(\theta) = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(h_{\theta}(x_i),\, y_i\bigr)
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "Choose the model parameters $\hat{\theta}$ that minimise the average training loss." This is the **fundamental principle underlying virtually every supervised ML algorithm** — from Linear Regression to Deep Neural Networks.

### Step 8 — Worked Numerical Example (Linear Regression with MSE Loss)

Suppose we have $N = 3$ data points and a **simple linear hypothesis** $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta x$ with $\theta = 2$ (current estimate).

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i = 2 x_i$ | Error $y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | Squared Error |
|----|-------|-------|---------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 2 | 5 | 4 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 | 6 | 0 | 0 |

Applying the MSE formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{emp}(\theta) &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{3} (1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2) \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* With $\theta = 2$, our model produces an average squared error of **0.667**. ERM would now ask: "Is there a $\theta$ that gives a smaller value?" — leading directly to the **Gradient Descent** optimisation procedure studied in later modules.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bias-Variance Decomposition (Predictive Error)

A 7-mark derivation that appears frequently in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

### Step 1 — Start with the Squared Error of a Single Prediction

For a fixed test point $x$ with true target $y = f(x) + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\text{Error}(x) = (y - \hat{f}(x))^2
$$

### Step 2 — Take the Expectation Over the Training Data Distribution

We wish to compute the *expected* prediction error:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Add and Subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$

A standard algebraic trick: introduce the *expected prediction* $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ into the expression.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\bigl(y - \bar{f} + \bar{f} - \hat{f}\bigr)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[(y - \bar{f})^2\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2\right] + 2 \mathbb{E}\left[(y - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* We used the identity $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + 2ab$ with $a = y - \bar{f}$ and $b = \bar{f} - \hat{f}$.

### Step 4 — Eliminate the Cross Term

The cross-term vanishes because $\bar{f} - \hat{f}$ is independent of the noise $\epsilon$ and has zero mean over the training data:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\right] = (\bar{f} - \bar{f}) \cdot \mathbb{E}[\bar{f} - \hat{f}] = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Expand the First Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \bar{f})^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[(f + \epsilon - \bar{f})^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[(f - \bar{f})^2\right] + 2 \mathbb{E}\left[\epsilon (f - \bar{f})\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[\epsilon^2\right] \\
&= (f - \bar{f})^2 + 0 + \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The cross-term with noise vanishes because $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$, leaving the deterministic bias squared plus the irreducible noise variance.

### Step 6 — Combine to Get the Canonical Decomposition

$$
\boxed{\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2}_{Bias^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]}_{Variance} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{Irreducible\;Noise}}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The total expected prediction error on unseen data is the sum of three unavoidable sources — the model's systematic deviation from the truth (**Bias²**), its sensitivity to the specific training sample (**Variance**), and the inherent randomness in the data (**$\sigma^2$**).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Machine Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    problemStart["STEP 1 PROBLEM DEFINITION Identify Task T and Metric P"] --> dataCollect["STEP 2 DATA COLLECTION Gather dataset D"]
    dataCollect --> preprocess["STEP 3 DATA PREPROCESSING Clean, Normalize, Encode"]
    preprocess --> split["STEP 3B TRAIN VALIDATION TEST SPLIT 70 15 15"]
    split --> modelSelect["STEP 4 MODEL SELECTION Choose algorithm"]
    modelSelect --> train["STEP 5 TRAINING Minimize Loss Function L theta on training set"]
    train --> evalCheck{"STEP 6A EVALUATION Generalization gap acceptable"}
    evalCheck -- YES --> deploy["STEP 6B DEPLOYMENT Serve model in production"]
    evalCheck -- NO --> tune["STEP 6C HYPERPARAMETER TUNING Adjust and retrain"]
    tune --> train
    deploy --> monitor["STEP 7 MONITORING Track performance drift"]
    monitor --> tune
```

> *Explanation:* The diagram maps the **closed-loop ML pipeline** from problem definition to live monitoring. The feedback arrow from monitoring back to tuning illustrates that deployed ML systems are **never static** — they require continuous re-training as data distributions drift (concept drift).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Taxonomy of Machine Learning Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mlRoot["MACHINE LEARNING"] --> sup["SUPERVISED LEARNING Labelled data x y"]
    mlRoot --> unsup["UNSUPERVISED LEARNING Unlabelled data x only"]
    mlRoot --> rl["REINFORCEMENT LEARNING Agent environment interaction"]
    mlRoot --> semi["SEMI SUPERVISED LEARNING Few labels many unlabelled"]

    sup --> supClass["CLASSIFICATION Discrete output"]
    sup --> supReg["REGRESSION Continuous output"]

    supClass --> algClassA["Logistic Regression"]
    supClass --> algClassB["Decision Tree"]
    supClass --> algClassC["SVM"]
    supClass --> algClassD["k Nearest Neighbour"]

    supReg --> algRegA["Linear Regression"]
    supReg --> algRegB["Random Forest Regression"]
    supReg --> algRegC["Neural Network Regression"]

    unsup --> unsupClust["CLUSTERING Group similar instances"]
    unsup --> unsupDr["DIMENSIONALITY REDUCTION Compress features"]
    unsup --> unsupAsso["ASSOCIATION RULE MINING Co occurrence patterns"]

    unsupClust --> algClustA["K Means"]
    unsupClust --> algClustB["DBSCAN"]
    unsupDr --> algDrA["PCA"]
    unsupDr --> algDrB["t SNE"]

    rl --> rlEle["AGENT ENVIRONMENT STATE ACTION REWARD POLICY"]
    rlEle --> rlAlgA["Q Learning"]
    rlEle --> rlAlgB["Deep Q Network DQN"]
    rlEle --> rlAlgC["Policy Gradient PPO"]
```

> *Explanation:* This taxonomy diagram presents the **complete hierarchical view** of ML paradigms along with their canonical algorithms. In KTU examinations, students are expected to identify the correct paradigm from the problem statement — e.g., "predict house prices" → *Supervised Regression*; "segment customers" → *Unsupervised Clustering*; "play chess against an opponent" → *Reinforcement Learning*.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Bias-Variance Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lowC["LOW COMPLEXITY"] -->|"Bias dominates"| underfit["UNDERFITTING High train error High test error"]
    midC["OPTIMAL COMPLEXITY Sweet spot"] -->|"Bias and Variance balanced"| goodFit["GOOD FIT Minimum test error"]
    highC["HIGH COMPLEXITY"] -->|"Variance dominates"| overfit["OVERFITTING Low train error High test error"]

    underfit --> remedy1["REMEDY Increase model complexity Add more features"]
    overfit --> remedy2["REMEDY Regularization L1 L2 More training data Drop features Early stopping"]
    goodFit --> keep["DEPLOY Generalises well"]
```

> *Explanation:* The schematic illustrates the **non-monotonic relationship** between model complexity and test error. KTU students must remember: as complexity increases, *bias decreases* monotonically, but *variance increases* monotonically — the *sum* of the two yields the characteristic U-shaped test error curve.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Machine Learning. State and explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Machine Learning is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance over time without being explicitly programmed.

**Mitchell's Definition (1.5 Marks):** *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

**Example (0.5 Mark):** In a spam classification system:
- **Task (T):** Classify emails as spam or not-spam.
- **Experience (E):** The labelled dataset of historical emails.
- **Performance (P):** Classification accuracy (or F1-score) on a held-out test set.

As the system processes more labelled emails (E increases), its accuracy (P) on classifying new emails (T) improves.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Format Recommended):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|--------|------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Data Type** | Labelled $(x, y)$ | Unlabelled $(x)$ | Interaction tuples $(s, a, r)$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure | Maximise cumulative reward |
| **Feedback** | Direct (labels) | None | Delayed reward signal |
| **Example** | Email spam classification | Customer segmentation using K-Means | AlphaGo learning to play Go |
