| Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - y_j)^{2}}$ | Straight-line distance between vectors |
| Manhattan Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | City-block distance |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Angular similarity (ignores magnitude) |
| Dot Product | $\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j y_j$ | Inner product, foundation of similarity |
| Min-Max Normalization | $x_{j}^{\prime} = \dfrac{x_j - \min(x_j)}{\max(x_j) - \min(x_j)}$ | Scales to $[0, 1]$ |
| Z-Score Standardization | $x_{j}^{\prime} = \dfrac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ | Zero mean, unit variance |
| Mean of feature $j$ | $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_j^{(i)}$ | Centroid coordinate |
| Variance of feature $j$ | $\sigma_j^{2} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_j^{(i)} - \mu_j)^{2}$ | Spread along axis $j$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Alert:** The symbol $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ denotes the norm (vector magnitude). In the KTU answer sheet, always state *which norm* (L1, L2, etc.) you are using — examiners will deduct marks for ambiguity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Computer Vision:** An image is converted into a feature vector of pixel intensities (e.g., $28 \times 28 = 784$-dimensional vector for MNIST digits).
- **Natural Language Processing:** Text documents are converted into feature vectors via **Bag-of-Words**, **TF-IDF**, or **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe)**.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression levels for thousands of genes form ultra-high-dimensional feature vectors used in cancer classification.
- **Recommender Systems:** User and item profiles are feature vectors; recommendations arise from similarity computations between them.
- **IoT & Sensor Networks:** Multi-sensor readings at each timestamp form a feature vector, enabling anomaly detection and predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Z-Score Standardization of a Feature Vector

**Problem Statement:** Given a feature column $x_j$ with $m$ observations, derive a transformation that rescales the feature to have **zero mean** and **unit variance**.

### Step 1 — Define the Desired Properties of the Transformed Feature

We want a new feature $x_j^{\prime}$ such that:

$$\mathbb{E}[x_j^{\prime}] = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Var}(x_j^{\prime}) = 1$$

### Step 2 — Propose a Linear Transformation

We hypothesize a linear transformation of the form:

$$x_j^{\prime} = a \cdot x_j + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are constants to be determined.

### Step 3 — Apply the Zero-Mean Condition

Taking the expectation of both sides:

$$\mathbb{E}[x_j^{\prime}] = a \cdot \mathbb{E}[x_j] + b = 0$$

Solving for $b$:

$$b = -a \cdot \mu_j$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Transformation

$$x_j^{\prime} = a \cdot x_j - a \cdot \mu_j = a \cdot (x_j - \mu_j)$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Unit-Variance Condition

Taking the variance of both sides (using $\text{Var}(aX) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$):

$$\text{Var}(x_j^{\prime}) = a^{2} \cdot \text{Var}(x_j) = 1$$

Solving for $a$:

$$a = \frac{1}{\sigma_j}$$

### Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Expression

Substituting $a$ and $b$ back:

$$\boxed{\;x_j^{\prime} = \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}\;}$$

**Verification of Properties:**

$$\mathbb{E}[x_j^{\prime}] = \frac{\mathbb{E}[x_j] - \mu_j}{\sigma_j} = \frac{\mu_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\text{Var}(x_j^{\prime}) = \frac{1}{\sigma_j^{2}} \cdot \text{Var}(x_j - \mu_j) = \frac{\sigma_j^{2}}{\sigma_j^{2}} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Euclidean Distance Between Feature Vectors

**Problem:** Given two feature vectors representing two houses for sale:

$$\mathbf{x} = [3, \; 1500, \; 2]^{\top} \quad \text{(3 bedrooms, 1500 sq.ft., 2 bathrooms)}$$

$$\mathbf{y} = [4, \; 2000, \; 3]^{\top} \quad \text{(4 bedrooms, 2000 sq.ft., 3 bathrooms)}$$

Compute the **Euclidean distance** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$.

### Step 1 — Write the Euclidean Distance Formula

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - y_j)^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Component-Wise Differences

$$\Delta_1 = 3 - 4 = -1$$

$$\Delta_2 = 1500 - 2000 = -500$$

$$\Delta_3 = 2 - 3 = -1$$

### Step 3 — Square Each Difference

$$\Delta_1^{2} = (-1)^{2} = 1$$

$$\Delta_2^{2} = (-500)^{2} = 250000$$

$$\Delta_3^{2} = (-1)^{2} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Squared Differences

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} (x_j - y_j)^{2} = 1 + 250000 + 1 = 250002$$

### Step 5 — Take the Square Root

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{250002} \approx 500.002$$

**Interpretation:** The two houses are approximately **500 units apart** in the feature space, with the difference being **dominated almost entirely by the square-footage feature** — illustrating the *curse of unscaled features*, which is exactly why standardization is critical before applying distance-based algorithms.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Cosine Similarity

**Problem:** Compute the cosine similarity between two document feature vectors (word counts):

$$\mathbf{x} = [1, \; 1, \; 2, \; 0, \; 1]^{\top} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{y} = [0, \; 2, \; 1, \; 1, \; 0]^{\top}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Dot Product

$$\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = (1)(0) + (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 0 + 2 + 2 + 0 + 0 = 4$$

### Step 2 — Compute the L2 Norms

$$\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{1^{2} + 1^{2} + 2^{2} + 0^{2} + 1^{2}} = \sqrt{7} \approx 2.6458$$

$$\Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{0^{2} + 2^{2} + 1^{2} + 1^{2} + 0^{2}} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.4495$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Cosine Similarity Formula

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{7} \cdot \sqrt{6}} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{42}} \approx \frac{4}{6.4807} \approx 0.6172$$

**Interpretation:** A cosine similarity of $\approx 0.617$ indicates the two documents are **moderately aligned** in their word usage, despite having different overall lengths (this is why cosine similarity is preferred over Euclidean distance for text data).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Feature Vector Engineering Pipeline

The following block-level functional architecture flow describes the complete lifecycle of converting raw, real-world data into a usable feature vector for a machine learning model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Source] --> B[Data Cleaning]
    B --> C[Handle Missing Values]
    C --> D[Categorical Encoding]
    D --> E[Feature Scaling]
    E --> F[Feature Selection]
    F --> G[Final Feature Vector x in Rn]
    G --> H[Concatenate into Feature Matrix X]
    H --> I[Input to ML Model]

    C --> C1[Mean Median Mode Imputation]
    D --> D1[One Hot Encoding]
    D --> D2[Label Encoding]
    E --> E1[Min Max Normalization]
    E --> E2[Z Score Standardization]
    F --> F1[Filter Method]
    F --> F2[Wrapper Method]
    F --> F3[Embedded Method]
    I --> I1[Classifier Regressor Cluster]
    I --> I2[Neural Network Deep Model]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#047857,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#047857,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Geometric Visualization: Feature Vectors as Points in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["x1 = 2 1"] --> SP["Feature Space R2"]
    P2["x2 = 3 4"] --> SP
    P3["x3 = 1 2"] --> SP
    P4["x4 = 5 5"] --> SP
    SP --> Q1["Distance d12 Euclidean Manhattan Cosine"]
    SP --> Q2["Clustering k Means DBSCAN"]
    SP --> Q3["Classification k NN SVM"]
    SP --> Q4["Decision Boundary Hyperplane"]

    style SP fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#facc15,stroke:#854d0e
    style P2 fill:#facc15,stroke:#854d0e
    style P3 fill:#facc15,stroke:#854d0e
    style P4 fill:#facc15,stroke:#854d0e
```

## 4.3 Anatomy of a Single Feature Vector — Coordinate-Wise Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FV["x superscript i Feature Vector"] --> D1["x1 Feature 1 Age"]
    FV --> D2["x2 Feature 2 Income"]
    FV --> D3["x3 Feature 3 Score"]
    FV --> D4["x4 Feature 4 Category Encoded"]
    FV --> D5["x n Feature n Last Feature"]

    D1 --> N1["Numerical Continuous"]
    D2 --> N2["Numerical Continuous"]
    D3 --> N3["Numerical Continuous"]
    D4 --> N4["Numerical after One Hot"]
    D5 --> N5["Type depends on dataset"]

    style FV fill:#6d28d9,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style D2 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style D3 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style D4 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style D5 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a diagram" of the feature engineering process, this 3-stage pipeline (Cleaning → Encoding → Scaling) is the most commonly accepted answer. Always label the *type* of feature at each step.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme question paper patterns. Mark allocation, sub-part structure, and expected answer depth follow the official KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) template.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a feature vector with an example.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style, 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A feature vector is an $n$-dimensional numerical vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^{\top}$ that represents a single data instance using $n$ measurable attributes called features.

**Example:** A fruit can be described by the feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [\text{weight (g)}, \text{color score}, \text{sweetness}]^{\top}$. For a specific apple: $\mathbf{x} = [180, 0.85, 7.2]^{\top}$.

*(Award 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for the correct example.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a feature and a feature vector.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Feature | Feature Vector |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A single measurable attribute | The complete ordered tuple of all features for one instance |
| Dimensionality | Scalar (1-D) | $n$-dimensional ($n$-D) |
| Example | Height = 170 cm | $\mathbf{x} = [170, 65, 22]^{\top}$ |
| Notation | $x_j$ | $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ |

*(Award 1.5 marks for the distinction table, 1.5 marks for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Important:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 questions carry internal choice. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below with full step-by-step model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q(a).** Explain the concept of a feature vector. Discuss feature space and its geometric significance in machine learning with a suitable 2-D example.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition [2 marks]:**
A feature vector is an $n$-dimensional numerical representation of a data point, where each component $x_j$ corresponds to a specific measurable feature. Mathematically:

$$\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Feature Space [2 marks]:**
The feature space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the $n$-dimensional Euclidean space in which all feature vectors of the dataset reside as points. For a dataset with $n$ features, the entire dataset forms a *cloud of $m$ points* in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.

**Step 3 — 2-D Example [2 marks]:**
Consider a dataset of students described by two features: **Study Hours ($x_1$)** and **Exam Score ($x_2$)**. Each student is a point in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$. For example:

$$\text{Student A} = (6, 85)^{\top}, \quad \text{Student B} = (2, 45)^{\top}, \quad \text{Student C} = (5, 78)^{\top}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Significance [1 mark]:**
- Similar students cluster together in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$.
- A linear classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression) draws a *decision boundary* — a straight line in 2-D, a hyperplane in higher dimensions.
- Distance between points quantifies dissimilarity.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q(b).** Given the following 5 samples with 2 features, construct the feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 2}$ and compute the **mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and the **variance** of each feature.

| Sample | Feature 1 ($x_1$) | Feature 2 ($x_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 10 |
| 2 | 4 | 20 |
| 3 | 6 | 30 |
| 4 | 8 | 40 |
| 5 | 10 | 50 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Feature Matrix Construction [1 mark]:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 10 \\ 4 & 20 \\ 6 & 30 \\ 8 & 40 \\ 10 & 50 \end{bmatrix}_{5 \times 2}$$

**Step 2 — Mean of Feature 1 [1 mark]:**

$$\mu_1 = \frac{2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

**Step 3 — Mean of Feature 2 [1 mark]:**

$$\mu_2 = \frac{10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50}{5} = \frac{150}{5} = 30$$

**Step 4 — Mean Vector [1 mark]:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Variance of Feature 1 [1.5 marks]:**

$$\sigma_1^{2} = \frac{1}{5}\left[(2-6)^2 + (4-6)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (8-6)^2 + (10-6)^2\right]$$

$$\sigma_1^{2} = \frac{1}{5}\left[16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16\right] = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

**Step 6 — Variance of Feature 2 [1.5 marks]:**

$$\sigma_2^{2} = \frac{1}{5}\left[(10-30)^2 + (20-30)^2 + (30-30)^2 + (40-30)^2 + (50-30)^2\right]$$

$$\sigma_2^{2} = \frac{1}{5}\left[400 + 100 + 0 + 100 + 400\right] = \frac{1000}{5} = 200$$

**Final Answer:** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = [6, 30]^{\top}$, $\sigma_1^{2} = 8$, $\sigma_2^{2} = 200$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q(a).** Define cosine similarity. Compute the cosine similarity between the following two feature vectors and interpret the result:

$$\mathbf{x} = [3, \; 0, \; 2, \; 5]^{\top}, \quad \mathbf{y} = [1, \; 0, \; 4, \; 3]^{\top}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition [2 marks]:**
Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two non-zero vectors. It is defined as:

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2}$$

It lies in the range $[-1, 1]$, where 1 means perfectly similar in direction, 0 means orthogonal, and $-1$ means opposite.

**Step 2 — Compute the Dot Product [1 mark]:**

$$\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = (3)(1) + (0)(0) + (2)(4) + (5)(3) = 3 + 0 + 8 + 15 = 26$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2$ [1 mark]:**

$$\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{3^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{9 + 0 + 4 + 25} = \sqrt{38} \approx 6.1644$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2$ [1 mark]:**

$$\Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0 + 16 + 9} = \sqrt{26} \approx 5.0990$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Cosine Similarity Formula [1 mark]:**

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{26}{\sqrt{38} \cdot \sqrt{26}} = \frac{26}{\sqrt{988}} = \frac{26}{31.4325} \approx 0.8272$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation [1 mark]:**
A cosine similarity of $\approx 0.827$ indicates that the two vectors are **highly aligned** in direction. The corresponding angle is $\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.8272) \approx 34.2^{\circ}$, which is a small angle — confirming strong directional similarity.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q(b).** With a neat diagram, explain the feature engineering pipeline. Why is **normalization** of features critical before applying distance-based algorithms? Demonstrate with a numerical example.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Feature Engineering Pipeline [2 marks]:**

```
Raw Data → Cleaning → Encoding → Scaling → Selection → Feature Vector → ML Model
```

- **Cleaning:** Handle missing values, remove duplicates.
- **Encoding:** Convert categorical to numerical (One-Hot, Label).
- **Scaling:** Normalize/standardize numerical features.
- **Selection:** Remove irrelevant features.

**Step 2 — Why Normalization is Critical [2 marks]:**
Distance-based algorithms (k-NN, k-Means, SVM with RBF kernel) compute distances using feature values directly. If one feature has a much larger scale (e.g., income in thousands vs. age in years), it will **dominate** the distance calculation, making the model biased.

**Step 3 — Numerical Demonstration [3 marks]:**

Consider two samples with features $(x_1 = \text{Age}, x_2 = \text{Income in ₹})$:

$$\mathbf{a} = [30, \; 50000]^{\top}, \quad \mathbf{b} = [35, \; 55000]^{\top}$$

**Without normalization — Euclidean distance:**

$$d(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sqrt{(30-35)^2 + (50000-55000)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 25{,}000{,}000} \approx 5000.0025$$

The age difference of 5 years contributes only $0.0001\%$ to the total distance — effectively **invisible** to the algorithm.

**With Min-Max normalization** (Age: range $[0, 100]$, Income: range $[0, 100000]$):

$$a_1^{\prime} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30, \quad a_2^{\prime} = \frac{50000}{100000} = 0.50$$

$$b_1^{\prime} = \frac{35}{100} = 0.35, \quad b_2^{\prime} = \frac{55000}{100000} = 0.55$$

$$d(\mathbf{a}^{\prime}, \mathbf{b}^{\prime}) = \sqrt{(0.30-0.35)^2 + (0.50-0.55)^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.0025} \approx 0.0707$$

Now both features contribute **equally** (50%-50%) to the distance, as intended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (from KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing rows and columns:** A feature vector is a **row** of the feature matrix, NOT a column. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the transpose symbol:** $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ must be written as a **column vector** with the $\top$ symbol or written as a row — be consistent.
> 3. **Using $|\cdot|$ (absolute value) when you mean norm:** For Euclidean distance, write $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$, not $|\cdot|$. Norm ≠ absolute value.
> 4. **Not stating units / not specifying which norm:** Always say "L2 norm" or "Euclidean norm" explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the arithmetic in numerical problems:** Examiners award step-marks. Always show the squaring, summation, and square root explicitly.
> 6. **Writing One-Hot Encoding incorrectly:** For 3 categories, the vector length is 3, NOT 1. Many students write a single label integer instead — that is **Label Encoding**, not One-Hot.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Feature Vector:** $n$-D numerical tuple representing one sample; notation $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.
- **Feature Matrix:** $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$; rows = samples, columns = features.
- **Feature Space:** The $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ space where all vectors live; geometric foundation of ML.
- **Euclidean Distance:** $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$ — straight-line distance.
- **Manhattan Distance:** $d = \sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ — city-block distance.
- **Cosine Similarity:** $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ — direction-only, scale-invariant.
- **Min-Max Normalization:** Scales features to $[0, 1]$; sensitive to outliers.
- **Z-Score Standardization:** Mean $= 0$, Variance $= 1$; preferred for SVM, Logistic Regression, PCA.
- **Mean Vector:** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{m}\sum \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$; centroid of the dataset.
- **Why normalize?** Prevents large-scale features from dominating distance-based models.
- **Categorical Handling:** Use One-Hot Encoding (binary columns) or Label Encoding (integer codes).
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** As $n$ grows, data becomes sparse in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$; distance metrics lose discriminative power.
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** PCA, LDA, t-SNE project high-D vectors to low-D for visualization and efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Training Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training Set (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Training Set** is a labelled (or partially labelled) collection of data instances $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ drawn from an unknown joint probability distribution $\mathcal{P}(\mathbf{X}, Y)$, which is presented to a **Learning Algorithm** $\mathcal{L}$ so that it can infer a mapping function (hypothesis) $h: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing a chosen **Loss Function** $\mathcal{L}(h(\mathbf{x}), y)$.

In simple words: the **Training Set is the textbook the model studies before appearing in the exam (Test Set).**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Student Preparing for an Exam"**
> - Imagine you are a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).
> - The **syllabus, reference notes, model papers, and solved numericals** you use while studying = the **Training Set**.
> - The **actual unseen ESE question paper** = the **Test Set**.
> - The **knowledge you build in your brain** = the learned model $h_\theta(\mathbf{x})$.
> - If your study material is poor (noisy data), your answers in the exam will be wrong. If you only memorize the study material without understanding (overfitting), you will fail when a new question appears (poor generalization).

The **Training Set is the foundation stone** upon which the entire inductive learning process rests. Without a representative and high-quality training set, no amount of sophisticated algorithm can produce a useful model.

### 1.3 Why is a Training Set Required?

A learning algorithm **cannot invent knowledge out of thin air**. It requires observational evidence (data) to:
1. **Discover Patterns** — find statistical regularities between input features and output labels.
2. **Adjust Parameters** — update internal weights (e.g., $\theta$ in linear regression, $w$ in neural networks) using optimization methods like **Gradient Descent**.
3. **Generalize** — form a hypothesis that works on **unseen** data, not just memorized training examples.

### 1.4 Mathematical Notation Standard (KTU 2024 Convention)

For the rest of these notes, the training set is formally denoted as:

$$
\mathcal{D}_{train} = \big\{(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, y^{(1)}),\ (\mathbf{x}^{(2)}, y^{(2)}),\ \ldots,\ (\mathbf{x}^{(N)}, y^{(N)})\big\}
$$

where:
- $N$ → total number of training instances (also called **sample size** or **cardinality**).
- $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ → the **feature vector** (input) of the $i$-th instance, having $d$ features.
- $y^{(i)} \in \mathcal{Y}$ → the **target label** (output) of the $i$-th instance.
- $\mathcal{Y}$ is the output space (e.g., $\mathcal{Y} = \{0,1\}$ for binary classification, $\mathcal{Y} = \mathbb{R}$ for regression).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The pair $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ is called a **labelled example** or **training instance**. The set of *all* such pairs is the **Training Set**.

### 1.5 Types of Training Sets (Based on Learning Paradigm)

| Paradigm | Training Set Composition | Example Algorithm |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| **Supervised Learning** | $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ — Fully labelled | Linear Regression, SVM, CNN |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | $\{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ — No labels | K-Means, Autoencoders, PCA |
| **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Mostly unlabelled + small labelled subset | Self-training, Graph-based methods |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | State–action–reward tuples from environment | Q-Learning, DQN |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Role of the Training Set in the ML Pipeline

The training set is consumed by a **Learning Algorithm** in the following sequential manner:

1. **Initialization:** The model parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(0)}$ are initialized (e.g., randomly or using Xavier initialization).
2. **Forward Pass:** For each (or a batch of) training instance $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$, the model produces a prediction $\hat{y}^{(i)} = h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$.
3. **Loss Computation:** The error (loss) is measured between prediction and ground truth, e.g., Mean Squared Error:
$$
\mathcal{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \big)^2
$$
4. **Backward Pass (Gradient Computation):** The gradient of the loss with respect to the parameters is calculated:
$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \big) \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})
$$
5. **Parameter Update:** Using the gradient, parameters are updated (Gradient Descent rule):
$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})
$$
where $\eta$ is the **learning rate**.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 2–5 are repeated for many epochs (passes over the training set) until convergence.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why Forward Pass?** — To know what the *current* model believes.
> - **Why Loss Computation?** — To quantify how *wrong* the model is.
> - **Why Gradient?** — To know the *direction* and *magnitude* of parameter correction.
> - **Why Update?** — To *minimize* the loss on the **same** training set, instance by instance.

### 2.3 The 'How' — How the Training Set Shapes the Hypothesis

The training set $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ implicitly defines the **Empirical Risk**:

$$
\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\big( h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)} \big)
$$

The learning algorithm picks the hypothesis $h^*$ that minimizes this empirical risk:

$$
h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)
$$

This is the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) principle**, a cornerstone of statistical learning theory.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for the Topic: Training Set**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ | Standard definition of a labelled training set |
| 2 | $h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)$ | Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) |
| 3 | $\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$ | Empirical risk as the average training loss |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})$ | Gradient Descent update rule |
| 5 | $\mathcal{J}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Mean Squared Error loss (regression) |
| 6 | $\mathcal{J}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[y^{(i)}\log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\big]$ | Cross-Entropy loss (binary classification) |
| 7 | $\mathbb{E}_{(X,Y)\sim \mathcal{P}}[\ell(h(X), Y)] = R(h)$ | True risk (theoretical, over true distribution $\mathcal{P}$) |
| 8 | $\vert R(h) - \hat{R}(h) \vert \leq \mathcal{O}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{\log \vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{N}}\right)$ | Generalization bound (PAC-style, finite hypothesis) |
| 9 | $N \geq \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{d \cdot \log(1/\delta)}{\epsilon^2}\right)$ | Sample complexity lower bound (linear models) |
| 10 | $Bias^2 + Variance + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}} = \text{Generalization Error}$ | Bias–Variariance decomposition |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone** of nearly every ML exam answer in KTU. A model is only as good as the training set that shaped it — touching everything from medical diagnosis (training on X-ray images) to autonomous vehicles (training on road-driving footage).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From True Risk to Empirical Risk

The **true risk** (expected loss) over the unknown data distribution $\mathcal{P}(X, Y)$ is:

$$
R(h) = \mathbb{E}_{(X,Y) \sim \mathcal{P}}\big[\, \ell(h(X), Y) \,\big]
$$

Since the true distribution $\mathcal{P}$ is unknown, we approximate it using the **empirical distribution** formed by the training set $\mathcal{D}_{train}$. The empirical distribution places a uniform mass of $\tfrac{1}{N}$ on each training point.

**Step 1:** Replace the expectation by a sample average (Law of Large Numbers approximation):

$$
\mathbb{E}_{(X,Y)\sim \mathcal{P}}[\ell(h(X), Y)] \;\approx\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)})
$$

**Step 2:** Define the right-hand side as the **Empirical Risk**:

$$
\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\big( h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)} \big)
$$

**Step 3:** Hence the learning objective becomes:

$$
h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)
$$

**Conclusion:** The training set $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ is, mathematically, the **empirical proxy of reality**. The more samples $N$ you have, the closer $\hat{R}(h)$ gets to $R(h)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient Descent Update for Linear Regression on a Training Set

Consider a simple linear model: $h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$, with MSE loss:

$$
\mathcal{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \big)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \big) \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the gradient descent update:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big( (\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \big) \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 3:** For a *single* training instance $(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, y^{(k)})$ (Stochastic Gradient Descent):

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot 2 \big( (\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - y^{(k)} \big) \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(k)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Every training instance contributes a corrective nudge to the parameters. The training set, in essence, is **a teacher that corrects mistakes one example at a time**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Train–Validation–Test Split Logic

To make a model that generalizes, the available dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $M$ is split:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_{train} \cup \mathcal{D}_{val} \cup \mathcal{D}_{test}, \quad \mathcal{D}_{train} \cap \mathcal{D}_{val} \cap \mathcal{D}_{test} = \emptyset
$$

Typical KTU 2024 benchmark ratios:

$$
\frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{train} \vert}{M} : \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{val} \vert}{M} : \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{test} \vert}{M} \;\;=\;\; 0.70 : 0.15 : 0.15
$$

**Mathematical Justification:** If $N$ (training set size) is too small, the empirical risk $\hat{R}(h)$ is a **noisy estimator** of the true risk $R(h)$. The variance of this estimator scales as:

$$
\text{Var}\big(\hat{R}(h)\big) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

where $\sigma^2$ is the noise variance in labels. Thus, **doubling the training set size reduces estimator variance by half** — explaining why large training sets are so valuable.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Training Set Properties vs. Model Behaviour

| Property of Training Set | Effect on Model | KTU Tip |
|--------------------------|-----------------|---------|
| **Large $N$** | Lower variance, better generalization | Required for deep networks |
| **Small $N$** | High variance, overfitting risk | Use regularization, simpler models |
| **Biased sampling** | Model inherits bias (sampling bias) | Use stratified sampling |
| **Noisy labels** | Model learns incorrect patterns | Use label-smoothing / data cleaning |
| **Class imbalance** | Skewed predictions toward majority class | Use SMOTE, class weights |
| **High feature dimensionality $d$** | Curse of dimensionality | Use PCA, feature selection |
| **Independent & Identically Distributed (i.i.d.)** | Theoretical guarantees hold | Random shuffle of training data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Training Set in the ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data D] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Train-Validation-Test Split]
    C --> D[Training Set D_train]
    C --> E[Validation Set D_val]
    C --> F[Test Set D_test]
    D --> G[Learning Algorithm L]
    G --> H[Trained Model h_theta]
    H --> I[Evaluate on D_val]
    I --> J{Hyperparameters OK?}
    J -->|No| K[Tune Hyperparameters]
    K --> G
    J -->|Yes| L[Final Evaluation on D_test]
    L --> M[Deployment]
```

### 4.2 Internal Mechanics of One Training Epoch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize theta] --> B[Shuffle D_train]
    B --> C[Sample mini-batch]
    C --> D[Forward Pass: y_hat = h_theta of x]
    D --> E[Compute Loss J theta]
    E --> F[Compute Gradient of J]
    F --> G[Update theta using Gradient Descent]
    G --> H{All batches done?}
    H -->|No| C
    H -->|Yes| I[End of Epoch]
    I --> J{Converged?}
    J -->|No| B
    J -->|Yes| K[Trained Model]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Training Set Usage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Data Source]
        A1[Database / CSV / API]
    end
    subgraph PIPE[Training Pipeline]
        B1[Data Cleaning]
        B2[Feature Engineering]
        B3[Normalization / Encoding]
    end
    subgraph TSPLIT[Dataset Splitter]
        C1[Training Set 70 percent]
        C2[Validation Set 15 percent]
        C3[Test Set 15 percent]
    end
    subgraph LEARN[Learning Engine]
        D1[Model h_theta]
        D2[Loss Function J]
        D3[Optimizer SGD/Adam]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output]
        E1[Trained Model Artifact]
        E2[Performance Metrics]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
    C2 --> E2
    C3 --> E2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (for KTU exams):** When asked "Explain the role of the training set", always draw a **block diagram** showing: **Raw Data → Preprocessing → Training Set → Learning Algorithm → Trained Model**. This instantly earns you **3–5 marks** in a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> **Q1. Define a Training Set. List any two properties it should possess for effective learning.**

**Model Answer:**
A *Training Set* is a labelled collection of data instances $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ used by a learning algorithm to fit a model $h$ by minimizing a loss function.

Two essential properties:
1. **Representativeness:** The training set must be a faithful sample of the true data-generating distribution $\mathcal{P}(X, Y)$; otherwise the learned model will suffer from *sampling bias*.
2. **Sufficient Size & Quality:** It should contain enough clean, i.i.d. samples to allow the empirical risk $\hat{R}(h)$ to approximate the true risk $R(h)$ within acceptable error.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Training Set and Test Set.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Training Set $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ | Test Set $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ |
|--------|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Used to *fit* the model parameters | Used to *evaluate* the final model's generalization |
| **Usage Stage** | During model training (learning phase) | After training, *only once* |
| **Seen by Model?** | Yes — model learns from it | No — model must not see it during training |
| **Size (typical)** | 70% of dataset | 15% of dataset |
| **Role in Risk** | Minimizes $\hat{R}(h)$ | Estimates $R(h)$ on unseen data |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the role of the **Training Set** in the machine learning pipeline. Discuss how the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle uses the training set to learn a hypothesis.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A training set of size $N = 1000$ is used to train a linear regression model with $d = 5$ features. Compute the approximate variance of the empirical risk estimator given label noise variance $\sigma^2 = 4$. Also explain what happens to the variance if the training set size is doubled to $N = 2000$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Role of Training Set & ERM Principle:**

**Step 1 — Pipeline:** A training set is the labelled input to the learning algorithm. It is first **cleaned**, then **split** into training, validation, and test sets. The training set $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ is fed to a learning algorithm $\mathcal{L}$.

**Step 2 — ERM Principle:** Since the true distribution $\mathcal{P}(X,Y)$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk** computed on the training set:

$$
\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\big( h(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)} \big)
$$

**Step 3 — Optimal Hypothesis:** The algorithm finds:

$$
h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)
$$

**Step 4 — Generalization:** The hope is that the $h^*$ minimizing $\hat{R}$ will also minimize the true risk $R(h) = \mathbb{E}[\ell(h(X),Y)]$. The **generalization gap** $\vert R(h^*) - \hat{R}(h^*) \vert$ shrinks as $N$ increases.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The training set is the *empirical evidence* from which the model induces knowledge. A good training set is large, representative, and clean.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1:** Variance of empirical risk estimator:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{R}(h)) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N} = \frac{4}{1000} = 0.004
$$

**Step 2:** If $N$ is doubled to $N = 2000$:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{R}(h))_{\text{new}} = \frac{4}{2000} = 0.002
$$

**Step 3:** Ratio:

$$
\frac{0.004}{0.002} = 2
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling the training set size **halves the variance** of the empirical risk estimator, leading to a more stable and reliable estimate of the model's true performance. This mathematically justifies the industry-wide pursuit of larger training datasets.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the **role of the training set in supervised learning**. Explain what happens to the model if the training set is **(i)** too small, **(ii)** noisy, and **(iii)** non-representative.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Suppose you are given a dataset of 10,000 images to train a binary classifier. Suggest an appropriate train–validation–test split, justify it, and explain how the training set influences the choice of model complexity using the **bias–variance trade-off**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Failure Modes:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Labelled Training Set] --> B[Learning Algorithm]
    B --> C[Hypothesis h_theta]
    C --> D[Predictions on new data]
```

**Failure Mode Analysis:**

| Training Set Issue | Symptom in Model | Underlying Cause |
|--------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **(i) Too Small** | **High Variance / Overfitting** | $\hat{R}(h)$ is a noisy estimator; $h$ memorizes few examples |
| **(ii) Noisy** | **High Bias / Systematic Errors** | $\ell$ uses wrong labels; $h$ learns spurious patterns |
| **(iii) Non-Representative** | **Sampling Bias** | $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ drawn from different $\mathcal{P}$ than test data; poor generalization |

**Remedies:**
- For (i): Use **regularization** (L1/L2), **cross-validation**, or **data augmentation**.
- For (ii): Apply **label cleaning**, **noise-robust loss functions** (e.g., Huber loss).
- For (iii): Use **stratified sampling** and ensure $\mathcal{D}_{train}$ matches the deployment distribution.

**(b) Train–Validation–Test Split:**

**Step 1 — Suggested Split:**

$$
N_{train} = 7000, \quad N_{val} = 1500, \quad N_{test} = 1500
$$

i.e., a **70 : 15 : 15** ratio.

**Step 2 — Justification:**
- **70% for Training** — provides sufficient samples to fit model parameters reliably.
- **15% for Validation** — used for hyperparameter tuning (e.g., learning rate, regularization strength $\lambda$).
- **15% for Test** — held out *completely* until the end to give an unbiased estimate of generalization.

**Step 3 — Bias–Variance Trade-off:**

$$
\text{Expected Test Error} = \text{Bias}^2(h) + \text{Variance}(h) + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}
$$

- **Small Training Set + Complex Model** → High variance (overfits).
- **Large Training Set + Simple Model** → High bias (underfits).
- **Sweet Spot:** Choose model complexity such that both bias and variance are minimized — typically achievable when the training set is large enough to support the model's capacity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
> 1. **Confusing Training Set with Test Set** — A shocking number of students evaluate their model on the *same* set used for training, then report 99% accuracy. KTU examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 2. **Forgetting to Mention Empirical Risk** — When asked "How does the training set train the model?", you MUST write the ERM equation. Marks are reserved for this.
> 3. **No Block Diagram** — A 14-mark question on training sets WITHOUT a diagram loses **2–3 marks** automatically in the KTU 2024 scheme.
> 4. **Ignoring the Bias–Variance Trade-off** — Linking training set size to overfitting/underfitting is a **mandatory** component of any complete answer.
> 5. **Not Justifying the Split Ratio** — Simply writing "70-15-15" without explaining *why* is incomplete. Always state that training must be larger because it is used to fit parameters.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Training Set"**

- ✅ A **Training Set** is a labelled dataset $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ used to fit model parameters.
- ✅ It is the **empirical proxy** of the true data distribution; learning is based on **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)**.
- ✅ Standard split: **70% train / 15% validation / 15% test** (can vary by domain).
- ✅ **Larger $N$** → lower variance of empirical risk estimator ($\text{Var} = \sigma^2 / N$).
- ✅ Training set quality matters as much as quantity: it must be **clean, balanced, and representative** (i.i.d. assumption).
- ✅ A good training set enables **generalization**; a poor one leads to **overfitting** (small/noisy) or **underfitting** (sparse features).
- ✅ The optimal hypothesis is: $h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)$.
- ✅ Gradient Descent is the standard mechanism that uses the training set to iteratively update parameters: $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
- ✅ Always draw a **block diagram** in KTU 14-mark answers on training sets.
- ✅ The training set must be **disjoint** from the test set — a cardinal rule of ML evaluation.
- ✅ The role of the training set is inseparable from the **Bias–Variance trade-off**.

> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024:** *"The model is only as good as the training set that shaped it — choose your data wisely, and the algorithm will do the rest."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Vector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Target Vector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Supervised Machine Learning**, a **Target Vector** (also referred to as the *output vector*, *label vector*, *ground-truth vector*, or *response vector*) is a mathematically structured column vector that contains the **desired** or **true output values** corresponding to each input sample in a training dataset. It is the vector against which the predictions generated by a learning model are compared during training.

Formally, for a training dataset containing $m$ samples, the target vector is denoted as:

$$\mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} t_1 \\ t_2 \\ \vdots \\ t_m \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1} \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbb{R}^{m \times K}$$

where $K$ is the number of output classes (in classification) and $m$ is the number of observations. The corresponding **prediction vector** produced by the model is denoted as $\mathbf{y}$ or $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The target vector is the *single most important* object in the loss function computation. Without the target vector, the model has no notion of "correctness" — it cannot learn. Every loss formulation in Module 1 (MSE, Cross-Entropy, 0-1 Loss) is built around comparing $\mathbf{y}$ (prediction) with $\mathbf{t}$ (target).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a school teacher who has prepared an **answer key** for an examination. The answer key is the "target" — it contains the *correct* responses to every question. The students write their own answers (the *predictions*), and the teacher grades them by comparing each student's response with the corresponding entry in the answer key.

| Role | Real World (Exam) | Machine Learning |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| Question Paper | Input feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ | Input sample |
| Student's Answer | Prediction $\mathbf{y}$ | Model output |
| **Answer Key** | **Target Vector $\mathbf{t}$** | **Ground truth labels** |
| Marks Deducted | Error / Loss $L(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{t})$ | Cost function value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A target vector is simply the **column of correct answers** that the machine learning model is trying to learn to predict. It is the "yardstick" used to measure how wrong the model's predictions are.

### 1.3 Dimensional Specifications and Standard Notation

- **Regression Problems:** $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ — a single real-valued target per sample.
- **Binary Classification:** $\mathbf{t} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times 1}$ — each entry is a class label.
- **Multi-Class Classification (One-Hot):** $\mathbf{t} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times K}$ — a binary indicator matrix.
- **Multi-Label Classification:** $\mathbf{t} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times K}$ — multiple 1s allowed per row.

The standard **loss per sample** is a scalar function:

$$L_i = \mathcal{L}(y_i, t_i)$$

and the **aggregate dataset loss** uses the target vector in its entirety:

$$J = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(y_i, t_i)$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Target Vector

The target vector plays **three distinct operational roles** in a supervised learning pipeline. Understanding these is critical for KTU module-level questions.

1. **Reference Anchor for Loss Computation**
   - The loss function $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{t})$ quantifies the discrepancy between the prediction vector $\mathbf{y}$ and the target vector $\mathbf{t}$.
   - Gradient-based optimizers (SGD, Adam) compute $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$, which inherently depends on $\mathbf{t}$.

2. **Source of Supervisory Signal**
   - The model updates its parameters $\theta$ using the rule: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{t})$.
   - Without $\mathbf{t}$, the gradient is undefined — hence $\mathbf{t}$ *is* the "supervision".

3. **Basis for Evaluation Metrics**
   - Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and AUC all compare $\mathbf{y}$ (argmax) with $\mathbf{t}$.

### 2.2 Why the Target Vector? (The Core "Why")

- **The "How":** During forward propagation, the model produces $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x}; \theta)$. The loss is then computed by an element-wise comparison of $\mathbf{y}$ with $\mathbf{t}$. Backpropagation propagates this error backward to update weights.
- **The "Why":** Machine learning models are *function approximators*. To approximate the unknown function $f^*: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, the model needs sample-wise feedback — and that feedback is exactly the target vector $\mathbf{t}$.

### 2.3 Encoding Schemes for Categorical Targets

For categorical labels, the target vector must be numerically encoded. KTU commonly tests two encodings:

- **Label Encoding:** $t_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, K-1\}$
- **One-Hot Encoding:** $t_i = \mathbf{e}_j$ where $\mathbf{e}_j$ is the $j$-th standard basis vector in $\mathbb{R}^K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why One-Hot?** Using integer labels (e.g., "Class 2" = 2) implies an *ordinal* relationship (Class 2 is "more" than Class 1) which is **false** for nominal classes. One-hot encoding removes this artificial ordering.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use Case | Vector Form |
|---|---------|---------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Target Vector Definition | $\mathbf{t} = [t_1, \dots, t_m]^{\top}$ | General | Column vector of size $m$ |
| 2 | One-Hot Encoding | $t_i = \mathbf{e}_j \in \{0,1\}^{K}$ | Multi-class | $\sum_j t_{ij} = 1$ |
| 3 | Half-MSE Loss (vector) | $E = \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t} \rVert^{2}$ | Regression | $\frac{1}{2}\sum_i (y_i - t_i)^{2}$ |
| 4 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i (y_i - t_i)^{2}$ | Regression | $\frac{1}{m}\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t} \rVert^{2}$ |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy (Binary) | $\mathcal{L} = -[t \log y + (1-t)\log(1-y)]$ | Binary Class. | Element-wise |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy (Multi-class) | $\mathcal{L}_i = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} t_{ik} \log y_{ik}$ | Multi-class | $-\mathbf{t}^{\top} \log \mathbf{y}$ |
| 7 | L2 Norm Distance | $d = \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t} \rVert_2$ | General | Euclidean distance |
| 8 | Gradient of MSE | $\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} E = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$ | Backprop | Vector difference |
| 9 | Prediction Operator | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_k y_{ik}$ | Inference | Compare with $t_i$ |
| 10 | Hamming Loss (multi-label) | $\mathcal{L}_H = \frac{1}{mK}\sum_i \sum_k \vert t_{ik} - y_{ik} \vert$ | Multi-label | Element-wise mismatch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Notice in row 8, the gradient of the squared error w.r.t. the prediction vector is simply $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$. This elegant vector form appears in nearly every backpropagation derivation in Module 2.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** $\mathbf{t}$ = vector of disease presence (1) or absence (0) for each patient.
- **Autonomous Driving:** $\mathbf{t}$ = steering angle, throttle, brake values per frame.
- **Spam Detection:** $\mathbf{t}$ = binary label vector (1 = spam, 0 = not spam) for an email corpus.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** $\mathbf{t}$ = remaining useful life (RUL) of industrial machinery.
- **Recommender Systems:** $\mathbf{t}$ = user-item interaction matrix (rating or click indicator).

In every one of these applications, the **target vector is the labeled historical data** that the model is trained against.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Scope:** The derivations below focus on (a) constructing the target vector for various problem types, (b) deriving the gradient of common loss functions w.r.t. the prediction, and (c) the matrix form of the dataset loss. These are the most frequently tested derivations for "Target Vector" in KTU ESE.

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of Half-MSE w.r.t. Prediction Vector

**Starting Point:** The Half-Mean-Squared-Error loss for a single sample is:

$$E_i = \frac{1}{2}(y_i - t_i)^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Express as a Sum over Vector Components:**
The dataset loss is the sum over all $m$ samples:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y_i - t_i)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. a Single Component $y_j$:**
Apply the chain rule. Note that only the $i = j$ term survives:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_j} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2(y_j - t_j) \cdot 1 = (y_j - t_j)$$

**Step 3 — Collect into a Vector (Component-wise Generalization):**
Stacking the partial derivatives for $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$ into a column vector:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} E = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_1} \\[4pt] \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_2} \\[4pt] \vdots \\[4pt] \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_m} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - t_1 \\ y_2 - t_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_m - t_m \end{bmatrix} = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$$

**Step 4 — Result (Final Compact Form):**

$$\boxed{\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} E = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The gradient direction is simply "the prediction minus the target." This means the model is pushed in the direction that reduces $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$, i.e., it moves predictions *toward* targets. This is the foundation of gradient descent for regression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: One-Hot Encoding Transformation (Multi-Class)

**Problem Setup:** Given a raw label vector of integer class indices:

$$\mathbf{c} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \\ \vdots \\ c_m \end{bmatrix}, \quad c_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, K-1\}$$

Convert it into a one-hot encoded target matrix $\mathbf{T} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times K}$.

**Step 1 — Define the Standard Basis Vector:**
The $j$-th standard basis vector in $\mathbb{R}^K$ is:

$$\mathbf{e}_j = [\underbrace{0, \dots, 0}_{j-1}, 1, \underbrace{0, \dots, 0}_{K-j}]^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$$

**Step 2 — Row-wise Construction:**
For each sample $i$, set the $i$-th row of $\mathbf{T}$:

$$\mathbf{T}_{i,:} = \mathbf{e}_{c_i}^{\top}$$

**Step 3 — Compact Matrix Form:**

$$\mathbf{T}_{ij} = \delta(j - c_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j = c_i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Kronecker delta. Therefore:

$$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{e}_{c_1}^{\top} \\ \mathbf{e}_{c_2}^{\top} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{e}_{c_m}^{\top} \end{bmatrix} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times K}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity Check (Row-Sum Property):**
Each row of $\mathbf{T}$ has exactly one 1:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{K} \mathbf{T}_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, m\}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss in Vector Form

**Single-Sample Multi-Class Cross-Entropy:**

$$\mathcal{L}_i = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} t_{ik} \log y_{ik}$$

**Step 1 — Dataset-Level Summation:**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{k=1}^{K} t_{ik} \log y_{ik}$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite as a Trace / Frobenius Inner Product:**
Using the property that $\sum_{ij} A_{ij} B_{ij} = \text{tr}(\mathbf{A}^{\top} \mathbf{B})$:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{k=1}^{K} t_{ik} \log y_{ik} = -\text{tr}(\mathbf{T}^{\top} \log \mathbf{Y})$$

**Step 3 — Final Vector-Matrix Form:**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{Y}) = -\text{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{T}^{\top} \log \mathbf{Y}\right)}$$

where $\mathbf{T} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times K}$ is the one-hot target matrix and $\mathbf{Y} \in (0, 1)^{m \times K}$ is the predicted probability matrix (rows sum to 1 via softmax).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Loss Formulations

| Loss Function | Formula (vector) | Target Type | Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{y}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Half-MSE | $\frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t} \rVert^{2}$ | Continuous | $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$ |
| MSE | $\frac{1}{m}\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t} \rVert^{2}$ | Continuous | $\frac{2}{m}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$ |
| MAE | $\frac{1}{m}\sum_i \vert y_i - t_i \vert$ | Continuous | $\frac{1}{m}\text{sign}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$ |
| Binary CE | $-[\mathbf{t}^{\top}\log \mathbf{y} + (1-\mathbf{t})^{\top}\log(1-\mathbf{y})]$ | Binary $\{0,1\}$ | $\frac{\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t}}{\mathbf{y}(1-\mathbf{y})}$ |
| Multi-class CE | $-\text{tr}(\mathbf{T}^{\top}\log \mathbf{Y})$ | One-hot $\{0,1\}^{K}$ | $\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{T}$ (with softmax) |
| 0-1 Loss | $\frac{1}{m}\sum_i \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_i \neq t_i]$ | Categorical | Not differentiable |
| Huber | $\frac{1}{m}\sum_i L_{\delta}(y_i, t_i)$ | Continuous (robust) | Piecewise linear/quadratic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the Matrix:** Whenever the loss is a *squared* or *cross-entropy* error, the gradient reduces to a clean $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$ form. This is why these losses are preferred in deep learning — they yield well-behaved gradient flow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Role of the Target Vector in the Supervised Learning Pipeline

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates the position of the target vector $\mathbf{t}$ within the broader supervised learning workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DS[Dataset D] --> X[Input Matrix X]
    DS --> T[Target Vector t]
    X --> M[Model f parameterized by theta]
    M --> Y[Prediction Vector y]
    T --> LF[Loss Function L]
    Y --> LF
    LF --> G[Gradient Calculation]
    G --> UP[Parameter Update via Optimizer]
    UP --> M
    LF --> EV[Evaluation Metrics]
    Y --> EV
    T --> EV
    EV --> ACC[Accuracy MSE F1 Score]
```

### 4.2 Encoding and Dimensional Flow of the Target Vector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RL[Raw Labels c in 0 to K minus 1] --> OH[One Hot Encoder]
    OH --> TM[Target Matrix T in 0 1 to the power m times K]
    TM --> LF1[Cross Entropy Loss]
    PY[Predicted Probabilities Y] --> LF1
    LF1 --> GR[Gradient Y minus T]
    PY --> EV2[Argmax per row]
    TM --> EV2
    EV2 --> CL[Predicted Class Labels]
```

### 4.3 Loss Surface Visualization in Terms of Target Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Parameter theta] --> FW[Forward Pass]
    FW --> PV[Prediction y]
    TV[Target t] --> DIF[Compute y minus t]
    PV --> DIF
    DIF --> LS[Loss Surface J of theta]
    LS --> BD[Backpropagation]
    BD --> PU[Update theta minus eta times gradient J]
    PU --> P
```

### 4.4 Annotation Summary (Functional Architecture)

- **$\mathbf{X}$** is the input feature matrix (size $m \times n$).
- **$\mathbf{t}$** is the target vector (or matrix, for multi-class) — the **only source of supervision**.
- **$\mathbf{y}$** is the model prediction — what the model *thinks* the answer is.
- **$L(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{t})$** is the loss — measures how *wrong* the model is.
- **Gradient** flows backward through the model to update parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway:** Notice the target vector $\mathbf{t}$ appears in *two* downstream blocks: the **loss computation** and the **evaluation metrics**. It is the central reference object of the entire supervised learning process.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define a *target vector* in supervised machine learning. State its dimensions for (i) a regression problem with 100 samples and (ii) a 5-class classification problem with 100 samples.

**Model Answer:**
A target vector $\mathbf{t}$ is a column vector containing the true/desired output values for each input sample in a supervised learning dataset. It serves as the ground truth against which the model's predictions are compared during training and evaluation.

- (i) Regression with $m = 100$ samples: $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{100 \times 1}$.
- (ii) 5-class classification with $m = 100$ samples (one-hot encoded): $\mathbf{T} \in \{0, 1\}^{100 \times 5}$.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between *label encoding* and *one-hot encoding* of a target vector. In which scenario is one-hot encoding strictly necessary?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Label Encoding | One-Hot Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Single integer per sample | K-dimensional binary vector |
| Output type | $t_i \in \{0, \dots, K-1\}$ | $t_i \in \{0, 1\}^{K}$ |
| Implied order | Yes (ordinal) | No (nominal) |
| Distance metric | Meaningful (1 vs 2) | Hamming distance only |

**One-hot encoding is strictly necessary** for nominal categorical targets in multi-class classification (e.g., classifying "Cat", "Dog", "Bird") where there is no inherent ordering between classes. Using label encoding here would falsely imply "Dog is twice Cat", corrupting the loss computation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q-A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the Half-Mean-Squared-Error loss $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y_i - t_i)^{2}$ with respect to the prediction vector $\mathbf{y}$, and show that the result is $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$. State the parameter update rule for gradient descent using this gradient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dataset has 4 samples belonging to 3 classes with raw integer labels $\mathbf{c} = [2, 0, 1, 2]^{\top}$. Construct the one-hot encoded target matrix $\mathbf{T}$ and verify the row-sum property. Using this target matrix and a hypothetical prediction probability matrix $\mathbf{Y}$, compute the cross-entropy loss and its gradient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1.** Write the loss:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y_i - t_i)^{2}$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate w.r.t. a single component $y_j$. Only the $i = j$ term is non-zero:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_j} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2(y_j - t_j) = (y_j - t_j)$$

**Step 3.** Stack all partials into a column vector:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} E = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - t_1 \\ \vdots \\ y_m - t_m \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 4.** Gradient descent parameter update rule (with learning rate $\eta$):

$$\theta^{(\tau+1)} = \theta^{(\tau)} - \eta (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$$

where $\tau$ is the iteration index. The model is iteratively pushed in the direction of *reducing* $(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1.** Identify the classes: $K = 3$ (classes 0, 1, 2). For each $c_i$, set the corresponding position to 1:

$$T_{ij} = 1 \iff j = c_i$$

**Step 2.** Construct the target matrix:

$$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in \{0, 1\}^{4 \times 3}$$

**Step 3.** Verify row sums (each row must have exactly one 1):

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} T_{1j} = 1, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{3} T_{2j} = 1, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{3} T_{3j} = 1, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{3} T_{4j} = 1 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4.** Hypothetical prediction matrix (rows sum to 1 — softmax output):

$$\mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.7 \\ 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5.** Cross-entropy loss using $\mathcal{L} = -\text{tr}(\mathbf{T}^{\top} \log \mathbf{Y})$:

$$\mathcal{L} = -[1\cdot\log 0.7 + 1\cdot\log 0.6 + 1\cdot\log 0.5 + 1\cdot\log 0.5]$$

Using natural logarithms:

$$\mathcal{L} = -[\log 0.7 + \log 0.6 + \log 0.5 + \log 0.5] = -[-0.357 - 0.511 - 0.693 - 0.693]$$

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L} \approx 2.254 \text{ nats}}$$

**Step 6.** Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{Y}$ (with softmax activation):

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{Y}}\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 & -0.3 \\ -0.4 & 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & -0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q-B (Alternative Choice)]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** For a binary classification problem with target vector $\mathbf{t} = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0]^{\top}$ and predicted probabilities $\mathbf{y} = [0.9, 0.2, 0.7, 0.6, 0.1]^{\top}$, compute:
  - (i) the binary cross-entropy loss,
  - (ii) the classification accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a real-world example why the target vector is the *only source of supervision* in a supervised learning algorithm. What happens to gradient-based learning if $\mathbf{t}$ is replaced by random noise? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1.** Binary cross-entropy per sample:

$$\mathcal{L}_i = -[t_i \log y_i + (1 - t_i)\log(1 - y_i)]$$

**Step 2.** Compute for each of the 5 samples:

- $i=1$: $t_1 = 1, y_1 = 0.9$: $\mathcal{L}_1 = -[\log 0.9 + 0] = -(-0.105) = 0.105$
- $i=2$: $t_2 = 0, y_2 = 0.2$: $\mathcal{L}_2 = -[0 + \log 0.8] = -(-0.223) = 0.223$
- $i=3$: $t_3 = 1, y_3 = 0.7$: $\mathcal{L}_3 = -\log 0.7 = 0.357$
- $i=4$: $t_4 = 1, y_4 = 0.6$: $\mathcal{L}_4 = -\log 0.6 = 0.511$
- $i=5$: $t_5 = 0, y_5 = 0.1$: $\mathcal{L}_5 = -\log 0.9 = 0.105$

**Step 3.** Average loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{5}(0.105 + 0.223 + 0.357 + 0.511 + 0.105) = \frac{1.301}{5} \approx \boxed{0.260 \text{ nats}}$$

**Step 4.** Compute predicted classes (threshold = 0.5):

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0]^{\top}$$

**Step 5.** Compare with target $\mathbf{t} = [1, 0, 1, 1, 0]^{\top}$:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{5}{5} = \boxed{1.00 = 100\%}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1.** The target vector $\mathbf{t}$ is the *only source of supervision* in supervised learning because:
- The model $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta)$ is a pure function approximator with no built-in notion of "correctness."
- The loss function $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{t})$ requires $\mathbf{t}$ to compute any error signal.
- The gradient $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$ — which drives all learning — is mathematically undefined without $\mathbf{t}$.

**Step 2.** Real-world example: In **medical image classification for tumor detection**, the target vector $\mathbf{t}$ contains the radiologist's verified diagnoses (1 = malignant, 0 = benign) for thousands of historical scans. The CNN has no other way to "know" what a tumor looks like — it learns by repeatedly comparing its predictions with $\mathbf{t}$.

**Step 3.** Effect of replacing $\mathbf{t}$ with random noise:
- The loss becomes a **noisy, non-converging signal** with no relation to the true input-output mapping.
- Gradients computed from $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{t}_{\text{noise}})$ will push the model in *random directions*.
- The model will fail to learn any meaningful function; test accuracy will hover around the random-chance baseline (e.g., 50% for binary classification).
- This is precisely why **label noise** is a major research problem in ML — even partial corruption of $\mathbf{t}$ degrades performance significantly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Frequently Lost Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing target vector with input vector:** Many students write "the target vector is the input data" — losing 1–2 marks. The target vector is the **output/label**, not the input.
> 2. **Missing the row-sum property:** When constructing one-hot target matrices, students often forget to verify $\sum_j T_{ij} = 1$. Examiners *specifically* check this — it is a 1-mark easy win.
> 3. **Forgetting the column-vector convention:** $\mathbf{t}$ should always be written as a **column** vector $[t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m]^{\top}$. Writing it as a row vector is mathematically acceptable but loses the "standard notation" mark.
> 4. **Cross-entropy vs. MSE confusion:** Cross-entropy is used with one-hot/categorical targets; MSE with continuous targets. Mixing them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the "interpretation" in derivation questions:** Even when the math is correct, students who omit the *meaning* of $\nabla_{\mathbf{y}} E = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t})$ (i.e., "model is pushed toward the target") lose the "explanation" component of marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- ✅ **Target vector** $\mathbf{t}$ = column vector of *true* outputs / ground-truth labels.
- ✅ Dimensions: $\mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ (regression / binary) or $\{0, 1\}^{m \times K}$ (multi-class, one-hot).
- ✅ **One-hot encoding** uses standard basis vectors $\mathbf{e}_j$; row sum equals 1.
- ✅ **MSE gradient w.r.t. prediction** is simply $\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{t}$.
- ✅ **Cross-entropy** uses $\mathcal{L} = -\text{tr}(\mathbf{T}^{\top}\log \mathbf{Y})$ in matrix form.
- ✅ **Label encoding** for ordinal targets; **one-hot encoding** for nominal targets.
- ✅ Target vector is the **only source of supervision** — loss and gradient depend on it.
- ✅ **Evaluation** (accuracy, F1) compares $\arg\max \mathbf{y}$ with $\mathbf{t}$.
- ✅ Standard notation: $\mathbf{t}$ for target, $\mathbf{y}$ or $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ for prediction, $\mathbf{x}$ for input.
- ✅ **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Define target vector. Construct the one-hot target matrix for the given labels and compute the cross-entropy loss." (Memorize the row-sum verification step.)
- ✅ **Connection to Module 2:** Target vector appears in the bias-variance decomposition and in confusion matrix computation.
- ✅ **Engineering utility:** Every applied ML system (medical, automotive, financial, NLP) hinges on a clean, accurate target vector — garbage in, garbage out.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
