# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Feature Extraction. How is it different from Feature Selection?**

**Model Answer:**
Feature Extraction is the process of transforming raw input data into a reduced set of new, informative features by applying linear or non-linear transformations, with the goal of preserving the most relevant information while reducing dimensionality.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Feature Selection |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Creates **new** features via transformation | Selects a **subset** of existing features |
| Output Space | New feature space $\mathbb{R}^k$ | Same feature space, fewer dimensions |
| Examples | PCA, LDA, Autoencoders | Chi-square test, Recursive Feature Elimination |
| Interpretability | New features may lose original meaning | Retains interpretability of original features |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Feature Selection is a type of Feature Extraction" — this is **incorrect**. They are distinct concepts.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four feature extraction techniques used in image processing.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform):** Detects and describes local features in images, invariant to scale and rotation.
2. **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients):** Captures edge orientation distributions, widely used in pedestrian detection.
3. **LBP (Local Binary Patterns):** Encodes local texture information by thresholding neighbor pixels.
4. **CNN Feature Maps (Deep Features):** Hierarchical features automatically learned via convolutional layers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 contains internal choice. Both options below are valid 14-mark questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain Principal Component Analysis (PCA) as a feature extraction technique. Derive the mathematical formulation for finding the principal components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

PCA is an **unsupervised linear feature extraction method** that projects high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional subspace defined by the directions of maximum variance.

**Derivation:**

Consider centered data $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ with covariance matrix:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^T$$

We seek a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ ($\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$) that maximizes the variance of projections $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i$:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$$

**Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

Setting derivative to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \implies \mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$$

Thus, the optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the eigenvector of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ with the largest eigenvalue $\lambda$, and the captured variance equals $\lambda$. The first $k$ principal components are the top-$k$ eigenvectors.

**(b)** Given the data points: $(2,1), (3,2), (4,1), (5,3), (6,2)$. Perform PCA to extract the first principal component. Compute the projection of the first data point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left( \frac{2+3+4+5+6}{5}, \frac{1+2+1+3+2}{5} \right) = (4, 1.8)$$

**Step 2 — Centered Data** (subtract mean):

$$(-2, -0.8), (-1, 0.2), (0, -0.8), (1, 1.2), (2, 0.2)$$

**Step 3 — Covariance Matrix** (using $\frac{1}{N-1} = \frac{1}{4}$):

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} (-2)^2+(-1)^2+0+1^2+2^2 & (-2)(-0.8)+(-1)(0.2)+0+(1)(1.2)+(2)(0.2) \\ \text{Symmetric} & (-0.8)^2+(0.2)^2+(-0.8)^2+(1.2)^2+(0.2)^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 1.6+(-0.2)+0+1.2+0.4 \\ 3.0 & 0.64+0.04+0.64+1.44+0.04 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 0.75 \\ 0.75 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues:** Solve $\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$(2.5 - \lambda)(0.7 - \lambda) - 0.5625 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 - 3.2\lambda + 1.75 - 0.5625 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 - 3.2\lambda + 1.1875 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \frac{3.2 \pm \sqrt{10.24 - 4.75}}{2} = \frac{3.2 \pm \sqrt{5.49}}{2} = \frac{3.2 \pm 2.343}{2}$$

So $\lambda_1 \approx 2.77$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 0.43$.

**Step 5 — First Eigenvector** (for $\lambda_1 = 2.77$):

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_1 = \lambda_1 \mathbf{w}_1 \implies (2.5 - 2.77)w_{11} + 0.75 w_{12} = 0$$
$$-0.27 w_{11} + 0.75 w_{12} = 0 \implies w_{11} = \frac{0.75}{0.27} w_{12} \approx 2.78 w_{12}$$

Normalize: $w_{11}^2 + w_{12}^2 = 1 \Rightarrow (2.78)^2 w_{12}^2 + w_{12}^2 = 1 \Rightarrow w_{12} \approx 0.339$, $w_{11} \approx 0.941$.

So $\mathbf{w}_1 \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.941 \\ 0.339 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 6 — Project First Point** $(2,1)$:

$$z_1 = \mathbf{w}_1^T (\mathbf{x}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = (0.941)(-2) + (0.339)(-0.8) = -1.882 - 0.271 = -2.153$$

**Final Answer:** The first principal component direction is $\mathbf{w}_1 \approx (0.941, 0.339)^T$ and the extracted feature for the first point is $z_1 \approx -2.15$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Discuss Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) as a feature extraction technique. Explain the between-class and within-class scatter matrices with their mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

LDA is a **supervised linear feature extraction method** used primarily for classification. Unlike PCA (which maximizes variance), LDA finds projection directions that **maximize class separability**.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Given $C$ classes with mean vectors $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ and overall mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

**1. Within-Class Scatter Matrix** $\mathbf{S}_W$ — measures the spread *within* each class:

$$\mathbf{S}_W = \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in c} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c)^T$$

**2. Between-Class Scatter Matrix** $\mathbf{S}_B$ — measures the spread *between* class means:

$$\mathbf{S}_B = \sum_{c=1}^{C} N_c (\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_c - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$$

**3. Objective (Fisher's Criterion):** Find projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$ that maximizes:

$$J(\mathbf{W}) = \frac{\vert \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{W} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{W} \vert}$$

**Solution:** Set derivative to zero, yielding the **Generalized Eigenvalue Problem**:

$$\mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w} \implies \mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

The eigenvectors corresponding to the largest eigenvalues form $\mathbf{W}$. The maximum number of discriminative components is $C-1$.

**(b)** Compare and contrast PCA and LDA as feature extraction techniques. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| **Goal** | Preserve data variance | Maximize class separation |
| **Label Required** | No | Yes (class labels) |
| **Math Basis** | Eigenvectors of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ | Eigenvectors of $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B$ |
| **Max Dimensions** | $D$ (data dims) | $C-1$ (class count) |
| **Assumptions** | None | Gaussian distribution, equal covariance |
| **Use Case** | Compression, visualization | Classification preprocessing |

**When to Prefer PCA:**
- When labels are unavailable (unsupervised setting).
- For data compression and noise reduction.
- When preserving total variance is critical.
- As a preprocessing step for visualization (e.g., 2D plotting).

**When to Prefer LDA:**
- For multi-class classification tasks.
- When class labels are available.
- When class separation matters more than variance preservation.
- Limited to $C-1$ components — useful when $C$ is small but $D$ is large.

**Hybrid Approach:** In practice, a common pipeline is `PCA → LDA` to first remove noise (PCA) and then extract discriminative features (LDA).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Feature Extraction Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PCA with LDA:** Mark deduction up to **3 marks** if the question explicitly asks for supervised/unsupervised and the student mixes them up.
> 2. **Skipping the centering step:** Always state $\sum \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}$ before deriving PCA — omitting this loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the constraint $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$:** The unit norm constraint is essential to the Lagrangian derivation. Missing it = **2 marks lost**.
> 4. **Not computing eigenvalues correctly:** Numerical mistakes in quadratic equations cost 2-3 marks. Always double-check the discriminant.
> 5. **Missing reconstruction equation:** For 14-mark questions, include both $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{x}$ (forward) and $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{z} + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ (reconstruction).
> 6. **Failure to choose $k$:** Always justify the choice of $k$ using the **explained variance ratio threshold** (typically 0.95).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feature Extraction**

- **Definition:** Transformation of raw data $D$-dim → reduced $k$-dim informative features.
- **Primary Goal:** Combat *Curse of Dimensionality*, reduce noise, improve model performance.
- **PCA Core Equation:** $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ — find eigenvectors of covariance matrix.
- **LDA Core Equation:** $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1} \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ — Fisher's criterion.
- **Variance Retained Formula:** $\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j \big/ \sum_{j=1}^{D} \lambda_j \geq 0.95$.
- **Max LDA Components:** $C-1$ where $C$ = number of classes.
- **Preprocessing Step:** Always **standardize** data before applying PCA/LDA.
- **t-SNE:** Used for 2D/3D visualization only — *not* for general feature extraction due to non-convex optimization.
- **Autoencoders:** Neural-network-based non-linear feature extraction; bottleneck layer size = $k$.
- **Image Features:** SIFT (keypoints), HOG (edges), CNN (hierarchical deep features).
- **Text Features:** TF-IDF (statistical), Word2Vec/GloVe (semantic), BERT (contextual).
- **Audio Features:** MFCC (perceptually-motivated cepstral coefficients).
- **Difference Matrix:** PCA = unsupervised + variance; LDA = supervised + class separation; ICA = independence.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do **not** confuse Feature Extraction with Feature Selection.
- **Practical Tip:** When $D$ is very large (e.g., images), prefer **Kernel PCA** or **Autoencoders** over linear PCA.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Over-Fitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Over-Fitting ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Over-Fitting (Overfitting):** A pathological modelling phenomenon in supervised Machine Learning wherein a learning algorithm generates a hypothesis $h \in \mathcal{H}$ that achieves an exceptionally **low training error** $\hat{\epsilon}(h)$ but exhibits a **significantly higher generalization (test) error** $\epsilon(h)$ on unseen data drawn from the same underlying distribution $\mathcal{D}$. Mathematically, over-fitting is said to occur when: $$\hat{\epsilon}_{\text{train}}(h) \ll \hat{\epsilon}_{\text{test}}(h)$$ with the gap: $$\Delta = \hat{\epsilon}_{\text{test}}(h) - \hat{\epsilon}_{\text{train}}(h) \to \text{large positive value}$$ The model has effectively **memorized** the finite training set $\mathcal{T} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, including its noise $\xi$ and outliers, instead of learning the **true underlying functional mapping** $f^*: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ such that $y = f^*(\mathbf{x}) + \xi$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Rote-Memorizing Student"** > > Imagine a student preparing for an examination using **only the previous year's question paper**. The student memorizes the exact answers, word-for-word, without understanding the underlying concepts or methods. > > - On the **training paper** (questions already seen): the student scores **100/100** ✅ > - On the **actual exam** (a new paper with different numbers but the same concepts): the student scores **20/100** ❌ > > This student has **over-fit** to the training set. A better student would learn the **principles** and **derive** the answers for any new paper — this is a **well-generalized** model. > > Similarly, in ML, a model that draws a wiggly curve passing through **every single training point** (capturing noise) will fail miserably on new data, while a smoother curve that captures the **trend** will generalize well. ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Indicators of Over-Fitting - **Training Accuracy:** > **95%** (often near 100%) - **Validation/Test Accuracy:** Drops to **60–75%** or lower - **Loss Gap:** $\vert \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}} - \mathcal{L}_{\text{train}} \vert$ widens progressively across epochs - **Model Complexity:** Effective parameters $\vert \theta \vert \gg$ intrinsic data complexity ### 1.4 Root Causes of Over-Fitting (KTU Board-Favorite Listing) 1. **Insufficient Training Data** — sample size $N$ is too small relative to hypothesis space $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ 2. **Excessive Model Complexity** — high-degree polynomials, deep unregularized networks, large decision trees 3. **Noisy / Corrupted Labels** — model fits annotation errors 4. **Excessive Training Duration** — training well past the optimal stopping point 5. **Irrelevant Feature Inclusion** — model latches onto spurious correlations 6. **Lack of Regularization** — no penalty term on parameter magnitudes ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Bias–Variance Decomposition (KTU High-Yield Topic) The expected prediction error of any learned model at a point $\mathbf{x}$ can be rigorously decomposed as: $$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2\left[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})\right]}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}\left[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})\right]}_{\text{sensitivity to training set}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$ #### Component-wise Interpretation - **Bias²:** Error from wrong/false assumptions in the learning algorithm. **High bias → under-fitting.** - **Variance:** Amount by which $\hat{f}$ would change if we estimated it using a different training set $\mathcal{T}'$. **High variance → over-fitting.** - **$\sigma^2$ (Irreducible Error):** Noise inherent in the data generation process; **cannot be eliminated** by any model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Over-fitting corresponds to the region where increasing model complexity further drives VARIANCE ↑↑ while only marginally reducing BIAS².** Total error therefore *increases* — this is the operational signature of over-fitting. ### 2.2 Generalization Bound (PAC-Learning Style) For a hypothesis $h$ with empirical error $\hat{\epsilon}(h)$ over $N$ i.i.d. samples, with probability $\geq 1 - \delta$: $$\epsilon(h) \leq \hat{\epsilon}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2N}}$$ The right-hand term is the **VC-style complexity penalty**. It grows with $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ (model capacity) and shrinks with $N$. Over-fitting arises when $\hat{\epsilon}(h) \to 0$ but the penalty term remains large. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning** | **Effect on Over-fitting** | |---|---|---| | $\hat{\epsilon}_{\text{train}}(h)$ | Empirical error on training set | ↓ in over-fit (false confidence) | | $\hat{\epsilon}_{\text{test}}(h)$ | Error on held-out test set | ↑ in over-fit (true performance) | | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2]$ | Mean Squared Error decomposition | Total generalization error | | $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Bias–Variance–Noise decomposition | Identifies the over-fit regime | | $\sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2N}}$ | Sample-complexity penalty (PAC bound) | ↑ when $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ is large, $N$ is small | | $\lambda \sum_j \theta_j^2$ | $L_2$ (Ridge) regularization penalty | ↑ λ ⇒ ↓ over-fitting | | $\lambda \sum_j \vert \theta_j \vert$ | $L_1$ (Lasso) regularization penalty | Promotes sparsity, ↓ over-fitting | | $J(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$ | Regularized objective | Balances fit vs. complexity | | $\text{VC}(h)$ | Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension | Capacity measure; ↑ VC ⇒ ↑ over-fit risk | | $k$-Fold CV | Cross-validation estimate of $\hat{\epsilon}_{\text{gen}}$ | Reliable detection of over-fitting | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Over-fitting diagnosis is **mission-critical** in: > - **Medical Diagnosis Systems** — model must generalize across hospitals > - **Autonomous Vehicles** — cannot memorize one road; must handle unseen scenarios > - **Financial Fraud Detection** — fraud patterns evolve; memorization is fatal > - **NLP & Speech Recognition** — must handle novel sentences > - **Recommendation Engines** — must predict on future user behaviour ### 2.5 Step-by-Step Detection Workflow 1. **Split Data:** $\mathcal{T} \to \mathcal{T}_{\text{train}}, \mathcal{T}_{\text{val}}, \mathcal{T}_{\text{test}}$ (typical ratio 70:15:15) 2. **Train Model:** Iterate over epochs, recording both $\mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$ 3. **Plot Learning Curves:** Identify the divergence point (epoch $e^*$) 4. **Apply Early Stopping** at $e^*$ or use a **regularization** mechanism 5. **Perform $k$-Fold CV** ($k=5$ or $k=10$) for a robust generalization estimate ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Bias–Variance Decomposition We derive the decomposition for a regression setting with squared loss. Let the true target be: $$y = f(\mathbf{x}) + \epsilon, \quad \text{where } \mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0, \;\; \text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$$ The expected prediction error at a fixed $\mathbf{x}$, averaged over the training-set distribution, is: $$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}, \epsilon}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x})\right)^2\right]$$ #### Step 1: Add and subtract the expected prediction $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\,f + \epsilon - \hat{f}\,\right)^2\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\,(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]) + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}) + \epsilon\,\right)^2\right] \end{aligned} $$ *(Insert the identity: $y - \hat{f} = (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]) + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}) + \epsilon$)* #### Step 2: Expand the squared trinomial $$ \begin{aligned} (y - \hat{f})^2 &= (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2 \\ &\quad + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})^2 \\ &\quad + \epsilon^2 \\ &\quad + 2(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}) \\ &\quad + 2(f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])\epsilon \\ &\quad + 2(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})\epsilon \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3: Take expectation, exploiting orthogonality Since $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}$ is independent of $\epsilon$ with zero mean, the cross-terms vanish. Also, $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon^2] = \sigma^2$. Thus: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] &= (f - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2 + \mathbb{E}\left[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f})^2\right] + \sigma^2 \\ &= \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})]}_{\text{squared bias}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})]}_{\text{model variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{noise}} \end{aligned} $$ **This is the canonical Bias–Variance–Irreducible Error decomposition.** ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Why Regularization Mitigates Over-Fitting The regularized empirical risk minimization (RERM) objective for Ridge ($L_2$) regression is: $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\theta}\right)^2}_{\text{Empirical Loss } \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta})} + \underbrace{\frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\theta_j^2}_{\text{Regularizer } \Omega(\boldsymbol{\theta})}$$ #### Step 1: Closed-form solution via the Normal Equation Setting $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} -\frac{1}{N}\mathbf{X}^\top(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \lambda \boldsymbol{\theta} &= 0 \\ \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} + N\lambda \boldsymbol{\theta} &= \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \\ (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} + N\lambda \mathbf{I}) \boldsymbol{\theta} &= \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \\ \boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} &= (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} + N\lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 2: Why this combats over-fitting - The matrix $(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} + N\lambda \mathbf{I})$ is always **positive definite**, hence invertible. - As $\lambda \uparrow$, the magnitudes of $\theta_j \downarrow$ (parameters are *shrunk*). - The model's effective complexity (VC dimension) is **reduced**, lowering variance. - This is the **classical $L_2$ regularization** solution — a primary countermeasure to over-fitting. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Behaviour vs. Model Complexity | **Model Complexity** | **Bias²** | **Variance** | **Total Error** | **Regime** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Very Low (e.g., linear on non-linear data) | **High** | Low | High | **Under-fitting** | | Optimal (just-right capacity) | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** | **Best Generalization** | | Very High (e.g., 20-degree polynomial on 30 points) | Low | **High** | High | **Over-fitting** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The "sweet spot" is the **minimum of the total error curve** — the operational goal of every well-designed ML pipeline. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Bias–Variance Trade-off Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Under-Fitting Region"] --> B["Optimal Model Region"] B --> C["Over-Fitting Region"] A --> A1["High Bias
Low Variance
Total Error HIGH"] B --> B1["Balanced Bias
Balanced Variance
Total Error MINIMUM"] C --> C1["Low Bias
High Variance
Total Error HIGH"] A1 -.->|Increase Model
Complexity| B1 B1 -.->|Further Increase
Complexity| C1 C1 --> D["Remedies:
Regularization, More Data,
Early Stopping, Dropout"] ``` ### 4.2 Over-Fitting Detection Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR raw["Raw Dataset D"] --> split["Train / Val / Test Split
70 / 15 / 15"] split --> train["Train Model
on Train Set"] train --> lossT["Compute L_train"] train --> lossV["Compute L_val
each epoch"] lossT --> plot["Plot Learning
Curves"] lossV --> plot plot --> check{"Is L_val
increasing?"} check -->|YES| overfit["Over-Fitting Detected"] check -->|NO| good["Healthy Training"] overfit --> remedy["Apply Countermeasure:
Regularization OR
Early Stopping OR
Dropout / Data Aug"] remedy --> retrain["Re-train Model"] retrain --> lossT ``` ### 4.3 Learning Curve Schematic (Mermaid Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph xAxis["X-Axis: Training Epochs"] direction LR e1["Epoch 1"] --> e5["Epoch 5"] --> e10["Epoch 10"] --> e20["Epoch 20"] end subgraph goodFit["GOOD FIT"] gT["L_train decreases &
stays close to L_val"] end subgraph overFit["OVER-FIT"] oT["L_train keeps dropping"] oV["L_val reaches minimum
then RISES sharply"] oStar["Optimal Stopping Point e*"] end xAxis --> goodFit xAxis --> overFit ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the curves:** The point $e^*$ where the validation loss is at its **global minimum** is the ideal early-stopping checkpoint. Continuing past $e^*$ is the operational definition of over-fitting in iterative training. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define **over-fitting** in the context of supervised machine learning. State any **two causes** of over-fitting. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition:** Over-fitting is a modelling phenomenon in which a learning algorithm produces a hypothesis $h$ that has a very low error on the training set but a substantially higher error on unseen test data — i.e., the model has memorized the training data including its noise rather than learning the general pattern. > > **Two Causes:** > 1. **Excessive model complexity** — e.g., using a high-degree polynomial or a deep, unregularized network when the true relationship is simple. > 2. **Insufficient training data** — when sample size $N$ is small relative to the hypothesis space $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$, the model fits idiosyncratic patterns of the few available examples. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two valid causes.)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between **over-fitting** and **under-fitting** with respect to the bias-variance trade-off. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Aspect** | **Under-Fitting** | **Over-Fitting** | |---|---|---| | Bias | **High** | Low | | Variance | Low | **High** | | Training Error | High | Very Low | | Test Error | High | High | | Model Capacity | Too low | Too high | > *Under-fitting arises when the model is too simple to capture the underlying trend (high bias). Over-fitting arises when the model is too complex and captures noise as if it were signal (high variance). Both yield high generalization error, but for opposite reasons.* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Bias-Variance decomposition** of expected prediction error. Derive the expression and discuss how it relates to over-fitting. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe **four regularization techniques** used in machine learning to prevent over-fitting. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Bias-Variance Decomposition (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Statement of the decomposition (1 mark):** For squared loss, the expected prediction error at a point $\mathbf{x}$ decomposes as: $$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T},\epsilon}\left[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})] + \text{Variance}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})] + \sigma^2$$ **Step 2 — Identification of terms (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})] &= \left(f(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})]\right)^2 \\ \text{Variance}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})] &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}\left[\left(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{T}}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})]\right)^2\right] \\ \sigma^2 &= \text{Var}(\epsilon) \quad \text{(irreducible noise)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Intuitive explanation of each term (2 marks):** - **Bias²** is the systematic error from a too-simple model → leads to **under-fitting**. - **Variance** is the sensitivity of the learned function to the specific training set → leads to **over-fitting**. - **$\sigma^2$** cannot be reduced by any algorithm. **Step 4 — Connection to over-fitting (2 marks):** As model complexity (e.g., polynomial degree) increases, **Bias² decreases monotonically** while **Variance increases monotonically**. Over-fitting is the regime where the marginal reduction in Bias² is dominated by the marginal increase in Variance, causing the **total error to rise again**. The optimal model lies at the minimum of the Bias² + Variance curve. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Regularization Techniques (7 Marks) **1. $L_2$ Regularization (Ridge) — 2 marks:** $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\theta_j^2$$ Penalizes large weights; shrinks all parameters toward zero. Closed-form solution: $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} + N\lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$. **2. $L_1$ Regularization (Lasso) — 2 marks:** $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert \theta_j \vert$$ Promotes **sparsity** — drives many weights to exactly zero, performing implicit feature selection. **3. Dropout (Neural Networks) — 1.5 marks:** During each forward pass, randomly "drop" a fraction $p$ of neurons. This prevents co-adaptation of features and acts as an implicit ensemble. **4. Early Stopping — 1.5 marks:** Monitor validation loss; halt training at epoch $e^*$ where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$ reaches its minimum. Effectively limits the optimization trajectory's length and hence the achievable complexity. *(Other acceptable techniques: Data Augmentation, Batch Normalization, Cross-Validation–based model selection, Ensemble methods (Bagging).)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **trade-off between model complexity and generalization error**. Identify the regimes of under-fitting and over-fitting. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **$k$-fold cross-validation** as a method to detect and prevent over-fitting. Mention its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Complexity vs. Generalization (7 Marks) **Step 1 — The trade-off curve (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Low Complexity
(e.g., linear model)"] -->|Increase capacity| B["Optimal Complexity
(sweet spot)"] B -->|Further increase| C["High Complexity
(e.g., 20-degree polynomial)"] A --> A1["Under-Fit:
High Bias"] C --> C1["Over-Fit:
High Variance"] B --> B1["Best Generalization:
Min Total Error"] ``` **Step 2 — Mathematical characterization (2 marks):** Total generalization error = $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$. - Low complexity → high $\text{Bias}^2$ dominates → **under-fitting**. - High complexity → high $\text{Variance}$ dominates → **over-fitting**. - Optimal point: minimum of the total curve. **Step 3 — Operational consequences (2 marks):** - **Under-fit regime:** Both training and test errors are high and similar. - **Optimal regime:** Both training and test errors are low and close to each other. - **Over-fit regime:** Training error is very low, but test error is significantly higher. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — $k$-Fold Cross-Validation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Partition the dataset $\mathcal{T}$ into $k$ equal-sized, mutually exclusive folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_k$. 2. For each $i \in \{1, \ldots, k\}$: - Train on $\mathcal{T} \setminus \mathcal{F}_i$ - Validate on $\mathcal{F}_i$, recording error $E_i$ 3. Compute the cross-validated error: $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i$ **Step 2 — Why it detects over-fitting (2 marks):** A model that has over-fit the training data will show **consistently high** $E_i$ across all held-out folds, because the spurious patterns it learned do not generalize. The variance of the $E_i$'s across folds is itself a diagnostic indicator. **Step 3 — Advantages & Limitations (2 marks):** **Advantages:** - Maximizes use of limited data; every sample is used for both training and validation. - Reduces variance in the performance estimate compared to a single hold-out split. - Robust model selection metric. **Limitations:** - Computationally expensive ($k$ times the training cost; typically $k=5$ or $k=10$). - Not ideal for **time-series** or **dependent** data (random splits leak future info). - Stratification needed for imbalanced classification. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Over-Fitting Questions:** > 1. **Writing the definition without mentioning the gap** between training error and test error — examiners *specifically* look for the phrase "low training error but high generalization error" (Loss: 1 mark). > 2. **Confusing Bias with Variance** in the decomposition — remember: high bias ⇒ under-fit, high variance ⇒ over-fit (Loss: 1–2 marks). > 3. **Forgetting the irreducible noise term** $\sigma^2$ in the decomposition (Loss: 1 mark). > 4. **Not providing the closed-form Ridge solution** when asked about regularization derivation (Loss: 2 marks). > 5. **Mixing up $L_1$ vs $L_2$** — Lasso promotes sparsity, Ridge shrinks but keeps all features (Loss: 1 mark). > 6. **In $k$-fold CV answers, omitting the final averaging step** $\text{CV} = \frac{1}{k}\sum E_i$ (Loss: 1 mark). > 7. **In learning-curve questions, failing to identify $e^*$** (the optimal early-stopping epoch) (Loss: 1 mark). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Over-Fitting — One-Page Rapid Recap:** > > ✅ **Definition:** Low training error, high test error — the model has memorized noise. > ✅ **Primary Indicator:** Widening gap between $\mathcal{L}_{\text{train}}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$. > ✅ **Bias–Variance Link:** Over-fitting = **low bias, high variance** regime. > ✅ **Decomposition:** $\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$. > ✅ **PAC Bound:** Error $\leq \hat{\epsilon} + \sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2N}}$. > ✅ **Causes:** Small $N$, large $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$, noisy labels, over-training, no regularization. > ✅ **Remedies:** > - **$L_1$ / $L_2$ regularization** ($J = \mathcal{L} + \lambda\Omega$) > - **Early stopping** at epoch $e^*$ > - **Dropout** in neural networks > - **Data augmentation** to increase $N$ > - **$k$-fold cross-validation** for robust model selection > - **Ensemble methods** (Bagging, Random Forests) > - **Reduce model complexity** (fewer layers, lower polynomial degree) > ✅ **Detection Tool:** Plot learning curves; identify divergence point of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$. > ✅ **Analogy:** A student who memorizes last year's paper word-for-word will fail the real exam. > ✅ **Real-world Impact:** Critical in medical, automotive, financial, and NLP applications where generalization is non-negotiable.

topics.title: Curse of Dimensionality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Course Code:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Curse of Dimensionality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curse of Dimensionality (CoD)** is a term coined by **Richard E. Bellman** (1957, Dynamic Programming) to describe the various phenomena that arise when analyzing and organizing data in **high-dimensional spaces** (often with dimensions exceeding 4–10) that do not occur in low-dimensional settings such as the 2-D plane or 3-D physical space we intuitively understand.

In the context of **Machine Learning and Pattern Recognition**, the Curse of Dimensionality refers to the exponential growth of data requirements and computational complexity as the number of features (dimensions) $n$ increases, making many learning algorithms (KNN, clustering, regression) infeasible or highly inaccurate in high dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PBRAT404 / Module 1):** This topic is foundational for understanding why **dimensionality reduction** (PCA, LDA, t-SNE) and **feature engineering** are mandatory sub-routines in any real-world ML pipeline.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a librarian and you want to place **one book per cubic meter** on a 1-D shelf (a single row).

- **1-D shelf** of length 10 m → you can hold **10 books**.
- **2-D room** of 10 m × 10 m → you can hold **100 books**.
- **3-D warehouse** of 10 m × 10 m × 10 m → you can hold **1,000 books**.
- **10-D hyper-warehouse** of 10 m per axis → you can hold $10^{10} = 10$ **billion** books.

Now imagine a student wants to find the nearest book. In 1-D, the average distance between neighboring books is $1$ m. In 10-D, the average distance to the *nearest* book is roughly **the same as the distance to the *farthest* book** — because the space is so vast and sparse that "near" loses its meaning.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Empty Space" Paradox:** As dimensions grow, the data points you have become tiny isolated islands floating in a vast, empty ocean of feature space. All your algorithms (KNN, distance-based clustering, density estimation) lose their statistical power because they were designed assuming points are "relatively close."

## 1.3 Key Manifestations of the Curse

| # | Phenomenon | One-Line Description |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Data Sparsity** | Available data points become exponentially sparse as $n$ grows. |
| 2 | **Distance Concentration** | The ratio $\frac{\text{std}(d)}{\text{mean}(d)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. |
| 3 | **Volume Migration to Corners** | Most of a hypercube's volume lies in its corners (not its center). |
| 4 | **Hubness Phenomenon** | Some points become "hubs" — nearest neighbors of almost every other point. |
| 5 | **Overfitting** | The number of parameters / samples ratio explodes → poor generalization. |
| 6 | **Computational Blow-up** | Distance computations scale as $O(n \cdot m)$ per query. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Curse

### Pillar 1: Exponential Growth of Space Volume
The volume of an $n$-dimensional unit hypercube is $1^n = 1$ (always), but the volume of its **inner inscribed hyper-sphere** (radius $r = 0.5$) shrinks drastically.

### Pillar 2: Sparsity of Data
If we sample $m$ points uniformly in $[0,1]^n$, the expected fraction of the unit cube's volume covered is:

$$f_{\text{covered}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{\text{Vol}}\right)^m \approx 1 - e^{-m/\text{Vol}}$$

For the unit cube, $\text{Vol} = 1$, so to maintain the *same density* of points when $n$ increases, $m$ must grow **exponentially**.

### Pillar 3: Breakdown of Distance Metrics
For any $p$-norm, as $n \to \infty$, the distance between the *closest* point and the *farthest* point in a sample becomes indistinguishable.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU board examiner expects a student to recognize or reproduce. Master these — they form the backbone of any 14-mark question on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Volume of $n$-D hypercube, side $a$ | $V_{\text{cube}} = a^n$ | cubic units (in $n$-D) |
| 2 | Volume of $n$-D hypersphere, radius $r$ | $V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}+1\right)} r^n$ | cubic units (in $n$-D) |
| 3 | Gamma function for half-integers | $\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)=\sqrt{\pi}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Required data for density $\rho$ | $m \propto \rho^{\,n}$ | $m$ grows exponentially |
| 5 | Mean distance between random pts (norm) | $\mathbb{E}[d] = \sqrt{2n}\,\sigma$ (for standard normal) | length units |
| 6 | Std deviation of pairwise distance | $\text{std}(d) = \sqrt{2n\!\left(1-\tfrac{1}{n}\right)}\,\sigma$ | length units |
| 7 | Coefficient of variation of distance | $C_V = \dfrac{\text{std}(d)}{\mathbb{E}[d]} \to \sqrt{1 - \tfrac{1}{n}} \to 1$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Volume fraction in corner sub-cube $\epsilon$ | $f_{\text{corner}} = (1-\epsilon)^n \to 0$ as $n \uparrow$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Volume fraction in center $\epsilon$-ball | $f_{\text{center}} = \epsilon^n \to 0$ as $n \uparrow$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Hughes phenomenon (sample complexity) | $P_{\text{error}} \propto \exp(-c \cdot m/n)$ | dimensionless prob. |
| 11 | Euclidean norm definition | $\Vert x \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | length units |
| 12 | Hamming distance (binary vectors) | $d_H(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[x_i \ne y_i]$ | count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used throughout:** $n$ = number of dimensions (features), $m$ = number of samples, $d$ = distance, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $\Gamma(\cdot)$ = Gamma function (extension of factorial).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why CoD matters |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Computer Vision** | A 224×224 RGB image has $n = 150{,}528$ features — raw pixels cannot be fed to KNN. |
| **Bioinformatics** | Microarray data: 20,000 genes, ~100 patients. The "small $m$, large $n$" regime is the worst case. |
| **NLP / LLMs** | Vocabulary sizes of 50,000+ tokens create ultra-high-dimensional sparse vectors. |
| **Recommender Systems** | User–item matrices are typically $10^6 \times 10^6$ — sparse and high-rank. |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Isolation forests and one-class SVMs rely on random splits in $n$-D — performance degrades fast. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume of an n-Dimensional Hypersphere

We begin with the standard recursive integral for the volume of an $n$-ball of radius $r$.

### Step 1: Set up the cross-sectional integral
The volume $V_n(r)$ can be built by integrating circular cross-sections of radius $\sqrt{r^2 - x_n^2}$ in the $(n-1)$-D plane at coordinate $x_n$:

$$V_n(r) = \int_{-r}^{r} V_{n-1}\!\left(\sqrt{r^2 - x_n^2}\right) dx_n$$

### Step 2: Substitute the volume of the lower-dim ball
Using the known formula $V_{n-1}(R) = C_{n-1}\, R^{n-1}$, where $C_{n-1}$ is the dimensionless constant:

$$V_n(r) = C_{n-1} \int_{-r}^{r} \left(r^2 - x_n^2\right)^{\!(n-1)/2} dx_n$$

### Step 3: Factor out $r^n$ and apply a trigonometric substitution
Let $x_n = r\sin\theta$, $dx_n = r\cos\theta\,d\theta$. The bounds become $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$:

$$V_n(r) = C_{n-1}\, r^n \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos^n\theta\, d\theta$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Wallis-style integral
For even/odd $n$, this integral evaluates to closed-form expressions, but more elegantly, we use the **Beta function**:

$$V_n(r) = C_{n-1}\, r^n \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n+2}{2}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Solve the recursive constant and consolidate
By induction, $C_{n-1} = \dfrac{\pi^{(n-1)/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n+1}{2}\right)}$. Multiplying and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\,V_n(r) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}+1\right)} r^n\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key numerical sanity check:** $n=2 \Rightarrow V_2 = \pi r^2$ (area of disk ✓). $n=3 \Rightarrow V_3 = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ (sphere ✓). $n=10 \Rightarrow V_{10} = \tfrac{\pi^5}{120} r^{10} \approx 2.55\,r^{10}$ — note that $V_{10}(1) < V_{10}(2) - V_{10}(1)$ for the same $r$, showing concentration of mass at the surface.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Distance Concentration in High Dimensions

### Step 1: Define the random experiment
Let $X, Y$ be two random vectors drawn i.i.d. from $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 I_n)$. Define $D = \Vert X - Y \Vert_2$.

Because $X - Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, 2\sigma^2 I_n)$, each squared component $(X_i - Y_i)^2$ has mean $2\sigma^2$ and variance $8\sigma^4$.

### Step 2: Compute the mean of $D^2$

$$\mathbb{E}[D^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[(X_i - Y_i)^2] = n \cdot 2\sigma^2 = 2n\sigma^2$$

### Step 3: Compute the mean of $D$ (using Jensen's inequality)

$$\mathbb{E}[D] \approx \sqrt{2n}\,\sigma \quad \text{(asymptotically, by CLT)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the variance of $D^2$

$$\text{Var}(D^2) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}\!\left((X_i-Y_i)^2\right) = n \cdot 8\sigma^4 = 8n\sigma^4$$

### Step 5: Apply the delta method to get $\text{Var}(D)$

For large $n$, $D \approx \sqrt{D^2}$, and by the **delta method**:

$$\text{Var}(D) \approx \frac{\text{Var}(D^2)}{4\,\mathbb{E}[D^2]} = \frac{8n\sigma^4}{4 \cdot 2n\sigma^2} = \sigma^2$$

### Step 6: Standard deviation of $D$

$$\text{std}(D) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(D)} = \sigma$$

### Step 7: The coefficient of variation

$$C_V = \frac{\text{std}(D)}{\mathbb{E}[D]} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2n}\,\sigma} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2n}}$$

### Step 8: The asymptotic conclusion

$$\boxed{\,C_V = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2n}} \xrightarrow{\,n \to \infty\,} 0\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** As $n$ grows, the *relative* spread of pairwise distances collapses. For $n=10$, $C_V \approx 0.224$; for $n=1000$, $C_V \approx 0.022$. This means "nearest" and "farthest" neighbors become **statistically indistinguishable** — a fatal flaw for KNN, K-means, and any distance-based algorithm.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volume Migration to the Corners of a Hypercube

### Step 1: Set up the geometry
Consider the unit hypercube $[0,1]^n$. The "inner" region (a centered hypercube of side length $1 - 2\epsilon$) has volume $(1 - 2\epsilon)^n$.

The "corner" region (one of the $2^n$ octants beyond the $\epsilon$-shell on every face) has volume $\epsilon^n$ per corner.

### Step 2: Volume of the inner hypercube
Using a first-order Taylor expansion $\ln(1 - 2\epsilon) \approx -2\epsilon$ for small $\epsilon$:

$$V_{\text{inner}} = (1 - 2\epsilon)^n = e^{n\ln(1-2\epsilon)} \approx e^{-2n\epsilon}$$

### Step 3: Volume of one corner sub-cube
Similarly:

$$V_{\text{corner}} = \epsilon^n = e^{n\ln\epsilon}$$

### Step 4: Ratio of corner-volume to total-volume
For $\epsilon = 0.01$ and $n = 100$:

$$V_{\text{inner}} = (0.98)^{100} \approx 0.132 \quad ; \quad V_{\text{corner}} = (0.01)^{100} = 10^{-200} \approx 0$$

### Step 5: Conclusion
$$\boxed{\,\lim_{n\to\infty} (1 - 2\epsilon)^n = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{n\to\infty} \epsilon^n = 0\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric meaning:** In high dimensions, *both* the "center" and the "corners" of the cube are essentially empty. The mass of the cube migrates to a thin "shell" just inside the surface — a region of measure zero in low-D but dominant in high-D.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Impact of the Curse on Common ML Algorithms

| Algorithm | Core Mechanism | Effect of CoD | Mitigation |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** | Vote of $k$ nearest points | "Nearest" loses meaning ($C_V \to 0$) | PCA, metric learning, Mahalanobis |
| **K-Means Clustering** | Iterative distance-based assignment | Cluster centers drift to surface | Spectral clustering, spherical k-means |
| **Linear Regression** | Matrix inversion $(X^TX)^{-1}$ | Condition number $\kappa(X^TX) \to \infty$ | Ridge / Lasso regularization |
| **Decision Trees** | Greedy axis-aligned splits | Exponential splits, overfitting | Random forests, gradient boosting |
| **Gaussian NB** | Density estimation in $n$-D | $P(x) \to 0$ everywhere | Feature selection, kernel density |
| **Neural Networks** | Weight matrix $\in \mathbb{R}^{h \times n}$ | Vanishing gradients, overparameterization | Dropout, batch norm, weight decay |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: How CoD Manifests in an ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw High-D Data: n features] --> B[Distance Computations]
    A --> C[Density Estimation]
    A --> D[Model Parameter Estimation]
    B --> E[Distance Concentration: std/mean to 0]
    C --> F[Sparse Density: P x to 0]
    D --> G[Overfitting: Variance high]
    E --> H[KNN / K-Means Failures]
    F --> I[NB / KDE Failures]
    G --> J[Poor Generalization]
    H --> K[ML Pipeline Output: Unreliable]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Mitigation Layer]
    L --> M[Feature Selection]
    L --> N[PCA / LDA / t-SNE]
    L --> O[Regularization: Ridge Lasso]
    L --> P[Domain Knowledge Engineering]
    M --> Q[Reduced Dim: n low]
    N --> Q
    O --> R[Stable Model]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Detection and Mitigation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Compute Dimensionality] --> S2{n exceeds 10 to 20}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Step 2: Check Sample Size Ratio]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Proceed with Standard ML]
    S3 --> S5{m divided by n less than 5}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 3a: Apply Feature Selection]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Step 3b: Apply Dimensionality Reduction]
    S6 --> S8[Step 4: Retrain and Validate]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 5: Monitor for Distance Collapse]
    S9 --> S10[Step 6: Deploy with Drift Checks]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: The Three Families of Mitigation Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CO[Curse of Dimensionality] --> F1[Family 1: Feature Selection]
    CO --> F2[Family 2: Feature Extraction]
    CO --> F3[Family 3: Regularization]
    F1 --> F1a[Filter Methods: Chi2, MI]
    F1 --> F1b[Wrapper Methods: RFE]
    F1 --> F1c[Embedded: Lasso, Tree Importance]
    F2 --> F2a[Linear: PCA, LDA, ICA]
    F2 --> F2b[Nonlinear: t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders]
    F2 --> F2c[Manifold: Isomap, LLE]
    F3 --> F3a[L1 Regularization]
    F3 --> F3b[L2 Regularization]
    F3 --> F3c[Dropout, Early Stopping]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CoD in a Real Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input Space: nD, m samples] --> P1[Preprocessing: Normalize, Encode]
    P1 --> P2[Train Test Split: 80 by 20]
    P2 --> P3[CoD Diagnostic Module]
    P3 --> P3a[Compute Mean Pairwise Distance]
    P3 --> P3b[Compute CV of Distances]
    P3 --> P3c[Check Sample to Feature Ratio]
    P3c --> D{CV less than 0.05 or ratio less than 5}
    D -- Yes --> R1[Apply PCA: keep 95 percent variance]
    D -- No --> R2[Use Original Features]
    R1 --> T1[Train KNN with k equals sqrt m]
    R2 --> T1
    T1 --> E1[Evaluate: Accuracy, F1, AUC]
    E1 --> E2{Loss Curve Diverges}
    E2 -- Yes --> R3[Add L2 Regularization]
    E2 -- No --> OUT[Final Deployed Model]
    R3 --> T1
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Answering**
> 1. **Confusing $n$ and $m$:** Examiners commonly deduct 2 marks if you swap "number of features" with "number of samples." State them explicitly at the start of every answer.
> 2. **Skipping the Gamma function:** When asked about the hypersphere volume, do not write $4\pi r^3 / 3$ for general $n$ — the only acceptable general form involves $\Gamma(\cdot)$.
> 3. **Forgetting the assumption:** Always state "assuming uniformly / normally distributed data" before computing the coefficient of variation.
> 4. **Missing units:** Mark loss if a numerical answer omits the unit or fails to specify "dimensionless."
> 5. **No examples in 14-mark questions:** A 14-mark answer without at least one *worked numerical example* is considered incomplete. Always include one.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Curse of Dimensionality. Why is it relevant to machine learning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Curse of Dimensionality (Bellman, 1957) refers to the exponential growth in data requirements and computational complexity as the number of features $n$ in a dataset increases. In ML, it is relevant because: (i) data becomes sparse, (ii) distances lose discriminative power ($C_V \to 0$), and (iii) models overfit. **Example:** a 30-D space with $m=1000$ points has the same density as a 1-D space with only $\approx 0.0003$ points, making KNN and density-based algorithms unreliable. *(Marks: Definition 1 + Relevance 1 + Example 1)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the formula for the volume of an $n$-dimensional hypersphere. What happens to this volume as $n \to \infty$ for fixed $r=1$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$V_n(1) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2 + 1)}$$
As $n$ increases, the volume rises initially (peak near $n \approx 7$), then **decreases** and tends to $0$ as $n \to \infty$. **Implication:** the entire mass of the unit ball migrates to a thin surface shell, and the "interior" becomes empty — a direct cause of the curse. *(Marks: Formula 1 + Behaviour 1 + Implication 1)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the volume of an $n$-dimensional hypersphere of radius $r$. Show numerically that the volume is concentrated in a thin shell as $n$ grows. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the "distance concentration" phenomenon in high dimensions and show that the coefficient of variation of pairwise distances tends to 0. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Recursive integral definition**

$$V_n(r) = \int_{-r}^{r} V_{n-1}\!\left(\sqrt{r^2 - x_n^2}\right) dx_n$$

**Step 2: Substitute and integrate via the Beta function**

$$V_n(r) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}+1\right)} r^n$$

**Step 3: Compute numerical values for $r=1$**

$$V_2(1) = \pi \approx 3.14$$
$$V_3(1) = \tfrac{4\pi}{3} \approx 4.19$$
$$V_5(1) = \tfrac{8\pi^2}{15} \approx 5.26$$
$$V_{10}(1) = \tfrac{\pi^5}{120} \approx 2.55$$
$$V_{20}(1) \approx 0.026$$

**Step 4: Interpret the shell concentration**
The volume $V_n(r) - V_n(r(1-\epsilon))$ for small $\epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.01$) accounts for nearly $100\%$ of the total volume when $n$ is large. For $n=100$ and $\epsilon = 0.05$:

$$\frac{V_{100}(1) - V_{100}(0.95)}{V_{100}(1)} = 1 - 0.95^{100} \approx 0.994$$

This proves the volume is **concentrated in a thin shell near the surface**. *(7 marks distributed as: Derivation 3 + Numerical 2 + Interpretation 2)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Setup**
Let $X, Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 I_n)$ i.i.d. Define $D = \Vert X - Y \Vert_2$.

**Step 2: Mean of $D$**

$$\mathbb{E}[D^2] = 2n\sigma^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbb{E}[D] \approx \sqrt{2n}\,\sigma$$

**Step 3: Variance of $D$**

$$\text{Var}(D) \approx \sigma^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{std}(D) \approx \sigma$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of variation**

$$C_V = \frac{\text{std}(D)}{\mathbb{E}[D]} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2n}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical demonstration**

| $n$ | $C_V$ |
|-----|-------|
| 2 | 0.500 |
| 10 | 0.224 |
| 100 | 0.071 |
| 1000 | 0.022 |
| 10000 | 0.007 |

**Step 6: Implication**
As $n \to \infty$, $C_V \to 0$, meaning the ratio of "farthest" to "nearest" distance approaches 1. KNN can no longer distinguish neighbors. *(7 marks distributed as: Setup 1 + Mean 1 + Variance 1 + $C_V$ 2 + Table 1 + Implication 1)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain how the volume of a hypercube is distributed between its center and corners as dimensions increase. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the impact of the Curse of Dimensionality on K-Nearest Neighbors and K-Means clustering. Suggest two mitigation techniques. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Define the regions**
Consider the unit hypercube $[0,1]^n$. Define the **inner sub-cube** of side $1 - 2\epsilon$ (centered at the origin) and the **corner sub-cube** of side $\epsilon$.

**Step 2: Volume of the inner sub-cube**

$$V_{\text{inner}} = (1 - 2\epsilon)^n$$

**Step 3: Volume of one corner sub-cube**

$$V_{\text{corner}} = \epsilon^n$$

**Step 4: Limiting behaviour**

$$V_{\text{inner}} \xrightarrow{n \to \infty} 0 \quad ; \quad V_{\text{corner}} \xrightarrow{n \to \infty} 0$$

**Step 5: Numerical illustration with $\epsilon = 0.05$**

| $n$ | $V_{\text{inner}}$ | $V_{\text{corner}}$ |
|-----|--------------------|---------------------|
| 2 | 0.81 | 0.0025 |
| 5 | 0.598 | $3.1 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| 10 | 0.358 | $9.7 \times 10^{-14}$ |
| 50 | 0.0045 | $\approx 0$ |
| 100 | $2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\approx 0$ |

**Step 6: Visual description (verbal, for exam)**
- **2-D square:** Most area is in the middle; corners are negligible.
- **10-D hypercube:** Inner region is tiny; corner regions are essentially zero volume; bulk mass lies in a thin "mantle" between them.

This is why uniform sampling in high-D gives you points almost exclusively **on the boundary** of the data manifold. *(7 marks: Region def 1 + Formulae 2 + Limit 1 + Table 2 + Interpretation 1)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Impact on KNN (3 marks):**
KNN relies on the assumption that "close" points are similar. With $C_V \to 0$ as $n$ grows, the nearest and farthest neighbors become equidistant. The algorithm can no longer find a meaningful local neighborhood, leading to unstable predictions and class-boundary blurring.

**Impact on K-Means (3 marks):**
K-Means assigns points to the nearest centroid using Euclidean distance. As dimensions increase:
- All points become nearly equidistant from all centroids.
- Cluster assignments become nearly random.
- Convergence slows; the inertia plateaus.

**Mitigation Techniques (1 mark each):**
1. **PCA / Dimensionality Reduction:** Project to a lower-dimensional subspace that retains $> 95\%$ of variance.
2. **Feature Selection / Regularization:** Use Lasso or mutual information to drop irrelevant features.
3. **(Bonus) Metric Learning / Domain Engineering:** Use Mahalanobis distance or domain-specific embeddings.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Bellman coined the term in 1957** in the context of dynamic programming.
- [x] **Two root causes:** (i) Exponential growth of $n$-D volume, (ii) Sample scarcity.
- [x] **Hypersphere volume:** $V_n(r) = \dfrac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)} r^n$ — peaks around $n \approx 7$ and decays for $n \to \infty$.
- [x] **Hypercube corner migration:** $(1-2\epsilon)^n \to 0$ and $\epsilon^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ — mass sits in the shell.
- [x] **Distance concentration:** $C_V = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2n}} \to 0$ — "nearest" = "farthest" in high-D.
- [x] **KNN fails** because it cannot distinguish neighbors; **K-Means fails** because all points look equidistant.
- [x] **Sample complexity:** To maintain density, $m$ must grow **exponentially** with $n$.
- [x] **Hubness:** Some points become universal nearest neighbors (a symptom of CoD).
- [x] **Three mitigations:** (1) Feature Selection, (2) Feature Extraction (PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders), (3) Regularization.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Image pixels (150K features), genomics (20K genes), NLP (50K vocab).
- [x] **Hughes phenomenon:** Adding features beyond a point *increases* classification error.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** (uniform/normal distribution) before applying CoD formulas.
- [x] **For 14-mark answers:** include a derivation + numerical table + interpretation. Never skip the worked example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transformation: Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Transformation: Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Transformation: Scaling** is a data preprocessing technique used in machine learning to standardize or normalize the range of independent variables (features) in a dataset. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBRAT404**, scaling refers to the mathematical adjustment of feature values so that they conform to a common scale or distribution, without distorting differences in the ranges of values or losing information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (PBRAT404 - Module 1)**
> *Feature scaling is the process of bringing all features to the same magnitude/scale so that no single feature dominates the learning algorithm due to its larger numerical range. It is a mandatory preprocessing step for distance-based and gradient-based ML algorithms.*

The fundamental motivation arises from the fact that features in real-world datasets often have **different units and magnitudes**. For example, a `Salary` feature may range in **Lakhs (0 to 1,000,000)**, while an `Age` feature ranges between **0 and 100**. When such features are fed directly into algorithms like **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), K-Means, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Principal Component Analysis (PCA), or Gradient Descent-based models**, the feature with the larger numerical range will disproportionately influence the model's learning process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology — Memorize These Exact Terms:**
> - **Normalization** → Min-Max Scaling (scales to a fixed range, usually [0, 1])
> - **Standardization** → Z-score Scaling (zero mean, unit variance)
> - **Robust Scaling** → Uses median and IQR (resistant to outliers)
> - **Unit Vector Scaling / Normalization (L2)** → Divides by the vector's norm

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **tourist visiting a foreign country** where distances are measured in *miles*, weights in *stones*, and temperatures in *Fahrenheit*. Even though all of these quantities describe physical properties, comparing them is awkward and confusing. To make sense of the data, you would **convert everything to a single, unified system** (e.g., SI units: meters, kilograms, Celsius). 

**Feature scaling is the SI-unit conversion of machine learning.** It ensures that every feature "speaks the same numerical language" so the model can fairly compare and weigh them.

A more domain-specific analogy: consider **grading exam papers**. A student who scores `95/100` in Math should not be unfairly ranked below a student who scores `5/10` in English just because the raw number is smaller. By converting both to percentages (a common scale), we obtain a fair comparison — this is exactly what scaling accomplishes.

> [!TIP]
> **Why scaling matters — Decision Rule of Thumb:**
> - **Distance-based algorithms** (KNN, K-Means, SVM, PCA) → Scaling is **MANDATORY**
> - **Tree-based algorithms** (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost) → Scaling is **NOT required** (they split on thresholds)
> - **Linear/Logistic Regression with Gradient Descent** → Scaling **SPEEDS UP convergence**
> - **Neural Networks (Deep Learning)** → Scaling is **CRITICAL** (activations and weights blow up otherwise)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Scaling? — The Core "Why"

When a machine learning algorithm computes distances (e.g., Euclidean distance) or performs gradient-based optimization, **features with larger magnitudes dominate the computation**. Let us analyze this with a simple two-feature example:

Consider two features:
- **$x_1$ = Salary**, ranging from **30,000 to 200,000**
- **$x_2$ = Age**, ranging from **20 to 70**

The Euclidean distance between two points is:
$$d = \sqrt{(x_1^{(1)} - x_1^{(2)})^2 + (x_2^{(1)} - x_2^{(2)})^2}$$

If a salary difference is **50,000** and an age difference is **5**, the squared salary difference ($2.5 \times 10^9$) will completely overwhelm the squared age difference (**25**). The model will essentially behave as if `Age` does not exist. Scaling fixes this.

## 2.2 Major Scaling Techniques — Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Min-Max Scaling (Normalization)

**Operational Steps:**
1. Identify the **minimum** and **maximum** value of the feature.
2. Subtract the minimum from every value.
3. Divide the result by the **range** (max − min).
4. Optionally rescale to a custom range $[a, b]$ (default: $[0, 1]$).

**Key Properties:**
- Bounded output range: **$[0, 1]$** (or custom $[a, b]$)
- **Highly sensitive to outliers** (since min/max are affected by extreme values)
- Preserves the **shape of the original distribution**
- Preserves **zero entries** (useful for sparse data)

### 2.2.2 Standardization (Z-Score Scaling)

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute the **mean ($\mu$)** of the feature.
2. Compute the **standard deviation ($\sigma$)** of the feature.
3. Subtract the mean from every value.
4. Divide by the standard deviation.

**Key Properties:**
- Output: **zero mean**, **unit variance** ($\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$)
- **NOT bounded** to a specific range (output can be negative or > 1)
- **Less sensitive to outliers** than Min-Max (mean and std are more robust than min/max)
- Does **NOT preserve zero entries** (poor for sparse data)
- Assumes (or works best when) data is **approximately normally distributed**

### 2.2.3 Robust Scaling

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute the **median (Q2)** of the feature.
2. Compute the **Interquartile Range (IQR = Q3 − Q1)**.
3. Subtract the median from every value.
4. Divide by the IQR.

**Key Properties:**
- **Extremely robust to outliers** (uses median and quartiles)
- Output: roughly centered around zero, but **not bounded**
- Recommended when dataset has **significant outliers**

### 2.2.4 Max Abs Scaling

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute the **maximum absolute value** of the feature.
2. Divide every value by this maximum absolute value.

**Key Properties:**
- Output range: **$[-1, +1]$**
- **Preserves zero entries** (sparse data friendly)
- Does **NOT shift/center** the data

### 2.2.5 Unit Vector Scaling (L2 Normalization)

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute the **L2 norm** of the feature vector: $\vert \vert x \vert \vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum x_i^2}$
2. Divide every value by this norm.

**Key Properties:**
- Scales each **sample/row** (not the feature column) to **unit length (norm = 1)**
- Commonly used in **text classification, cosine similarity, and clustering**

### 2.2.6 Logarithmic Transformation

**Operational Steps:**
1. Apply $x' = \log(x)$ or $x' = \log(x+1)$ for non-negative data.

**Key Properties:**
- **Compresses** the range of large values; **expands** small values
- Handles **skewed (right-tailed) distributions** (e.g., income, population)
- Useful for **linearizing exponential growth** patterns

### 2.2.7 Power Transformation (Box-Cox & Yeo-Johnson)

**Box-Cox** requires strictly **positive** data, while **Yeo-Johnson** supports **zero and negative** values. These transformations make the data more **Gaussian (normal)** in shape.

$$x' = \frac{x^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, \quad \lambda \neq 0 \quad \text{(Box-Cox)}$$

$$\ln(x), \quad \lambda = 0 \quad \text{(Box-Cox, limit case)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU ESE. Memorize all formulas and their boundary conditions.

| **Scaling Method** | **Formula** | **Output Range** | **Sensitive to Outliers?** | **Preserves Zeros?** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Min-Max (Normalization)** | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | $[0, 1]$ (default) | **Yes — High** | Yes | Neural Networks, Image pixels, Bounded inputs |
| **Min-Max (Custom Range $[a,b]$)** | $x' = a + \dfrac{(x - x_{\min})(b - a)}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | $[a, b]$ | Yes — High | Yes | Rescaling to a specific application range |
| **Standardization (Z-score)** | $x' = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $(-\infty, +\infty)$, $\mu'=0$, $\sigma'=1$ | Moderate | No | Linear/Logistic Regression, SVM, PCA, LDA |
| **Robust Scaling** | $x' = \dfrac{x - \text{median}(x)}{\text{IQR}} = \dfrac{x - Q_2}{Q_3 - Q_1}$ | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | **No — Robust** | No | Data with significant outliers |
| **Max Abs Scaling** | $x' = \dfrac{x}{\max(\vert x \vert)}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Yes | **Yes** | Sparse data (e.g., TF-IDF matrices) |
| **Unit Vector (L2)** | $x' = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{\sum_{i} x_i^2}}$ | Row norm = 1 | Yes | Yes | Text data, Cosine similarity, Clustering |
| **Log Transformation** | $x' = \log(x)$ or $\log(x+1)$ | Compressed | Somewhat | Conditional | Right-skewed data (income, counts) |
| **Box-Cox (Power)** | $x' = \dfrac{x^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}$ | Approximately normal | Conditional | Conditional | Making data more Gaussian |
| **Yeo-Johnson (Power)** | Supports $x \le 0$ | Approximately normal | Conditional | Conditional | Same as Box-Cox but for non-positive data |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** Always write absolute value as `\vert x \vert` in handwritten scans, never as `|x|`, since the single vertical bar can be misread as the digit **1** or the letter **l** during digital valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of Scaling** |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Normalizing ECG, EEG amplitude values to a common baseline before feature extraction. |
| **Image Processing / Computer Vision** | Min-Max scaling of pixel intensities to $[0, 1]$ for CNN input. |
| **Financial Modeling** | Standardizing stock prices and trading volumes (different units) for portfolio optimization. |
| **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** | L2 normalization of TF-IDF vectors for cosine-similarity-based document retrieval. |
| **IoT Sensor Fusion** | Robust scaling of heterogeneous sensor streams (temperature in °C, vibration in g, pressure in kPa). |
| **Geospatial Engineering** | Standardizing elevation (m) and rainfall (mm) for landslide susceptibility ML models. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Min-Max Scaling — Mathematical Foundation

We want to linearly map an arbitrary interval $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ to a target interval $[a, b]$.

**Step 1:** Define a linear transformation of the form:
$$x' = \alpha \cdot x + \beta$$

**Step 2:** Apply the boundary conditions:
- When $x = x_{\min}$, we want $x' = a$
- When $x = x_{\max}$, we want $x' = b$

**Step 3:** Set up the system of equations:
$$a = \alpha \cdot x_{\min} + \beta$$
$$b = \alpha \cdot x_{\max} + \beta$$

**Step 4:** Subtract the first equation from the second:
$$b - a = \alpha \cdot (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\alpha$:
$$\alpha = \frac{b - a}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\beta$ by substituting $\alpha$ back:
$$\beta = a - \alpha \cdot x_{\min} = a - \frac{(b - a) \cdot x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute both into the transformation:
$$x' = \frac{b - a}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}} \cdot x + a - \frac{(b - a) \cdot x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Step 8:** Factor out $(b - a)$ and combine the $x$ terms:
$$x' = a + (b - a) \cdot \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Step 9 (Special Case):** When $a = 0$ and $b = 1$ (the most common case for ML), the formula simplifies to:
$$x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Verification (Sanity Check):**
- $x = x_{\min} \Rightarrow x' = 0$ ✓
- $x = x_{\max} \Rightarrow x' = 1$ ✓
- $x = \frac{x_{\min} + x_{\max}}{2} \Rightarrow x' = 0.5$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-Score Standardization — Why It Yields Zero Mean and Unit Variance

We claim that if $x' = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, then the new mean is $0$ and the new variance is $1$.

**Step 1:** Compute the new mean $\mu'$:
$$\mu' = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x'_i$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x'_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$:
$$\mu' = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 3:** Pull the constant $\frac{1}{\sigma}$ outside the sum:
$$\mu' = \frac{1}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)$$

**Step 4:** Use the fundamental property of the mean: $\sum (x_i - \mu) = 0$:
$$\mu' = \frac{1}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{1}{N} \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Compute the new variance $\sigma'^2$:
$$\sigma'^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x'_i - \mu')^2$$

**Step 6:** Since $\mu' = 0$, this simplifies to:
$$\sigma'^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x'_i)^2$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $x'_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$:
$$\sigma'^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 8:** Pull the constant $\dfrac{1}{\sigma^2}$ outside:
$$\sigma'^2 = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 9:** Recognize that $\dfrac{1}{N} \sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sigma^2$:
$$\sigma'^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigorous Note:** The derivation above uses the **population variance** (dividing by $N$). In practice, ML libraries like scikit-learn use the **sample variance** (dividing by $N-1$), but the conceptual result — zero mean and unit variance — holds in both cases.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Min-Max Scaling

**Dataset (single feature $X$):** $\{2, 5, 8, 11, 14\}$

**Step 1:** Identify the minimum and maximum:
$$x_{\min} = 2, \quad x_{\max} = 14$$

**Step 2:** Compute the range:
$$x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 14 - 2 = 12$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Min-Max formula to each value:
- $x = 2 \Rightarrow x' = (2 - 2) / 12 = \mathbf{0.0000}$
- $x = 5 \Rightarrow x' = (5 - 2) / 12 = 3/12 = \mathbf{0.2500}$
- $x = 8 \Rightarrow x' = (8 - 2) / 12 = 6/12 = \mathbf{0.5000}$
- $x = 11 \Rightarrow x' = (11 - 2) / 12 = 9/12 = \mathbf{0.7500}$
- $x = 14 \Rightarrow x' = (14 - 2) / 12 = 12/12 = \mathbf{1.0000}$

**Step 4 (Verification):** Compute the new min and max:
- $x'_{\min} = 0$ ✓
- $x'_{\max} = 1$ ✓

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Z-Score Standardization

**Dataset (single feature $X$):** $\{2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9\}$

**Step 1:** Compute the mean:
$$\mu = \frac{2 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 5 + 5 + 7 + 9}{8} = \frac{40}{8} = 5$$

**Step 2:** Compute the variance (using $N$ for simplicity):
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{8} \sum (x_i - 5)^2$$

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \mu$ | $(x_i - \mu)^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | −3 | 9 |
| 4 | −1 | 1 |
| 4 | −1 | 1 |
| 4 | −1 | 1 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | 2 | 4 |
| 9 | 4 | 16 |
| **Sum** | | **32** |

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{32}{8} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = 2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Z-score formula to each value:
- $x = 2 \Rightarrow x' = (2 - 5) / 2 = \mathbf{-1.50}$
- $x = 4 \Rightarrow x' = (4 - 5) / 2 = \mathbf{-0.50}$
- $x = 4 \Rightarrow x' = \mathbf{-0.50}$
- $x = 4 \Rightarrow x' = \mathbf{-0.50}$
- $x = 5 \Rightarrow x' = (5 - 5) / 2 = \mathbf{0.00}$
- $x = 5 \Rightarrow x' = \mathbf{0.00}$
- $x = 7 \Rightarrow x' = (7 - 5) / 2 = \mathbf{+1.00}$
- $x = 9 \Rightarrow x' = (9 - 5) / 2 = \mathbf{+2.00}$

**Step 4 (Verification):** Compute new mean and variance:
$$\mu' = \frac{(-1.5) + 3 \cdot (-0.5) + 2 \cdot 0 + 1 + 2}{8} = \frac{-1.5 - 1.5 + 0 + 3}{8} = \frac{0}{8} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Custom Range Min-Max

Scale the original dataset $\{2, 5, 8, 11, 14\}$ to the range $[-1, +1]$.

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- $a = -1$, $b = +1$, $x_{\min} = 2$, $x_{\max} = 14$

**Step 2:** Apply the custom-range formula:
$$x' = a + (b - a) \cdot \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each transformed value:
- $x = 2 \Rightarrow x' = -1 + 2 \cdot (0/12) = \mathbf{-1.00}$
- $x = 5 \Rightarrow x' = -1 + 2 \cdot (3/12) = -1 + 0.5 = \mathbf{-0.50}$
- $x = 8 \Rightarrow x' = -1 + 2 \cdot (6/12) = -1 + 1.0 = \mathbf{0.00}$
- $x = 11 \Rightarrow x' = -1 + 2 \cdot (9/12) = -1 + 1.5 = \mathbf{+0.50}$
- $x = 14 \Rightarrow x' = -1 + 2 \cdot (12/12) = -1 + 2.0 = \mathbf{+1.00}$

**Verification:** Output range is $[-1, +1]$ ✓

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Min-Max Scaling** | **Z-Score Standardization** | **Robust Scaling** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bounding Box** | Fixed (e.g., $[0, 1]$) | Unbounded | Unbounded |
| **Outlier Resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Mean of Output** | Not necessarily 0 | Exactly **0** | Approximately 0 |
| **Std of Output** | Compressed to range | Exactly **1** | Approximately 1 |
| **Sparse Data Suitability** | Good (preserves 0) | Poor (shifts 0) | Poor (shifts 0) |
| **Distribution Assumption** | None | Approximately Normal | None |
| **Use with Neural Nets** | **Excellent** | Good | Good |
| **Use with Distance Algorithms** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Use with Tree Models** | Unnecessary | Unnecessary | Unnecessary |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Scaler

This diagram walks a student through the algorithmic decision process for selecting an appropriate scaling method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Feature Data] --> B{Is Data Sparse? TF-IDF, One-Hot?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Max Abs Scaling or L2 Normalization]
    B -- No --> D{Does Data Contain Significant Outliers?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Robust Scaling]
    D -- No --> F{Is Feature Distribution Highly Skewed?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Log or Power Transformation First]
    F -- No --> H{Need Bounded Output in 0 to 1 range?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Min-Max Scaling]
    H -- No --> J[Use Z-Score Standardization]
    C --> K[Pipeline Output]
    E --> K
    G --> H
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Scaling Pipeline Architecture

This block diagram shows the functional architecture of a typical ML data preprocessing pipeline with scaling as a central component.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Step 1: Handle Missing Values]
    B --> C[Step 2: Outlier Detection and Treatment]
    C --> D{Step 3: Scaling Required?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Step 4a: Fit Scaler on Training Data]
    E --> F[Step 4b: Transform Training Data]
    F --> G[Step 4c: Apply Same Scaler to Test Data]
    G --> H[Step 5: Scaled Data Ready for ML Model]
    D -- No --> H
    H --> I[ML Model Training: KNN, SVM, PCA, Neural Net]
    I --> J[Model Evaluation and Deployment]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Effect of Scaling on Data Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A1[Original Feature X]
        A2[Salary: 30000 to 200000]
    end
    subgraph B1[After Min-Max Scaling]
        B2[Salary: 0.0 to 1.0]
    end
    subgraph C1[After Z-Score Standardization]
        C2[Salary: mean 0, std 1, unbounded]
    end
    subgraph D1[After Robust Scaling]
        D2[Salary: median 0, IQR 1, outlier resistant]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> C1
    A1 --> D1
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Train/Test Scaling Protocol (Critical Pitfall)

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students scale the **entire dataset** (train + test combined) before splitting. This causes **data leakage** — the test set's statistics (mean, std, min, max) leak into the training process, producing an over-optimistic, invalid model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complete Dataset X] --> B[Step 1: Split into Train and Test]
    B --> C[Training Set X_train]
    B --> D[Test Set X_test]
    C --> E[Step 2: Fit Scaler on X_train only]
    E --> F[Compute mean, std OR min, max from X_train]
    F --> G[Step 3: Transform X_train using fitted scaler]
    G --> H[Step 4: Transform X_test using SAME fitted scaler]
    H --> I[Both sets now scaled with TRAIN statistics only]
    I --> J[Safely feed to ML Model]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule for KTU Answers:** Always state explicitly: *"The scaler is fit on training data only and then used to transform both training and test data, ensuring no data leakage."* This single sentence often earns 2 marks.
