## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Bayes Filter to Particle Filter (MCL)

We start from the recursive Bayes filter and show how the integral is replaced by a Monte Carlo sum.

**Step 1 — Assume the previous belief is represented by $N$ weighted samples:**

$$bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta\big(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]}\big)$$

Here, $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta, representing an impulse of "mass" at the particle's location. The expression says the *previous* belief is a discrete probability mass function with mass $\frac{w_{t-1}^{[i]}}{N}$ at each particle $x_{t-1}^{[i]}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the prediction integral:**

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta\big(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]}\big) \right] dx_{t-1}$$

Pull the summation outside the integral (linearity of integration):

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \delta\big(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]}\big) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sifting property of the Dirac delta:**

The sifting property states: $\int f(x) \, \delta(x - a) \, dx = f(a)$.

Applying this:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p\big(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t\big)$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result: sampling-based prediction.**

The integral is now a *sum of probability densities*. To convert this back to a particle representation, we draw $N$ new samples:

$$x_t^{[i]} \sim p\big(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t\big) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, N$$

The result is a *predicted* particle set with the *same* weights $w_{t-1}^{[i]}$.

**Step 5 — Apply the measurement update (Bayes' rule in multiplicative form):**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

Substitute the particle representation:

$$w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p\big(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}\big) \, w_{t-1}^{[i]}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the normalization constant $\eta$:**

By definition, weights must sum to 1. So:

$$\eta = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} p\big(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}\big) \, w_{t-1}^{[i]} \right]^{-1}$$

If we set $w_{t-1}^{[i]} = \frac{1}{N}$ (a common convention after resampling), this simplifies to:

$$\eta = \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} p\big(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}\big) \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Final weight update rule (MCL correction step):**

$$w_t^{[i]} = \frac{p\big(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}\big)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} p\big(z_t \mid x_t^{[j]}\big)}$$

This is the closed-form, KTU-blessed weight update equation for the standard MCL algorithm.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Likelihood Field Sensor Model (Beam Range Finder)

For a 2-D laser scan, the sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is typically the **likelihood field model** (Thrun, Burgard, Fox — Probabilistic Robotics). Let the scan contain $K$ beams with range $z_t^k$ and angle $\theta_t^k$ (in the robot frame).

**Step 1 — Transform beam endpoint to global coordinates:**

$$x_{z_t^k}^{\text{global}} = \begin{pmatrix} x_t + z_t^k \cos(\theta_t^k + \theta) \\ y_t + z_t^k \sin(\theta_t^k + \theta) \end{pmatrix}$$

This is a 2-D rotation + translation. $x_t, y_t, \theta$ are the robot's pose.

**Step 2 — Find the distance from the beam endpoint to the nearest obstacle in the map:**

$$d = \min_{x',y' \in \text{obstacles}} \sqrt{(x' - x_{z_t^k}^{\text{global},x})^2 + (y' - x_{z_t^k}^{\text{global},y})^2}$$

This is the *likelihood field* distance — typically pre-computed as a 2-D distance transform of the occupancy grid map.

**Step 3 — Compute the per-beam likelihood using a zero-mean Gaussian:**

$$p_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_{\text{hit}}^2}} \exp\!\left( - \frac{d^2}{2 \sigma_{\text{hit}}^2} \right)$$

The standard deviation $\sigma_{\text{hit}}$ is a hyperparameter, typically $\sigma_{\text{hit}} = \mathbf{0.2\,m}$ for indoor robots.

**Step 4 — Mix with random and max-range components to model spurious measurements:**

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{pmatrix} z_{\text{hit}} \\ z_{\text{short}} \\ z_{\text{max}} \\ z_{\text{rand}} \end{pmatrix}^{\!\top} \begin{pmatrix} p_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{\text{short}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{\text{max}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{\text{rand}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \end{pmatrix}$$

where $z_{\text{hit}} + z_{\text{short}} + z_{\text{max}} + z_{\text{rand}} = 1$ are mixing weights with $z_{\text{hit}}$ usually in $[0.85, 0.95]$.

**Step 5 — Combine the $K$ beams (independence assumption):**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 6 — Convert product to sum of log-likelihoods (numerical stability):**

$$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$

This is critical in practice, as direct product underflows IEEE 754 double precision when $K > 50$ and likelihoods are small.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Effective Sample Size (Resampling Trigger)

**Step 1 — Define the effective sample size (Kong-Liu-Del Moral, 1994):**

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$$

**Step 2 — Show that $1 \le N_{\text{eff}} \le N$:**

Using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality on the vector of weights (which sums to 1):

$$1 = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{[i]} \right)^2 \le N \sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{[i]})^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{\text{eff}} \le N$$

Equality holds only when all weights are equal, i.e., $w_t^{[i]} = \frac{1}{N}$.

**Step 3 — Trigger resampling when $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls below a threshold:**

$$\text{Resample if} \quad N_{\text{eff}} < \frac{N}{2}$$

This is the **standard KLD-resampling trigger** used in adaptive MCL (Fox, 2003).

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Frequently Asked Style)

A 1-D robot operates on a line. There are 3 landmarks at $L_1 = 2\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 5\,\texttext{m}$, $L_3 = 9\,\text{m}$. The robot uses $N = 4$ particles. At $t-1$, the weights are $w_{t-1} = (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4)$ and the particle poses are $x_{t-1} = (1.0, 3.0, 6.0, 8.0)$. The robot observes landmark $L_2$ at range $z_t = 2.0\,\text{m}$ with sensor noise $\sigma = 0.5\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the likelihood for each particle:**

$$w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left( - \frac{(z_t - \vert x_{t-1}^{[i]} - L_2 \vert)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

| $i$ | $x_{t-1}^{[i]}$ | $\vert x_{t-1}^{[i]} - L_2 \vert$ | $(z_t - \text{above})$ | $\exp(\cdots)$ | $w_t^{[i]}$ |
|-----|-----------------|----------------------------------|------------------------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | 1.0 | 4.0 | $-2.0$ | $e^{-8} \approx 0.000335$ | $0.1 \cdot 0.000335 = 0.0000335$ |
| 2 | 3.0 | 2.0 | $0.0$ | $e^{0} = 1.0$ | $0.2 \cdot 1.0 = 0.2$ |
| 3 | 6.0 | 1.0 | $1.0$ | $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$ | $0.3 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.0406$ |
| 4 | 8.0 | 3.0 | $-1.0$ | $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$ | $0.4 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.0541$ |

**Step 2 — Sum the weights and normalize:**

$$S = 0.0000335 + 0.2 + 0.0406 + 0.0541 = 0.2947$$

$$w_t = \left( \frac{0.0000335}{0.2947}, \frac{0.2}{0.2947}, \frac{0.0406}{0.2947}, \frac{0.0541}{0.2947} \right) = (0.000114, 0.679, 0.138, 0.184)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective sample size:**

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{(0.000114)^2 + (0.679)^2 + (0.138)^2 + (0.184)^2} = \frac{1}{0.5169} \approx 1.93$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since $N_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.93 < \frac{4}{2} = 2$, **resampling is triggered**.

**Step 5 — Stochastic Universal Resampling (SUR) of 4 particles proportional to weights:**

Cumulative weights: $0.000114, 0.679, 0.818, 1.000$.

Draw 4 uniform samples $u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4 \in [0, 0.25)$ starting at $u_0 \sim U(0, 0.25)$. The new particle set will be dominated by particle #2 (weight 0.679) and the other surviving particles.

This example illustrates the **KTU-typical "compute weights and decide to resample"** problem structure that appears in the ESE.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the MCL Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Map m, Initial Pose Distribution] --> B[Initialize Particle Set X0]
    B --> C{For each time step t}
    C --> D[Receive Odometry u_t]
    D --> E[Propagate Particles via Motion Model]
    E --> F[Receive Sensor Scan z_t]
    F --> G[Compute Importance Weights w_t via Sensor Model]
    G --> H[Normalize Weights w_t sums to 1]
    H --> I[Compute N_eff]
    I --> J{N_eff less than N by 2?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Resample Particles with Replacement]
    J -- No --> L[Keep Current Particle Set]
    K --> M[Output Estimated Pose hat x_t]
    L --> M
    M --> N{More Time Steps?}
    N -- Yes --> C
    N -- No --> O[End of Localization]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of MCL Local-Global-Kidnapped States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State A: Global Localization\n Particles Spread Across Map] --> S2[State B: Position Tracking\n Particles Clustered Around True Pose]
    S2 --> S3[State C: Kidnapped Robot\n Sudden Jump in Pose]
    S3 --> S1
    S1 -.->|If Compass Reading Matches| S2
    S2 -.->|If Random Sensor Spike| S3
    S3 -.->|If Re-initialization Triggered| S1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of the Sensor Model Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Robot Pose x_t, y_t, theta_t] --> T[Transform Beam Endpoints to Global Frame]
    T --> D[Lookup Likelihood Field Distance d]
    D --> G[Compute Gaussian Hit Probability p_hit]
    G --> MX[Mix with Short, Max, Random Components]
    MX --> P[Final Beam Likelihood p of z_t given x_t]
    P --> WT[Multiply Weights for All K Beams]
    WT --> W[Particle Importance Weight w_t]
```

### 4.4 Lifecycle of a Single Particle Across Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Particle at time t-1 with weight w t-1] --> P2[Apply Motion Model: x_t sampled from p of x_t given x t-1, u_t]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Weight: w t proportional to p of z_t given x_t]
    P3 --> P4{Is weight above survival threshold?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Particle Survives with probability w t]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Particle Eliminated]
    P5 --> P7[Carried forward to time t]
    P6 --> P8[Replaced by Duplicated Survivor]
    P8 --> P7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In a real MCL implementation, the entire particle set $\mathcal{X}_t$ is processed in parallel (vectorized) on a CPU or GPU. The above diagrams represent the *logical* flow per particle, but a single chip processes thousands of particles per second. KTU examiners appreciate seeing the parallel nature mentioned explicitly in viva-voce.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Monte Carlo Localization. State any two advantages of MCL over Kalman Filter based localization.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) is a *sample-based recursive Bayesian localization algorithm* in which the posterior belief over the robot's pose $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is represented by a finite set of $N$ weighted particles $\mathcal{X}_t = \{ x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]} \}_{i=1}^{N}$. The algorithm alternates between three phases: motion update (sampling), measurement update (importance weighting), and resampling.

**Advantages over Kalman Filter (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. MCL can represent **multi-modal** (non-Gaussian) belief distributions, whereas the EKF (Extended Kalman Filter) is restricted to a single Gaussian and thus fails in global localization when the robot has multiple equally plausible locations.
2. MCL makes **weaker assumptions** about the noise model (does not require Gaussianity of the process and measurement noise), enabling it to operate robustly with heavy-tailed, non-linear, or discontinuous sensor models.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common 1-mark loss occurs when students confuse MCL with the *Histogram Filter* or the *Grid-based localization*. MCL uses **continuous random samples**, while the histogram filter uses a **discretized grid**. Do not interchange these terms.

#### **Q2. What is the role of resampling in the Monte Carlo Localization algorithm? What is the "particle deprivation" problem?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of Resampling (2 marks):** Resampling is the third phase of MCL, in which $N$ new particles are drawn with replacement from the current particle set, where each particle $i$ is selected with probability equal to its importance weight $w_t^{[i]}$. It is mathematically described as: $P(i \text{ is drawn}) = w_t^{[i]}$. After resampling, all weights are reset to $\frac{1}{N}$, and the particle set again approximates the true posterior — but with computational effort concentrated on high-likelihood regions.

**Particle Deprivation (1 mark):** *Particle deprivation* is the failure mode in which all but one of the surviving particles have negligible weight after a few iterations. Without resampling, weight variance grows unboundedly, and the particle set becomes an inefficient approximation (one particle has weight $\approx 1$, all others $\approx 0$). Resampling prevents this by stochastically pruning low-weight particles.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Derive the recursive weight update equation for Monte Carlo Localization, starting from the Bayes filter recursion. Show clearly how the integral is replaced by a Monte Carlo sum. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the likelihood field sensor model used in MCL. Derive the per-beam likelihood function and explain why the log-likelihood formulation is preferred numerically. (7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Statement of Bayes filter (1 mark):** Start with the two-step recursion:
   $$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$
   $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$
2. **Particle representation of prior (1 mark):** Approximate $bel(x_{t-1})$ using $N$ particles:
   $$bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$
3. **Substitution and linearity (1 mark):** Substitute into the prediction integral and pull the sum outside:
   $$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$
4. **Sampling interpretation (1 mark):** Generate $N$ new particle poses:
   $$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$
5. **Apply measurement update (1 mark):**
   $$w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \, w_{t-1}^{[i]}$$
6. **Normalization (1 mark):** Compute $\eta$ such that weights sum to 1:
   $$\eta = \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{N} p(z_t \mid x_t^{[j]}) \, w_{t-1}^{[j]} \right]^{-1}$$
7. **Final compact form (1 mark):** With $w_{t-1}^{[i]} = \frac{1}{N}$ after resampling:
   $$w_t^{[i]} = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} p(z_t \mid x_t^{[j]})}$$

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Define inputs (0.5 mark):** Robot pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$, scan $z_t = \{z_t^k\}_{k=1}^{K}$, map $m$.
2. **Transform beam endpoint to global frame (1 mark):**
   $$x_{z,k} = \begin{pmatrix} x + z_t^k \cos(\theta_t^k + \theta) \\ y + z_t^k \sin(\theta_t^k + \theta) \end{pmatrix}$$
3. **Likelihood field distance (1 mark):** Pre-compute the distance transform of the map and read off $d = \text{dist}(x_{z,k}, m)$.
4. **Per-beam Gaussian (1 mark):**
   $$p_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_{\text{hit}}^2}} \exp\!\left( - \frac{d^2}{2\sigma_{\text{hit}}^2} \right)$$
5. **Mixture model (1 mark):** Add components for short, max-range, and random measurements to handle sensor artifacts.
6. **Combine beams (1 mark):** Independence assumption gives:
   $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$
7. **Log-likelihood justification (1.5 marks):** Direct product of small numbers (e.g., $0.01^{50} = 10^{-100}$) underflows IEEE 754 double precision (smallest positive normal $\approx 2.2 \times 10^{-308}$). Therefore,
   $$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$$
   is computed instead. The maximum is unaffected by the monotone log transformation.

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain the three phases of the Monte Carlo Localization algorithm with a suitable block diagram. How does MCL handle the kidnapped robot problem? (7 marks)**

**(b) What is KLD-sampling in adaptive MCL? Derive the bound on the number of particles $N$ in terms of the Kullback-Leibler divergence $\epsilon$ and the failure probability $\delta$. (7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Phase 1 — Initialization (1 mark):** For *global localization*, particles are uniformly sampled from the free space of the map $m$. For *position tracking*, particles are initialized as a tight Gaussian around an approximate initial pose. Each particle has weight $w_0^{[i]} = \frac{1}{N}$.
2. **Phase 2 — Motion Update (1 mark):** For each particle, apply the motion model:
   $$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$
   This reflects the change in pose due to control input $u_t$ plus process noise.
3. **Phase 3 — Measurement Update (1 mark):** Compute importance weights:
   $$w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$
   Normalize so $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$.
4. **Phase 4 — Resampling (1 mark):** Draw $N$ new particles with probability $w_t^{[i]}$ and reset weights to $\frac{1}{N}$.
5. **Block diagram (1 mark):** A flowchart with the four steps (drawn in the answer script).
6. **Kidnapped robot problem (1 mark):** Sudden teleportation of the robot to a far location, causing the particle cloud to be entirely incorrect. MCL recovers by **injecting random particles** uniformly into the map at each time step, with injection rate proportional to the average sensor likelihood (a low likelihood indicates possible kidnapping).
7. **Concluding remark (1 mark):** MCL handles all three localization states (global, tracking, kidnapped) in a unified probabilistic framework — a key advantage over EKF.

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Motivation (1 mark):** Standard MCL uses a fixed $N$. In open spaces, $N$ should be small (saves compute); in cluttered spaces (high belief entropy), $N$ should be large (avoids particle deprivation). *KLD-sampling* (Fox, 2003) adapts $N$ online.
2. **Kullback-Leibler divergence (1 mark):** Measures the distance between the sample-based estimate $\hat{p}$ and the true belief $p$:
   $$K(\hat{p}, p) = \sum_{x} \hat{p}(x) \log \frac{\hat{p}(x)}{p(x)}$$
3. **Binning the state space (1 mark):** Discretize the state space into $k$ bins. The chi-square statistic is:
   $$\chi^2 = 2 N \, K(\hat{p}, p) \approx 2 N \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left( \frac{n_j}{N} - p_j \right)^2 \frac{1}{p_j}$$
4. **Chernoff bound (1 mark):** The probability that $K(\hat{p}, p) > \epsilon$ is bounded:
   $$P(K(\hat{p}, p) > \epsilon) \le \frac{1}{2} e^{-2 N \epsilon}$$
5. **Set failure probability to $\delta$ (1 mark):**
   $$\frac{1}{2} e^{-2 N \epsilon} \le \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \ge \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \ln\!\left( \frac{1}{2\delta} \right)$$
6. **Bounded particle count formula (1 mark):** With $k$ populated bins, the bound becomes:
   $$N \le \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$$
   where $\chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$ is the upper $1-\delta$ quantile of a chi-square distribution with $k-1$ degrees of freedom.
7. **Adaptive algorithm summary (1 mark):** After each resampling, compute $k$ (number of non-empty bins). If $N < \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$, draw more particles; otherwise stop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Do not confuse $bel(x_t)$ and $\overline{bel}(x_t)$:** The overbar denotes the *predicted* belief *before* the measurement update. Examiners explicitly check this distinction. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Do not forget the $\eta$ normalization:** When writing the weight update, $\eta$ is mandatory. Forgetting it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **For the KLD-sampling question, write the chi-square quantile correctly.** A common error is to write $\chi^2_{1-\delta, k-1}$ (parameters swapped). The correct order is *degrees of freedom first, then probability quantile*: $\chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$.
> 4. **For the sensor model question, explicitly mention the log-likelihood transformation.** KTU examiners value numerical stability as a key engineering consideration — omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MCL is a particle filter** for mobile robot localization in a known map $m$.
- The belief is **approximated by $N$ weighted samples** $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}$.
- **Three core phases per time step:** (1) Motion update, (2) Measurement update, (3) Resampling.
- The **motion model** is $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ (often the *odometry motion model* with Gaussian noise).
- The **measurement model** is $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ (often a *beam-based* or *likelihood-field* model for range finders).
- The **weight update** is $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \, w_{t-1}^{[i]}$, with $\eta$ ensuring $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$.
- **Resampling** draws $N$ new particles with probability $w_t^{[i]}$; weights are then reset to $\frac{1}{N}$.
- **Effective sample size** $N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ triggers resampling when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$.
- MCL handles **global localization**, **position tracking**, and **kidnapped robot** scenarios in one framework.
- **KLD-sampling** adaptively chooses $N$ via the bound $N \le \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \chi^2_{k-1, 1-\delta}$.
- The **kidnapped robot** is recovered by injecting random particles into the map at each step.
- The **likelihood field** model is preferred over the beam-range model for computational efficiency (no ray casting).
- Use **log-likelihoods** to avoid numerical underflow when $K > 50$ beams.
- **Mean pose estimate:** $\hat{x}_t = \sum_i w_t^{[i]} \, x_t^{[i]}$.
- **MCL is non-parametric** — it can represent any belief shape, unlike the unimodal EKF.
- **KTU marker words to include in answers:** "Bayes filter", "Dirac delta approximation", "importance sampling", "sensor model", "resampling", "particle deprivation", "effective sample size", "KLD-sampling", "kidnapped robot".
- The MCL algorithm was popularized in the textbook *Probabilistic Robotics* (Thrun, Burgard, Fox). The MIT Killian Court dataset is the canonical experimental benchmark.
- **Common viva question:** "Why doesn't MCL use rejection sampling on the prior?" — *Answer: rejection sampling is inefficient in high-dimensional state spaces; importance sampling weights allow a richer distribution.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayes Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bayes Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bayes' Rule** (also called **Bayes' Theorem** or **Bayes' Law**) is a fundamental theorem in probability theory that describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis $H$ when new evidence $E$ is observed. Formally, it provides a mathematical mechanism for reversing conditional probabilities, allowing inference from $P(E \mid H)$ to $P(H \mid E)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Bayes' Rule is a probabilistic inference framework that computes the **posterior probability** $P(H \mid E)$ of a hypothesis $H$ given observed evidence $E$, by combining the **prior probability** $P(H)$, the **likelihood** $P(E \mid H)$, and the **evidence** (or marginal probability) $P(E)$.

Mathematically, for two events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

This identity is the cornerstone of **Bayesian Inference**, **Naive Bayes classifiers**, **Bayesian Belief Networks (BBN)**, and **probabilistic reasoning** in modern AI/ML systems.

---

### 1.2 The Four Building Blocks of Bayes' Rule

| # | Term | Notation | Real-World Meaning |
|---|------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Prior Probability** | $P(A)$ | Initial belief *before* observing evidence |
| 2 | **Likelihood** | $P(B \mid A)$ | Probability of evidence, given hypothesis is true |
| 3 | **Posterior Probability** | $P(A \mid B)$ | Updated belief *after* observing evidence |
| 4 | **Evidence (Marginal)** | $P(B)$ | Total probability of observing the evidence |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Medical Test Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: COVID-19 Rapid Testing**
>
> Imagine a person takes a COVID rapid test and gets a **positive** result. The patient panics, but a doctor (reasoning like a Bayesian agent) asks:
> *"Before seeing the result, what was the chance of having the disease?"* — this is the **Prior** $P(\text{Disease})$.
> *"If the patient actually has the disease, how likely is the test positive?"* — this is the **Likelihood** $P(+\mid \text{Disease})$.
> *"How likely is a positive result overall, in the entire population?"* — this is the **Evidence** $P(+)$.
>
> Bayes' Rule then combines these to give a calibrated **Posterior** — the *true* probability of having the disease after a positive test. This often surprises people: a test with 99% accuracy, in a low-prevalence population, may yield a posterior of only ~16%.

This reasoning pattern — **belief + new evidence → updated belief** — is the central paradigm of rational AI agents, expert systems, and statistical learning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Bayes' Rule

Bayes' Rule emerges naturally from the **symmetry of joint probability**. The probability that *both* $A$ and $B$ occur can be written in two equivalent ways:

1. First $A$, then $B$ given $A$ → $P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$
2. First $B$, then $A$ given $B$ → $P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$

Since both expressions equal $P(A \cap B)$, equating them yields Bayes' Rule.

---

### 2.2 Pre-Requisite: Conditional Probability

The probability of event $A$ given that event $B$ has occurred:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0
$$

Rearranging gives the **Product Rule** (chain rule of probability):

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Flow

- **Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability** for $P(A \mid B)$.
- **Step 2 — Substitute the joint probability** using the product rule.
- **Step 3 — Equate the two product-rule forms** of the joint probability.
- **Step 4 — Isolate $P(A \mid B)$** on one side of the equation.
- **Step 5 — Result** is the canonical Bayes' Rule formula.

---

### 2.4 Generalised Bayes' Rule (Multi-Hypothesis / Partition Form)

When the sample space is partitioned into mutually exclusive and exhaustive events $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$, and $B$ is an event with $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}
$$

Here, the denominator is the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Most numerical problems in the End-Semester Exam (ESE) test the **partition form** rather than the two-event form. Always check whether the problem asks for one of several mutually exclusive causes.

---

### 2.5 Bayes' Rule with Independence Assumption

If $A$ and $B$ are statistically independent, then $P(B \mid A) = P(B)$, and Bayes' Rule reduces to $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ — i.e., observing $B$ provides no information about $A$. This degenerate case is the *baseline* against which informative evidence is measured.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| # | Formula | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Product (Chain) Rule | Joint from conditionals |
| 2 | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Bayes' Rule (Two-Event) | Reverse a single conditional |
| 3 | $P(B) = \displaystyle\sum_{j} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)$ | Law of Total Probability | Find marginal $P(B)$ from partitions |
| 4 | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}$ | Bayes' Rule (Multi-Hypothesis) | Choose among $n$ causes |
| 5 | $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ | Independence Degenerate Case | When $A \perp B$ |
| 6 | $P(A \mid B, C) = \dfrac{P(C \mid A, B) \cdot P(A \mid B)}{P(C \mid B)}$ | Conditional Bayes' Rule | Sequential evidence updates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All probabilities in Bayes' Rule are bounded in the closed interval $[0, 1]$. The four components must be **dimensionless** (pure numbers) and the partition $\{A_j\}$ must be **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE)** for the partition form to be valid.

---

### 2.7 Engineering & AI Real-World Utility

- **Naive Bayes Classifier:** Text classification (spam detection, sentiment analysis).
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Expert-system inference engines in clinical decision support.
- **Bayesian Networks:** Probabilistic graphical models for reasoning under uncertainty.
- **Speech & Vision Recognition:** HMMs and CRF-based decoding rely on Bayesian posteriors.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter tuning.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Sensor fusion updates posterior beliefs about obstacles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bayes' Rule from First Principles

**Goal:** Prove $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$.

**Step 1:** Write the definition of conditional probability for event $A$ given $B$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

This is a direct consequence of restricting the sample space to outcomes where $B$ has occurred.

**Step 2:** Similarly, write the conditional probability of $B$ given $A$:

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange Step 2 to solve for the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

This is the **product rule** — the joint equals the prior of $A$ times the likelihood of $B$ given $A$.

**Step 4:** Substitute this expression for the joint probability into the Step 1 equation:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this as the canonical **Bayes' Rule**. The denominator $P(B)$ is the *normalizing constant* ensuring the posterior is a valid probability distribution summing to 1 over all hypotheses.

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation is symmetric — one could equally start with $P(B \mid A)$ and arrive at an expression for it. Bayes' Rule is therefore a **direct consequence of the commutativity of joint probability**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Medical Diagnosis (Two-Event Form)

> **Problem [KTU-style]:** A disease affects **1%** of a population. A diagnostic test for the disease has:
> - **Sensitivity** $P(+\mid D) = 0.99$ (true positive rate)
> - **Specificity** $P(-\mid \neg D) = 0.95$ (true negative rate)
>
> A randomly selected person tests **positive**. What is the probability that they actually have the disease?

**Given:**
- $P(D) = 0.01$ (prior)
- $P(\neg D) = 0.99$
- $P(+\mid D) = 0.99$
- $P(+\mid \neg D) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Compute the evidence $P(+)$** using the Law of Total Probability:

$$
P(+) = P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(+\mid \neg D) \cdot P(\neg D)
$$

$$
P(+) = (0.99)(0.01) + (0.05)(0.99)
$$

$$
P(+) = 0.0099 + 0.0495 = 0.0594
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule** to obtain the posterior:

$$
P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+)}
$$

$$
P(D \mid +) = \frac{(0.99)(0.01)}{0.0594}
$$

$$
P(D \mid +) = \frac{0.0099}{0.0594} \approx 0.1667
$$

**Result:** $P(D \mid +) \approx \mathbf{16.67\%}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:** Even with a 99% accurate test, a positive result in a low-prevalence population only means a ~17% chance of actually having the disease. This is the famous **"base-rate fallacy"** that Bayes' Rule corrects.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Three-Urn Partition (Multi-Hypothesis Form)

> **Problem [KTU-style]:** Three urns $U_1, U_2, U_3$ contain red and black balls as follows:
> - $U_1$: 2 Red, 3 Black (chosen with prob. $\tfrac{1}{2}$)
> - $U_2$: 4 Red, 1 Black (chosen with prob. $\tfrac{1}{4}$)
> - $U_3$: 1 Red, 4 Black (chosen with prob. $\tfrac{1}{4}$)
>
> A ball drawn at random from the selected urn is **Red**. Find the probability it came from $U_2$.

**Step 1 — Compute likelihoods** $P(R \mid U_i)$:

$$
P(R \mid U_1) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
$$

$$
P(R \mid U_2) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80
$$

$$
P(R \mid U_3) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the partition form of Bayes' Rule:**

$$
P(U_2 \mid R) = \frac{P(R \mid U_2) \cdot P(U_2)}{\sum_{i=1}^{3} P(R \mid U_i) \cdot P(U_i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = (0.80) \cdot (0.25) = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator** (Total Probability of Red):

$$
P(R) = (0.40)(0.50) + (0.80)(0.25) + (0.20)(0.25)
$$

$$
P(R) = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.45
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
P(U_2 \mid R) = \frac{0.20}{0.45} = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444
$$

**Result:** $P(U_2 \mid R) \approx \mathbf{44.44\%}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Naive Bayes (Text Classification Preview)

> **Problem:** In a spam filter, the word "offer" appears in **70%** of spam emails and **10%** of legitimate emails. Suppose **40%** of incoming emails are spam. An email contains the word "offer". What is the probability it is spam?

**Step 1 — Write down given probabilities:**

- $P(\text{Spam}) = 0.40$, $P(\text{Ham}) = 0.60$
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.70$
- $P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Ham}) = 0.10$

**Step 2 — Compute evidence** $P(\text{offer})$:

$$
P(\text{offer}) = (0.70)(0.40) + (0.10)(0.60) = 0.28 + 0.06 = 0.34
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Rule:**

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{offer}) = \frac{(0.70)(0.40)}{0.34} = \frac{0.28}{0.34} \approx 0.8235
$$

**Result:** $P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{offer}) \approx \mathbf{82.35\%}$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Form

| Scenario | Form to Use | Reason |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Two events, simple $P(A \mid B)$ | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Direct substitution |
| Multiple mutually exclusive causes | Partition form | Marginal $P(B)$ is sum over causes |
| Sequential / chained evidence | Conditional Bayes' Rule | Update belief over time |
| Spam/sentiment with multiple words | Naive Bayes (independent features) | Product of likelihoods in numerator |
| Network of random variables | Joint posterior via chain rule | Bayes' Ball / d-separation analysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bayesian Inference Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the **flow of probabilistic information** through a Bayesian update, from prior knowledge to posterior belief.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prior Belief P of A] --> C[Multiply with Likelihood P of B given A]
    B[Observed Evidence B] --> C
    C --> D[Joint Probability P of A intersect B]
    D --> E[Normalize by Evidence P of B]
    F[Law of Total Probability over partitions] --> E
    E --> G[Posterior Belief P of A given B]
    G --> H{Decision or Action}
    H --> I[Classify Predict Diagnose]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow moves left-to-right, beginning with the *a priori* belief $P(A)$. Evidence $B$ is observed, the joint probability is formed, the evidence is normalized out using the Law of Total Probability, and the *a posteriori* belief $P(A \mid B)$ drives a downstream AI decision.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of Bayes' Rule (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        P1[Prior P of A]
        P2[Likelihood P of B given A]
        P3[Evidence P of B]
    end
    subgraph Engine
        M1[Numerator: Prior times Likelihood]
        M2[Denominator: Total Probability]
        M3[Bayes Quotient]
    end
    subgraph Output
        O1[Posterior P of A given B]
        O2[Updated Belief for AI Agent]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    P2 --> M2
    P3 --> M2
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for ESE:** The **Numerator** captures the *joint* evidence-hypothesis co-occurrence, while the **Denominator** acts as a *normalizer* ensuring the posterior is a valid probability mass function. This separation is the standard "Bayesian Engine" architecture used in expert systems and probabilistic programming languages like PyMC and Stan.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Bayesian Belief Update (Iterative / Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Initial Prior P of H] --> S2[Observe Evidence E1]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Posterior P of H given E1]
    S3 --> S4[New Prior becomes P of H given E1]
    S4 --> S5[Observe Evidence E2]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Updated Posterior P of H given E1 and E2]
    S6 --> S7[Convergence or Decision Threshold]
    S7 --> S8[Final Belief State]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential Bayesian Updating** is the foundation of **online learning**, **Kalman filters**, and **recursive state estimation** in robotics. Each new evidence converts the previous posterior into the next prior.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**State Bayes' Theorem and explain the role of the prior and posterior probabilities in machine learning.**

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' Theorem states that for two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

- **Prior $P(A)$:** Initial degree of belief about hypothesis $A$ *before* observing evidence $B$. In ML, it reflects *base rates* in the training data.
- **Posterior $P(A \mid B)$:** Updated belief about $A$ *after* observing evidence $B$. In ML, it is the *predicted class probability* in algorithms like Naive Bayes.
- Bayes' Rule allows a learning system to **incorporate new evidence incrementally**, making it the mathematical foundation of probabilistic learning, expert systems, and Bayesian neural networks.

*(3 marks — Award: 1 mark formula, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for ML interpretation.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Differentiate between prior probability and likelihood in Bayes' Rule with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Prior $P(A)$ | Likelihood $P(B \mid A)$ |
|--------|--------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | Belief in $A$ before seeing $B$ | Probability of $B$ if $A$ is true |
| Role in Bayes | Multiplied into numerator | Multiplied into numerator |
| ML context | Class prior from training set | Probability of features given class |

**Example:** In spam detection, $P(\text{Spam}) = 0.40$ is the *prior*, while $P(\text{``offer"} \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.70$ is the *likelihood*. Bayes' Rule combines them to give $P(\text{Spam} \mid \text{``offer"}) \approx 0.82$, the *posterior*.

*(3 marks — Award: 1 mark distinction, 1 mark formula linking, 1 mark example.)*

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Full ESE Internal Choice Format)**

> Each Part B question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B** as per KTU ESE choice pattern.

---

### **OPTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

#### **Q. (a) Derive Bayes' Theorem from the definition of conditional probability. State all assumptions used in the derivation. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conditional Probability Definition:**

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0
$$

$$
P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}, \quad P(A) > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Joint Probability** $P(A \cap B)$:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** into the first equation:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the four components:**

- $P(A \mid B)$ — Posterior
- $P(B \mid A)$ — Likelihood
- $P(A)$ — Prior
- $P(B)$ — Evidence (Normalizing Constant)

**Assumptions Used:**

1. $0 \le P(\cdot) \le 1$ for all events.
2. $P(B) > 0$ (conditioning event has positive probability).
3. Joint probability is well-defined and finite.
4. The events belong to a Kolmogorov probability space (sample space $\Omega$, $\sigma$-algebra, probability measure $P$).

**Step 5 — State the Generalised Form** for $n$ mutually exclusive, exhaustive hypotheses $\{A_1, \dots, A_n\}$:

$$
P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Bayes' Theorem is a direct algebraic consequence of the definition of conditional probability and the symmetry of joint probability. *(7 marks — Award: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for assumptions, 2 marks for generalised form.)*

---

#### **Q. (b) A factory has three machines $M_1, M_2, M_3$ producing 25%, 35%, and 40% of the total output respectively. The defect rates are 2%, 3%, and 1% respectively. If a randomly selected item is defective, find the probability that it came from $M_2$. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the Given Data:**

| Machine | Prior $P(M_i)$ | Defect Rate $P(D \mid M_i)$ |
|---------|----------------|------------------------------|
| $M_1$ | 0.25 | 0.02 |
| $M_2$ | 0.35 | 0.03 |
| $M_3$ | 0.40 | 0.01 |

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule (Partition Form):**

$$
P(M_2 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid M_2) \cdot P(M_2)}{\sum_{i=1}^{3} P(D \mid M_i) \cdot P(M_i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = (0.03)(0.35) = 0.0105
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Denominator** (Total Probability of Defect):

$$
P(D) = (0.02)(0.25) + (0.03)(0.35) + (0.01)(0.40)
$$

$$
P(D) = 0.0050 + 0.0105 + 0.0040 = 0.0195
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
P(M_2 \mid D) = \frac{0.0105}{0.0195} = \frac{105}{195} = \frac{7}{13} \approx 0.5385
$$

**Result:** $P(M_2 \mid D) \approx \mathbf{53.85\%}$.

**Conclusion:** Despite $M_2$ producing only 35% of the output, it accounts for ~54% of all defective items because of its higher defect rate.

*(7 marks — Award: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for denominator calculation, 1 mark for numerator, 1 mark for division, 1 mark for final answer with units/percentage.)*

---

### **OPTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

#### **Q. (a) Explain the concept of conditional independence. How does the Naive Bayes classifier use Bayes' Rule along with the conditional independence assumption to perform classification? (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Conditional Independence:**

Two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are **conditionally independent** given $Z$ if:

$$
P(X, Y \mid Z) = P(X \mid Z) \cdot P(Y \mid Z)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
P(X \mid Y, Z) = P(X \mid Z)
$$

That is, once $Z$ is known, $Y$ provides **no additional information** about $X$.

**Step 2 — Naive Bayes Assumption:**

Given class label $C$ and feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, Naive Bayes assumes all features are conditionally independent given the class:

$$
P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

This is the *naive* (often unrealistic) assumption that makes the computation tractable.

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Rule** for classification:

$$
P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across classes, classification reduces to:

$$
\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C} \left[ P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Justification:**

Despite the strong independence assumption, Naive Bayes works surprisingly well in:
- Text classification (spam, sentiment)
- Document categorization
- Medical diagnosis
- Real-time prediction tasks

**Step 6 — Example:** For spam classification with words $w_1, w_2, w_3$:

$$
P(\text{Spam} \mid w_1, w_2, w_3) \propto P(\text{Spam}) \cdot P(w_1 \mid \text{Spam}) \cdot P(w_2 \mid \text{Spam}) \cdot P(w_3 \mid \text{Spam})
$$

**Conclusion:** Conditional independence converts an intractable joint estimation problem into a tractable product of one-dimensional likelihoods, making Naive Bayes one of the most efficient and widely used probabilistic classifiers in ML.

*(7 marks — Award: 2 marks for conditional independence definition, 2 marks for Naive Bayes assumption, 2 marks for Bayes' application, 1 mark for example.)*

---

#### **Q. (b) A blood test for a rare disease has 95% sensitivity and 90% specificity. The disease prevalence in the population is 2%. If a person tests positive, what is the probability that they actually have the disease? Use Bayes' Theorem. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Probabilities:**

- Prevalence (Prior): $P(D) = 0.02$, so $P(\neg D) = 0.98$
- Sensitivity: $P(+\mid D) = 0.95$
- Specificity: $P(-\mid \neg D) = 0.90$, so $P(+\mid \neg D) = 0.10$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Evidence** using the Law of Total Probability:

$$
P(+) = P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(+\mid \neg D) \cdot P(\neg D)
$$

$$
P(+) = (0.95)(0.02) + (0.10)(0.98)
$$

$$
P(+) = 0.0190 + 0.0980 = 0.1170
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Posterior:**

$$
P(D \mid +) = \frac{(0.95)(0.02)}{0.1170} = \frac{0.0190}{0.1170} \approx 0.1624
$$

**Result:** $P(D \mid +) \approx \mathbf{16.24\%}$.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Even with a 95% sensitive and 90% specific test, a positive result in this population only implies a ~16% chance of actually having the disease. This illustrates the **base-rate fallacy**: low prevalence dramatically reduces the positive predictive value (PPV) of any test.

**Step 6 — General Formula for PPV:**

$$
\text{PPV} = \frac{\text{Sensitivity} \times \text{Prevalence}}{\text{Sensitivity} \times \text{Prevalence} + (1 - \text{Specificity}) \times (1 - \text{Prevalence})}
$$

**Conclusion:** Bayes' Theorem provides a rigorous, quantitative basis for medical decision-making, demonstrating that test accuracy alone is insufficient — **disease prevalence critically determines the diagnostic value of a positive result**.

*(7 marks — Award: 2 marks for given data, 2 marks for evidence calculation, 2 marks for posterior calculation, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Law of Total Probability** in the denominator when the problem involves multiple causes — the most common single mistake in ESE.
> 2. **Confusing $P(A \mid B)$ with $P(B \mid A)$** — the entire purpose of Bayes' Rule is to *reverse* the conditioning; examiners actively look for this reversal.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption $P(B) > 0$** when writing the formula — a full 1-mark deduction is typical.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in the denominator** — always cross-check by summing the partition probabilities, which should equal **1**.
> 5. **Omitting the "interpretation step"** in numerical problems — KTU 2024 scheme rewards the *qualitative conclusion* drawn from a numerical posterior, e.g., "the test is not very reliable due to low prevalence."
> 6. **Using the wrong form** — the two-event form cannot be applied directly to a three-urn problem; students must use the partition form.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU ESE):**

- **Bayes' Rule** reverses conditional probabilities: $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$.
- The **four components** are: **Prior**, **Likelihood**, **Posterior**, **Evidence**.
- The **denominator $P(B)$** in the multi-hypothesis form is computed via the **Law of Total Probability**.
- The partition $\{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$ must be **mutually exclusive and exhaustive (MECE)**.
- The **prior $P(A)$** is the initial belief; the **posterior $P(A \mid B)$** is the *updated* belief after evidence.
- If $A$ and $B$ are **independent**, $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ — Bayes' Rule degenerates to the prior.
- **Naive Bayes classifier** = Bayes' Rule + conditional independence assumption over features.
- **Posteriori $\propto$ Prior $\times$ Likelihood** — the denominator is just a normalizer.
- The **base-rate fallacy** explains why high-accuracy tests can yield low PPV in low-prevalence populations.
- Bayes' Rule is the foundation of **Bayesian inference, Bayesian networks, Naive Bayes, Kalman filters, HMMs, and probabilistic programming**.
- In multi-feature problems, the **Naive Bayes assumption** factorizes the joint likelihood into a product of marginal likelihoods.
- All probabilities lie in $[0, 1]$ and the posterior distribution over hypotheses must **sum to 1**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theorem of Total Probability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIA & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theorem of Total Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem of Total Probability (TTP)**
> Let $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ be a finite collection of **mutually exclusive** and **collectively exhaustive** events defined on a sample space $S$, where each event $A_i$ has a **non-zero probability** of occurrence, i.e., $P(A_i) > 0$ for all $i$. Then, for any arbitrary event $B$ defined on the same sample space, the probability of $B$ is given by:
>
> $$P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$$

The condition of **mutual exclusivity** ensures that the events $A_i$ do not overlap:
$$A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset \quad \text{for all } i \neq j$$

The condition of **collective exhaustiveness** ensures that the events cover the entire sample space:
$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i = S$$

Together, these two conditions define what is formally known as a **partition** of the sample space $S$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation — "The Rain Umbrella Problem"**
> Imagine you are a meteorologist trying to estimate the probability that it will rain on a given day. You do not have a single direct measurement, so you split the problem into three possible weather conditions: **Sunny**, **Cloudy**, and **Stormy**. Each of these weather states has its own probability of occurring, and each carries its own *likelihood* of producing rain.
>
> To find the **overall** probability of rain, you simply:
> 1. Calculate how often it rains *given* each weather state.
> 2. Multiply that by how often the weather state itself happens.
> 3. Sum all the weighted possibilities.
>
> That is precisely the Theorem of Total Probability — it is a **divide-and-conquer strategy** for computing an event's probability by breaking the sample space into manageable, non-overlapping pieces.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

> [!TIP]
> In Machine Learning, the TTP forms the **mathematical backbone** of:
> - **Bayesian Inference** (computing prior + likelihood = marginal probability)
> - **Naive Bayes Classifiers** (marginalization over hidden class variables)
> - **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** (forward algorithm for sequence probability)
> - **Mixture Models and Ensemble Methods** (weighted aggregation of component predictions)
> - **Probabilistic Graphical Models** (marginalization in Bayesian Networks)

The theorem effectively says: *"If a direct computation of $P(B)$ is hard, decompose it using a known partition of the sample space."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Prerequisites (Required Background)

Before applying the TTP, the student must ensure the following are satisfied:

- **Sample Space ($S$)**: The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment.
- **Event ($B$)**: A subset of $S$ for which we want to compute the probability.
- **Partition Events ($A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$)**: A set of events such that:
  - They are **pairwise disjoint**: $A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.
  - They are **collectively exhaustive**: $A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \cdots \cup A_n = S$.
- **Non-zero probabilities**: $P(A_i) \neq 0$ for all $i$ (to avoid division by zero in conditional probabilities).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Partition the sample space.**
Choose a set of events $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ that forms a partition of $S$. This partition is the *strategy* you adopt to break the problem.

**Step 2 — Express $B$ as a union of disjoint intersections.**
Because the $A_i$'s cover the entire sample space, the event $B$ can be written as:
$$B = B \cap S = B \cap (A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \cdots \cup A_n) = (B \cap A_1) \cup (B \cap A_2) \cup \cdots \cup (B \cap A_n)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the disjointness property.**
Since the $A_i$'s are mutually exclusive, the sets $B \cap A_1, B \cap A_2, \ldots, B \cap A_n$ are also mutually exclusive. Thus, the probability of their union is the sum of their probabilities:
$$P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \cap A_i)$$

**Step 4 — Use the definition of conditional probability.**
By the definition of conditional probability, $P(B \mid A_i) = \dfrac{P(B \cap A_i)}{P(A_i)}$, which gives $P(B \cap A_i) = P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$.

**Step 5 — Substitute.**
Plug the result of Step 4 into Step 3 to obtain the final result:
$$P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table (Exam Essential)**

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / When to Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Total Probability Theorem | $P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$ | Compute $P(B)$ by partitioning sample space |
| Conditional Probability | $P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}$, $\;P(A) > 0$ | Used to derive TTP from the product rule |
| Product Rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ | Expresses joint probability |
| Partition Condition | $A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset,\; \bigcup A_i = S$ | Must hold for TTP to be valid |
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)}$ | TTP is the *denominator* of Bayes' Theorem |
| Two-Event Special Case | $P(B) = P(B \mid A) P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) P(A^c)$ | Most common exam variant |
| Law of Total Expectation | $E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X \mid A_i] P(A_i)$ | Expectation analog of TTP |
| Marginal from Joint | $P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B, A_i)$ | Equivalent before applying product rule |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems**: Probability a patient has a disease, computed over multiple possible diagnostic test results.
- **Spam Filtering (Naive Bayes)**: Probability of observing certain word features, marginalizing over spam vs. ham classes.
- **Speech Recognition (HMMs)**: Forward algorithm computes observation likelihood by summing over all possible hidden state sequences.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Manufacturing**: Probability of a system failure, computed by marginalizing over possible operating modes.
- **Risk Analysis in Finance**: Probability of a portfolio loss, weighted across different market scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Theorem of Total Probability

We will prove the theorem from first principles using the axioms of probability.

**Given:**
- $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ is a partition of $S$, meaning:
  - $A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$
  - $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i = S$
- $B \subseteq S$ is any event of interest.
- $P(A_i) > 0$ for all $i$.

**Proof:**

Starting from the fact that the partition covers the sample space, the event $B$ can be expressed as the intersection of $B$ with the entire sample space:

$$
B = B \cap S
$$

Since the $A_i$'s form a partition, $S$ equals the union of all $A_i$'s. Substituting this:

$$
B = B \cap \left( \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i \right)
$$

By the **distributive law** of set intersection over union, we obtain:

$$
B = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (B \cap A_i)
$$

Because the $A_i$'s are mutually exclusive, the intersections $B \cap A_i$ are also mutually exclusive. By the **third axiom of probability** (finite additivity for disjoint events):

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \cap A_i)
$$

Now, using the **definition of conditional probability**, for each $i$:

$$
P(B \cap A_i) = P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

Substituting back into the summation:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Two-Partition Case (Foundation Level)

> **Problem:** A factory has two machines, $M_1$ and $M_2$. Machine $M_1$ produces **60%** of all items, and machine $M_2$ produces the remaining **40%**. The probability that a randomly chosen item is defective is **2%** if produced by $M_1$ and **3%** if produced by $M_2$. Find the **overall probability** that a randomly selected item is defective.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the partition.**
The events $A_1 = \{\text{Item is from } M_1\}$ and $A_2 = \{\text{Item is from } M_2\}$ form a partition.

**Step 2 — Extract the given probabilities.**
$$P(A_1) = 0.60, \quad P(A_2) = 0.40$$

**Step 3 — Extract the conditional probabilities.**
$$P(D \mid A_1) = 0.02, \quad P(D \mid A_2) = 0.03$$

where $D$ is the event that the item is defective.

**Step 4 — Apply the TTP formula.**
$$
P(D) = P(D \mid A_1) P(A_1) + P(D \mid A_2) P(A_2)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
P(D) = (0.02)(0.60) + (0.03)(0.40)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute each term.**

$$
P(D) = 0.012 + 0.012
$$

**Step 7 — Final answer.**

$$
P(D) = 0.024 = 2.4\%
$$

The overall probability that a randomly selected item is defective is **2.4%**.

### 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Three-Partition Case (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** In a college, students are classified as belonging to one of three streams: Science (**50%**), Commerce (**30%**), and Humanities (**20%**). The probability that a student *passes* an exam is **0.80** for Science, **0.70** for Commerce, and **0.60** for Humanities. A student is selected at random. Find the probability that the student **passes** the exam.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the events.**
- $A_1 = \{\text{Science}\},\; A_2 = \{\text{Commerce}\},\; A_3 = \{\text{Humanities}\}$
- $B = \{\text{Student passes}\}$

**Step 2 — List known probabilities.**

$$
P(A_1) = 0.50, \quad P(A_2) = 0.30, \quad P(A_3) = 0.20
$$

$$
P(B \mid A_1) = 0.80, \quad P(B \mid A_2) = 0.70, \quad P(B \mid A_3) = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the TTP formula.**

$$
P(B) = P(B \mid A_1) P(A_1) + P(B \mid A_2) P(A_2) + P(B \mid A_3) P(A_3)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
P(B) = (0.80)(0.50) + (0.70)(0.30) + (0.60)(0.20)
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply each pair.**

$$
P(B) = 0.40 + 0.21 + 0.12
$$

**Step 6 — Sum.**

$$
P(B) = 0.73
$$

The probability that a randomly chosen student passes the exam is **0.73 (or 73%)**.

### 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Connection to Bayes' Theorem (Advanced)

> **Problem:** Continuing Example 3.2, if a student is found to have **passed** the exam, what is the probability that the student belongs to the **Science** stream?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify what we need.** We need $P(A_1 \mid B)$.

**Step 2 — State Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(A_1 \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_1) P(A_1)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 3 — The denominator $P(B)$ is exactly what TTP computed in Example 3.2.**

$$
P(B) = 0.73
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator computation.**

$$
P(B \mid A_1) P(A_1) = (0.80)(0.50) = 0.40
$$

**Step 5 — Final substitution.**

$$
P(A_1 \mid B) = \frac{0.40}{0.73} \approx 0.5479
$$

The probability that a passing student belongs to the Science stream is approximately **54.79%**.

> [!TIP]
> This example illustrates the **fundamental relationship**: the **TTP computes the denominator of Bayes' Theorem**. Together, they form a complete framework for **inverting conditional probabilities**.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Step** | **Action** | **Key Result** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the partition $\{A_i\}$ | Verify mutual exclusivity and exhaustiveness |
| 2 | Extract prior probabilities $P(A_i)$ | Required for weighting |
| 3 | Extract conditional probabilities $P(B \mid A_i)$ | Must be given or computable |
| 4 | Apply the sum-product formula | $P(B) = \sum P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)$ |
| 5 | Numerically evaluate each term | Multiply then sum |
| 6 | Sanity check the answer | $0 \leq P(B) \leq 1$ must hold |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Partition-Based Computational Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Event B has unknown probability]) --> A[Step 1: Identify a partition of sample space S]
    A --> B[Step 2: Verify that events A1, A2, ..., An are mutually exclusive]
    B --> C[Step 3: Verify that events A1, A2, ..., An are exhaustive]
    C --> D[Step 4: Extract prior probabilities P Ai]
    D --> E[Step 5: Extract conditional probabilities P B given Ai]
    E --> F[Step 6: For each i, compute the joint P B intersect Ai = P B given Ai times P Ai]
    F --> G[Step 7: Sum all joint probabilities across the partition]
    G --> H[Step 8: Output P B as the total probability]
    H --> I{Use P B as denominator in Bayes Theorem if needed}
    I -- Yes --> J[Compute posterior P Ai given B for any desired i]
    I -- No --> K([End: Total probability computed])
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Decision Tree View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root([Sample Space S]) --> A1[A1 — Prior P A1]
    root --> A2[A2 — Prior P A2]
    root --> A3[A3 — Prior P A3]
    A1 --> B1[B intersect A1 — Joint P B given A1 times P A1]
    A2 --> B2[B intersect A2 — Joint P B given A2 times P A2]
    A3 --> B3[B intersect A3 — Joint P B given A3 times P A3]
    B1 --> sum((Summation Operator))
    B2 --> sum
    B3 --> sum
    sum --> result[P B — Total Probability of Event B]
```

### 4.3 Venn Diagram Representation (Textual Schematic)

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
                │          SAMPLE SPACE  S                │
                │                                         │
                │   ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────┐  │
                │   │          │  │          │  │      │  │
                │   │   A1     │  │   A2     │  │  A3  │  │
                │   │  ┌────┐  │  │  ┌────┐  │  │┌────┐│  │
                │   │  │B∩A1│  │  │  │B∩A2│  │  ││B∩A3││  │
                │   │  └────┘  │  │  └────┘  │  │└────┘│  │
                │   │          │  │          │  │      │  │
                │   └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────┘  │
                │                                         │
                │   P(B) = P(B∩A1) + P(B∩A2) + P(B∩A3)   │
                └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The diagram shows that the event $B$ is split into three non-overlapping slices $B \cap A_1$, $B \cap A_2$, and $B \cap A_3$, each living entirely inside one cell of the partition. Their probabilities sum to $P(B)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Theorem of Total Probability. Mention the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

The Theorem of Total Probability states that if $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ is a partition of the sample space $S$ (i.e., the events are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive) with $P(A_i) > 0$ for all $i$, then for any event $B$:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
$$

**Conditions of applicability:**
1. The events $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ must be **mutually exclusive** ($A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$).
2. The events must be **collectively exhaustive** ($\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i = S$).
3. Each $P(A_i)$ must be **strictly positive** (to ensure conditional probabilities are defined).
4. The event $B$ must be a valid event on the same sample space $S$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**How is the Theorem of Total Probability related to Bayes' Theorem? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

The Theorem of Total Probability computes the **denominator** of Bayes' Theorem. Bayes' Theorem is given by:

$$
P(A_k \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_k) \cdot P(A_k)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}
$$

The denominator $\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$ is precisely $P(B)$, computed using the TTP. Thus, TTP serves as the **normalization constant** in Bayesian inference, allowing us to convert a joint probability $P(B \mid A_k) P(A_k)$ into a valid conditional (posterior) probability $P(A_k \mid B)$. In essence, **TTP enables Bayes' Theorem** by providing the marginal probability $P(B)$ needed to "invert" the conditional.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** A software company uses three different servers — $S_1$, $S_2$, and $S_3$ — to process requests. Server $S_1$ handles **50%** of the requests, $S_2$ handles **30%**, and $S_3$ handles **20%**. The probability of a request failing is **0.01** on $S_1$, **0.02** on $S_2$, and **0.03** on $S_3$. Find the total probability that a randomly selected request **fails**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Given that a request has failed, determine the probability that it was processed by **server $S_2$**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the events.**
Let $A_1, A_2, A_3$ denote the events that a request is processed by $S_1, S_2, S_3$ respectively. Let $F$ be the event that the request fails.

**Step 2 — Given data.**

$$
P(A_1) = 0.50, \quad P(A_2) = 0.30, \quad P(A_3) = 0.20
$$

$$
P(F \mid A_1) = 0.01, \quad P(F \mid A_2) = 0.02, \quad P(F \mid A_3) = 0.03
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the TTP formula.**

$$
P(F) = P(F \mid A_1) P(A_1) + P(F \mid A_2) P(A_2) + P(F \mid A_3) P(A_3)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
P(F) = (0.01)(0.50) + (0.02)(0.30) + (0.03)(0.20)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute each term.**

$$
P(F) = 0.005 + 0.006 + 0.006
$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{P(F) = 0.017 = 1.7\%}
$$

The total probability of a failed request is **1.7%**.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — We need to find $P(A_2 \mid F)$ using Bayes' Theorem.**

**Step 2 — Bayes' Theorem states:**

$$
P(A_2 \mid F) = \frac{P(F \mid A_2) P(A_2)}{P(F)}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$
P(F \mid A_2) P(A_2) = (0.02)(0.30) = 0.006
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator (from Part a).**

$$
P(F) = 0.017
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
P(A_2 \mid F) = \frac{0.006}{0.017} = \frac{6}{17} \approx 0.3529
$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{P(A_2 \mid F) \approx 0.3529 = 35.29\%}
$$

Given a failed request, there is approximately a **35.29%** probability that it was processed by server $S_2$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a)** Derive the **Theorem of Total Probability** using the axioms of probability. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**b)** In a factory, three machines $M_1$, $M_2$, and $M_3$ manufacture **20%**, **30%**, and **50%** of the total production respectively. From past records, the fractions of defective items produced are **0.005**, **0.010**, and **0.020** respectively. An item is chosen at random from the total production and is found to be defective. What is the probability that it was produced by **machine $M_3$**? **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Assumption 1:** Let $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ be a partition of the sample space $S$, meaning the $A_i$'s are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.

**Assumption 2:** Each $P(A_i) > 0$ to ensure all conditional probabilities are defined.

**Step 1.** Since the $A_i$'s cover the entire sample space, $B$ can be written as:

$$
B = B \cap S = B \cap \left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Using the distributive law:

$$
B = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (B \cap A_i)
$$

**Step 3.** Since the $A_i$'s are mutually exclusive, the events $B \cap A_i$ are also mutually exclusive. By the finite additivity axiom:

$$
P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \cap A_i)
$$

**Step 4.** By the definition of conditional probability $P(B \cap A_i) = P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)$. Substituting:

$$
\boxed{P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}
$$

This is the Theorem of Total Probability. $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define events.**
$A_i$: item is produced by machine $M_i$, for $i = 1, 2, 3$.
$D$: item is defective.

**Step 2 — Given probabilities.**

$$
P(A_1) = 0.20, \quad P(A_2) = 0.30, \quad P(A_3) = 0.50
$$

$$
P(D \mid A_1) = 0.005, \quad P(D \mid A_2) = 0.010, \quad P(D \mid A_3) = 0.020
$$

**Step 3 — Find $P(D)$ using the TTP.**

$$
P(D) = (0.005)(0.20) + (0.010)(0.30) + (0.020)(0.50)
$$

$$
P(D) = 0.0010 + 0.0030 + 0.0100 = 0.0140
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to find $P(A_3 \mid D)$.**

$$
P(A_3 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid A_3) P(A_3)}{P(D)} = \frac{(0.020)(0.50)}{0.0140} = \frac{0.0100}{0.0140} = \frac{10}{14} = \frac{5}{7}
$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical answer.**

$$
\boxed{P(A_3 \mid D) = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143 = 71.43\%}
$$

Given that the item is defective, there is approximately a **71.43%** probability that it was produced by machine $M_3$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify partition conditions** — Students often apply TTP without confirming that the $A_i$'s are mutually exclusive and exhaustive. The KTU examiner **will deduct 1–2 marks** if these are not explicitly stated.
> 2. **Mixing up prior and conditional probabilities** — In Bayes' Theorem problems, students frequently swap $P(A_i)$ with $P(B \mid A_i)$. Always remember: $P(A_i)$ is the **prior**, $P(B \mid A_i)$ is the **likelihood**, and $P(A_i \mid B)$ is the **posterior**.
> 3. **Skipping the TTP step in Bayes' problems** — In KTU valuation, the **denominator $P(B)$ must be explicitly computed using TTP** before substitution. Writing only the Bayes' formula and not showing the TTP computation loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Unit/decimal mistakes** — Convert all percentages to decimals (e.g., 20% = 0.20) **before** substituting, not after. Mixing units is a recurring error.
> 5. **Not stating the result with units** — Always express the final probability as both a **decimal** and a **percentage** for clarity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- **TTP Formula**: $P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)$ — memorize verbatim.
- **Partition Conditions**: Mutual exclusivity + collective exhaustiveness = **mandatory** for TTP applicability.
- **Non-zero priors**: Each $P(A_i) > 0$ — required for conditional probabilities to be defined.
- **Two-event shortcut**: $P(B) = P(B \mid A) P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) P(A^c)$ — most common exam variant.
- **Bayes' Connection**: TTP = **denominator of Bayes' Theorem**. TTP $\Rightarrow$ marginal; Bayes' $\Rightarrow$ posterior.
- **Sanity Check**: The final answer must satisfy $0 \leq P(B) \leq 1$. If not, re-check arithmetic.
- **ML Applications**: Naive Bayes, HMM forward algorithm, Bayesian networks, mixture models — all rely on TTP for marginalization.
- **Common Mistake**: Forgetting the $P(A_i)$ weight — each conditional probability must be **weighted** by its prior.
- **Notation Discipline**: $P(B \mid A)$ is the *probability of $B$ given $A$ already occurred*, not the reverse.
- **Always show the TTP computation step explicitly** in Bayes' Theorem problems — KTU examiners reward the explicit marginalization step with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Motion Planning Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Motion Planning Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion Planning Problem (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> The *Motion Planning Problem* is defined as the computational problem of finding a continuous sequence of valid configurations that moves a movable object (the **robot** or **agent**) from an initial configuration $q_{start}$ to a goal configuration $q_{goal}$, while avoiding collisions with static and dynamic obstacles in the environment, and respecting any kinematic and dynamic constraints of the system.

Mathematically, a basic motion planning problem is defined as the **5-tuple**:

$$\Pi_{MP} = (C, C_{obs}, C_{free}, q_{start}, q_{goal})$$

where:
- $C$ = the **Configuration Space** of the robot (the space of all possible placements/orientations).
- $C_{obs}$ = the **Obstacle Region** in configuration space (set of invalid configurations).
- $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$ = the **Free Space** (set of valid, collision-free configurations).
- $q_{start} \in C_{free}$ = the initial configuration.
- $q_{goal} \in C_{free}$ = the target configuration.

The objective is to compute a continuous path $\tau : [0, 1] \to C_{free}$ such that $\tau(0) = q_{start}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{goal}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Motion Planning is categorized under *Classical Planning* in AI. It is the geometric counterpart of classical *state-space search* (such as STRIPS), where the state is replaced by a continuous geometric configuration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car through a busy city to reach a hospital:
- The **start point** is your home.
- The **goal point** is the hospital.
- The **streets and lanes** form the drivable area.
- The **buildings, dividers, and one-way restrictions** are the *obstacles* and *constraints*.

The *Motion Planning Problem* is the AI-version of this driving task: an algorithm must mathematically figure out **which turns, accelerations, and steering angles** to apply so that the robot reaches the goal without crashing.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** If classical AI planning (STRIPS) answers *“what sequence of actions solves a puzzle?”*, Motion Planning answers *“what sequence of movements navigates the world?”*. It is the bridge between **discrete AI reasoning** and **continuous robotics geometry**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

| Metric / Parameter | Symbol | Unit / Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Degrees of Freedom | **DOF** | Dimensionless (integer $\geq 1$) |
| Configuration Space Dimension | $n$ | Real-valued |
| Path Length | $L(\tau)$ | Meters (m) |
| Planning Time Complexity | $T(n)$ | Seconds |
| Clearance (Safety margin) | $\delta$ | Meters (m) |
| Workspace Dimension | $d$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Algorithm Completeness | — | Boolean (True / False) |

> [!NOTE]
> For a rigid body in 2D, the standard robot is a **polygon** $R$ with rotation $\theta$. A configuration is $q = (x, y, \theta)$, making $C = \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ (a 3D space).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Components of a Motion Planning Problem

A complete motion planning problem consists of three fundamental components. Let us examine each in detail:

- **A. The Robot ($R$)**
  - The robot is the movable object that the planner controls.
  - It is described by a geometric shape (e.g., polygon, polyhedron, disc, point) plus kinematic constraints (e.g., non-holonomic, joints).
  - In its simplest form, $R$ is modeled as a **point robot** ($R = \{p\}$).

- **B. The Workspace ($W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2}$ or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$)**
  - The workspace is the physical 2D or 3D universe in which the robot operates.
  - It contains obstacles $O_i$ and the free region.
  - Formally, $W = W_{free} \cup \bigcup O_i$.

- **C. The Configuration Space ($C$)**
  - The configuration space is the abstract, mathematical space of all possible placements of the robot.
  - Each point in $C$ uniquely specifies a pose (position + orientation) of the robot.
  - **Why do we need it?** Direct planning in the workspace is hard because the robot's shape changes the effective obstacle region as it moves. By mapping the robot to a point in $C$, the problem becomes a classical point-navigation problem.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the C-Space Obstacle

For a rigid translating robot $R$ at position $q = (x, y)$, the **C-space obstacle** induced by workspace obstacle $O$ is defined as:

$$C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset \}$$

In other words, $C_{obs}$ is the set of all configurations where the robot, when placed at $q$, *intersects* the workspace obstacle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minkowski Sum Representation (Critical for KTU 2024):**
> For a translating robot, the C-space obstacle is computed using the **Minkowski sum** with the reflected robot:
> $$C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R) = \{ o - r \mid o \in O, r \in R \}$$
> This operation "grows" the obstacle by the shape of the robot, effectively turning the robot into a point.

### 2.3 Classification of Motion Planning Problems

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes motion planning into the following sub-problems:

| # | Sub-Problem | Description | Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Piano Mover’s Problem** | Pure geometric planning for static, known obstacles | Moving a sofa in a hallway |
| 2 | **Point-to-Point (Goal-Query)** | Find any path from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ | Robot reaching a cupboard |
| 3 | **Coverage Planning** | Visit every free configuration | Lawn mower / floor cleaner |
| 4 | **Optimal Planning** | Minimize cost (length, time, energy) | Self-driving car shortest route |
| 5 | **Kinodynamic Planning** | Respect velocity, acceleration limits | Drone flight planning |
| 6 | **Planning with Uncertainty** | Handle noisy sensors / partial maps | Vacuum robot in unknown room |
| 7 | **Multi-Robot Planning** | Coordinate $N$ robots simultaneously | Warehouse fleet coordination |
| 8 | **Planning with Moving Obstacles** | Time-varying $C_{obs}(t)$ | Pedestrian-aware autonomous car |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam (Memorize these!)**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Configuration | $q = (x, y, \theta)$ (2D rigid body) | $q \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ |
| C-Space Obstacle | $C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset \}$ | Set of invalid configurations |
| Free Space | $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$ | Search domain |
| Minkowski Sum (C-Obstacle) | $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$ | Translating robot |
| Workspace to C-Space | $q \mapsto R(q)$ | Maps configuration to robot pose |
