| Path Length | $L(\tau) = \int_{0}^{1} \vert \tau'(s) \vert \, ds$ | Integral of norm of velocity |
| DOF for Rigid Body | $\text{DOF} = 6$ (3D) or $3$ (2D) | Position + Orientation |
| DOF for Articulated | $\text{DOF} = n_{rigid} - n_{constraints}$ | Grubler’s Formula |
| Potential Field Force | $F_{att} = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})$ | Attractive toward goal |
| Repulsive Force | $F_{rep} = k_{rep}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}\nabla \rho$ | From obstacle at distance $\rho$ |
| Clearance | $\delta = \min_{q \in \tau} d(q, C_{obs})$ | Minimum safety margin along path |
| Completeness | Returns a path if one exists, else reports failure | Strong vs. Weak |
| Optimality | Minimizes chosen cost function $c(\tau)$ | E.g., $c(\tau) = L(\tau)$ |

### 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' — Real-World Utility

**Why is this topic so important in KTU 2024 syllabus?**
Modern engineering systems — from **autonomous vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) to **surgical robots** (Da Vinci) and **warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva bots) — all depend on motion planning as their central decision-making module. The KTU 2024 scheme highlights this topic under AI to bridge symbolic AI planning with continuous-world robotics.

**How is it used in engineering practice?**
- **Industrial Robotics:** Welding and painting arms in car assembly lines use motion planners to optimize cycle time.
- **Aerospace:** NASA’s Mars rovers use online motion planning to navigate around rocks and dunes.
- **Healthcare:** Robotic surgery requires sub-millimeter accuracy motion planning in cluttered anatomy.
- **Gaming & VFX:** NPCs (Non-Player Characters) use motion planning for believable navigation in 3D worlds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Translating a Point Robot in 2D Workspace

**Problem Setup:** A point robot $R = \{p_0\}$ (zero-sized) operates in a 2D workspace $W = \mathbb{R}^{2}$ containing a single circular obstacle $O$ of radius $r$, centered at $(c_x, c_y)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration space.**

Since the robot is a point, a configuration is just a 2D coordinate.

$$q = (x, y)$$

Therefore:

$$C = \mathbb{R}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the C-space obstacle.**

The C-space obstacle is the set of configurations where the point lies inside the obstacle.

$$C_{obs} = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \mid (x - c_x)^{2} + (y - c_y)^{2} \leq r^{2}\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the free space.**

The free space is everything outside the circle.

$$C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^{2} \setminus C_{obs}$$

**Step 4 — Path definition.**

Any continuous function $\tau : [0, 1] \to C_{free}$ satisfying the boundary conditions is a valid path.

$$\tau(0) = q_{start}, \quad \tau(1) = q_{goal}$$

**Step 5 — Compute path length using the arc-length integral.**

$$L(\tau) = \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{ds}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{dy}{ds}\right)^{2}} \, ds$$

This is the standard Euclidean arc-length formula for a parametric curve in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** For a point robot, motion planning reduces to a 2D navigation problem with a circular forbidden zone. The shortest path is a *tangent path* that grazes the boundary of $C_{obs}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: C-Space Obstacle for a Translating Disc Robot

**Problem Setup:** Robot $R$ is a disc of radius $\rho$ centered at $p_0$. Workspace obstacle $O$ is a convex polygon with vertices $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Minkowski sum definition.**

The C-space obstacle is the *grown* obstacle that encompasses all positions of the disc center that cause collision.

$$C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Minkowski sum.**

$$C_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \mid \exists (o_x, o_y) \in O, \, (r_x, r_y) \in R : (x, y) = (o_x, o_y) - (r_x, r_y) \}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify for disc robot.**

For a disc of radius $\rho$, $R = \{(u, v) \mid u^{2} + v^{2} \leq \rho^{2}\}$, so $-R = R$ (disc is symmetric about origin). The Minkowski sum becomes:

$$C_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \mid \text{dist}((x, y), O) \leq \rho \}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric interpretation.**

$$C_{obs} = \text{the polygon } O \text{ offset outward by distance } \rho$$

For a convex polygon, the offset polygon has the same number of edges as $O$ plus rounded corners (circular arcs of radius $\rho$).

**Step 5 — Free space reduction.**

The disc robot can now be modeled as a *point* navigating in $C_{free}$, where:

$$C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^{2} \setminus C_{obs}$$

This transformation is the cornerstone of *configuration-space planning* and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Planning Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Key Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Visibility Graph** | Combinatorial Roadmap | Resolution-Complete | Optimal (shortest) | $O(n^{3})$ | 2D polygon obstacles |
| **Voronoi Diagram** | Combinatorial Roadmap | Resolution-Complete | Maximizes clearance | $O(n \log n)$ | High-safety navigation |
| **Exact Cell Decomposition** | Combinatorial | Complete | Sub-optimal (cell-wise) | Exponential | High-precision, sparse obstacles |
| **Approximate Cell Decomposition** | Sampling | Resolution-Complete | Sub-optimal | Polynomial | Real-time large-scale |
| **RRT (Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree)** | Sampling (single-query) | Probabilistically Complete | Sub-optimal | Polynomial in $n$ | High-dimensional, dynamic |
| **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)** | Sampling (multi-query) | Probabilistically Complete | Sub-optimal | Polynomial | Multi-query static environment |
| **A\* Search (in C-space)** | Discrete Graph Search | Complete on grid | Optimal (with admissible heuristic) | $O(b^{d})$ | Known grids |
| **Potential Field** | Reactive / Local | Not complete (local minima) | Not optimal | $O(1)$ per step | Real-time reactive control |
| **D\* / D\* Lite** | Incremental Search | Complete | Optimal | Re-plans efficiently | Unknown / changing maps |
| **RRT\*** | Sampling | Asymptotically Optimal | Optimal as $n \to \infty$ | Polynomial | Modern optimal planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Expect at least one Part B question contrasting *Roadmap* methods with *Cell Decomposition* and *Potential Field* methods. Memorize the **completeness** and **optimality** columns.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Visibility Graph Construction

**Given:** A 2D workspace with a rectangular obstacle $O$ with vertices $\{(1,1), (4,1), (4,3), (1,3)\}$ and start $q_s = (0,0)$, goal $q_g = (5,4)$.

**Step 1 — Augment obstacle vertices.**

For a convex polygon, add the start and goal as additional "vertices" to ensure they lie on the graph.

$$V = \{(0,0), (1,1), (4,1), (4,3), (1,3), (5,4)\}$$

**Step 2 — Construct edges between mutually visible pairs.**

Two vertices are *visible* if the line segment between them does not intersect the interior of the obstacle.

**Step 3 — Compute edge weights (Euclidean distances).**

$$w(u, v) = \sqrt{(v_x - u_x)^{2} + (v_y - u_y)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Run shortest-path algorithm (e.g., Dijkstra's or A\*).**

The result is the **shortest collision-free path**, which consists of straight-line segments tangent to the obstacle.

> [!TIP]
> This example is a classic KTU 2024 model question. The expected answer is a labeled diagram of the visibility graph and the resulting optimal path.

### 3.5 Clearance and Safety Margins — Engineering Derivation

**Definition of Clearance:**

$$\delta(\tau) = \min_{s \in [0, 1]} d(\tau(s), C_{obs})$$

where $d(q, C_{obs})$ is the Euclidean distance from configuration $q$ to the C-space obstacle.

**Engineering Significance:**
- In **medical robotics** (e.g., neurosurgery), $\delta \geq 1\,\text{mm}$ is mandatory.
- In **autonomous cars**, $\delta$ is incorporated into the cost function:

$$c(\tau) = L(\tau) - \lambda \cdot \delta(\tau)$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is a trade-off weight balancing path length and safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pyq Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-question often asks *"Define clearance and explain its role in safe motion planning with an example cost function."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Motion Planning Architecture & Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W] --> B[Define Robot R]
    B --> C[Compute Configuration Space C]
    A --> D[Define Obstacles O]
    D --> E[Compute C-Space Obstacle Cobs]
    E --> F[Minkowski Sum: Cobs = O + minus R]
    C --> G[Compute Free Space Cfree]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Choose Planning Algorithm]
    H --> H1[Roadmap: Visibility Graph]
    H --> H2[Cell Decomposition]
    H --> H3[Potential Field]
    H --> H4[Sampling: RRT, PRM]
    H1 --> I[Search in Roadmap]
    H2 --> J[Search in Cell Graph]
    H3 --> K[Gradient Descent in Field]
    H4 --> L[Random Exploration]
    I --> M[Path Tau Found]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Validate Path: Tau subset Cfree]
    N --> O[Execute Path on Robot]
    O --> P[Done]
    N --> Q[No Path Exists]
    Q --> R[Report Failure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pipeline begins with the workspace and robot definition, then mathematically transforms obstacles into C-space, and finally invokes a planner. If a valid path is found, it is validated against $C_{free}$ before execution.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Motion Planning Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MP[Motion Planning] --> MP1[Combinatorial]
    MP --> MP2[Sampling-Based]
    MP --> MP3[Reactive]
    MP1 --> MP1a[Roadmap Methods]
    MP1 --> MP1b[Cell Decomposition]
    MP1a --> MP1a1[Visibility Graph]
    MP1a --> MP1a2[Voronoi Diagram]
    MP1a --> MP1a3[Silhouette Method]
    MP1b --> MP1b1[Exact Cell Decomp]
    MP1b --> MP1b2[Approximate Cell Decomp]
    MP2 --> MP2a[PRM Multi-Query]
    MP2 --> MP2b[RRT Single-Query]
    MP2 --> MP2c[RRT Star Optimal]
    MP3 --> MP3a[Potential Field]
    MP3 --> MP3b[Vector Field Histogram]
    MP3 --> MP3c[DWA Dynamic Window]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Online Motion Planning Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sensor Layer: Lidar Camera GPS] --> S2[Perception Module]
    S2 --> S3[Map Builder Occupancy Grid]
    S3 --> S4[Localizer Pose Estimation]
    S4 --> S5[Global Planner C-space Search]
    S5 --> S6[Local Planner Trajectory Optimization]
    S6 --> S7[Controller Velocity Commands]
    S7 --> S8[Robot Actuators Motors]
    S8 --> S9[Environment Updates]
    S9 --> S2
    S1 -.feedback.-> S7
    S5 --> S10[Cost Maps Safety Weights]
    S10 --> S6
    S6 --> S11[Collision Checker Geometric Tests]
    S11 --> S6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** This block diagram is the *online* (real-time) planning stack used in self-driving cars (Apollo, Autoware) and warehouse robots. The *perception → planning → control* loop runs at **10–100 Hz**. The KTU examiner often asks students to label such a stack in 14-mark questions.

### 4.4 State Machine: Planner Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sensing: New Goal Received
    Sensing --> Mapping: Update Map
    Mapping --> Planning: C-Space Built
    Planning --> Searching: Algorithm Selected
    Searching --> Validating: Candidate Path Found
    Validating --> Executing: Path in Cfree
    Validating --> Planning: Path Invalid
    Searching --> Failure: Time Limit Reached
    Executing --> Monitoring: Path Tracked
    Monitoring --> Sensing: Replan Triggered
    Monitoring --> Done: Goal Reached
    Done --> Idle
    Failure --> Idle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Motion Planning Problem. List its four essential components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Motion Planning Problem** is the computational task of determining a continuous, collision-free path that takes a robot from an initial configuration $q_{start}$ to a goal configuration $q_{goal}$ in a workspace containing obstacles.

**Four essential components:**
1. **Robot description ($R$)** — the movable object and its kinematic constraints.
2. **Workspace ($W$)** — the physical 2D/3D environment.
3. **Obstacles ($O$)** — regions the robot must avoid.
4. **Initial and goal configurations** ($q_{start}, q_{goal}$) — the start and target poses.

> [!TIP]
> Award **1 mark** for the definition, **1 mark** for the four components listed, and **1 mark** for the formula $\tau : [0,1] \to C_{free}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Configuration Space? How is it different from Workspace?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Configuration Space ($C$):** The mathematical space of all possible placements/orientations of a robot. Each point in $C$ represents a complete specification of the robot's pose (e.g., $q = (x, y, \theta)$ in 2D).
- **Workspace ($W$):** The physical 2D or 3D environment in which the robot actually moves.

**Key Difference:** The workspace describes *where the robot is physically located*, whereas the configuration space describes *all possible states the robot can be in*. In $C$, the robot is reduced to a single point, which simplifies planning dramatically.

| Aspect | Workspace | Configuration Space |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Represents | Physical environment | All robot poses |
| Robot shape | Yes (obstacle interaction) | Collapsed to a point |
| Obstacles | $O \subseteq W$ | $C_{obs} \subseteq C$ |
| Used for | Visualization | Mathematical planning |

> [!TIP]
> Award **1 mark** for $C$ definition, **1 mark** for $W$ definition, and **1 mark** for the comparative distinction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> A very common mistake students make is conflating *Workspace* with *Configuration Space*. When you compute the **C-space obstacle**, you must apply the **Minkowski sum** to *grow* the obstacle, not the robot. Writing $C_{obs} = O - R$ (i.e., subtracting the robot directly) is **worth 0 marks** — the correct expression is $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$ for translating robots. The KTU 2024 valuation key specifically checks this distinction.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **Configuration Space** and **C-Space Obstacle** for a translating disc robot of radius $\rho$ moving in a 2D workspace with a polygonal obstacle. Show mathematically how the C-space obstacle is obtained using the **Minkowski sum**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the three classical approaches to motion planning: **Roadmap methods**, **Cell Decomposition**, and **Potential Field methods**. Discuss their **completeness** and **optimality** properties with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Configuration Space Definition (2 marks)**
- Configuration space $C$ is the set of all possible placements of the robot.
- For a translating disc in 2D, $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$, so $C = \mathbb{R}^{2}$.
- For a translating-and-rotating polygon, $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$, so $C$ is 3-dimensional.

**Step 2: C-Space Obstacle Definition (2 marks)**
- A configuration is *invalid* (in $C_{obs}$) if the robot at that configuration collides with any workspace obstacle.
- Mathematically:

$$C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid R(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset \}$$

- This can be visualized as the **set of robot positions where the disc's interior overlaps the polygon**.

**Step 3: Minkowski Sum Derivation (3 marks)**
For a translating disc robot $R$ of radius $\rho$:

$$C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$$

Expanding the definition:

$$C_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \mid \exists r \in R : q + r \in O \}$$

Geometrically, this means: **inward-offset the polygon $O$ by distance $\rho$** (i.e., shrink the free space by $\rho$, which is equivalent to growing the obstacle by $\rho$). The resulting $C_{free}$ is:

$$C_{free} = \mathbb{R}^{2} \setminus C_{obs}$$

**Diagram Description (for KTU answer sheet):**
Draw a square polygon $O$, then draw a "grown" version of $O$ where each edge is offset outward by $\rho$ and each corner becomes a quarter-circle of radius $\rho$. Label the original polygon as $O$, the grown region as $C_{obs}$, and the outside as $C_{free}$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Approach | Mechanism | Completeness | Optimality | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Roadmap** | Construct a 1D graph in $C_{free}$, then graph-search | Complete (resolution-complete) | Optimal (with $A^*$) | **Visibility Graph** for indoor room navigation |
| **Cell Decomposition** | Partition $C_{free}$ into cells, build adjacency graph | Complete (exact) | Sub-optimal | **Trapezoidal decomposition** for warehouse robots |
| **Potential Field** | Robot follows gradient of artificial potential | **Not complete** (local minima) | Not optimal | **Reactive obstacle avoidance** in mobile robots |

**Real-world examples (1 mark each, plus 1 mark for completeness/optimality table):**
- **Roadmap:** Used in **Google's self-driving cars** for global route planning in road networks.
- **Cell Decomposition:** Used in **floor-cleaning robots** (e.g., iRobot Roomba) for systematic area coverage.
- **Potential Field:** Used in **Mars rovers** for real-time local obstacle avoidance while traversing rough terrain.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip:** Award 2 marks for the comparative table, 2 marks for real-world examples, and 3 marks for correctly explaining completeness and optimality of each method.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Define the **Piano Mover's Problem**. Explain the role of **degrees of freedom (DOF)** in motion planning with the example of a planar rigid body. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Visibility Graph** algorithm? Construct the visibility graph for a workspace containing a convex hexagonal obstacle with vertices at $(2,2), (4,2), (5,4), (4,6), (2,6), (1,4)$, start point $(0,0)$, and goal point $(6,5)$. Show how the shortest path is obtained. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Piano Mover's Problem (3 marks)**
- Originally formulated by **Schwartz and Sharir (1983)**.
- Defined as: given a 3D rigid body (the "piano") and a static 3D environment with obstacles, find a collision-free path to move the body from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
- It is a *purely geometric* problem with **complete information** and **no dynamic constraints**.
- It is **PSPACE-hard** in general, meaning exact algorithms have exponential complexity.

**Step 2: DOF Definition (2 marks)**
- The number of independent parameters needed to uniquely specify a robot's configuration.
- For a free-flying rigid body in 2D: $\text{DOF} = 3$ ($x$-position, $y$-position, rotation $\theta$).
- For a free-flying rigid body in 3D: $\text{DOF} = 6$ ($x, y, z$, roll, pitch, yaw).
- For a planar rigid polygon $R$, each vertex needs $(x_i, y_i)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, but rigid-body constraints reduce it to 3 DOF total.

**Step 3: Jointed and Articulated Robots (2 marks)**
- A robot with $N$ links connected by revolute joints has $\text{DOF} = N$ (each joint adds one rotational DOF).
- A 6-DOF industrial arm (e.g., PUMA 560) is fully general in 3D workspace.
- **Grübler's Formula** (for completeness):

$$\text{DOF} = 6(N - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

where $N$ = number of links, $j_i$ = number of joints of type $i$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Visibility Graph Definition (2 marks)**
A **Visibility Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is constructed as follows:
- **Vertices ($V$):** The start point $q_s$, goal point $q_g$, and all obstacle vertices.
- **Edges ($E$):** An edge exists between two vertices $u, v$ if and only if the line segment $\overline{uv}$ does **not** intersect the interior of any obstacle (i.e., $u$ and $v$ are *mutually visible*).
- The shortest path in the visibility graph, computed via **Dijkstra's algorithm**, gives the *shortest Euclidean collision-free path*.

**Step 2: Construction for the given hexagon (3 marks)**

Vertices:
- $q_s = (0, 0)$
- $v_1 = (2, 2)$
- $v_2 = (4, 2)$
- $v_3 = (5, 4)$
- $v_4 = (4, 6)$
- $v_5 = (2, 6)$
- $v_6 = (1, 4)$
- $q_g = (6, 5)$

**Mutually visible pairs** (no edge crosses hexagon interior):
- $q_s$ is visible to: $v_1$, $v_6$
- $q_g$ is visible to: $v_3$, $v_4$
- Adjacent hexagon edges are visible: $(v_1, v_2), (v_2, v_3), (v_3, v_4), (v_4, v_5), (v_5, v_6), (v_6, v_1)$

**Step 3: Shortest path via Dijkstra (2 marks)**

Compute edge weights using $w(u, v) = \sqrt{(v_x - u_x)^2 + (v_y - u_y)^2}$:
- $w(q_s, v_1) = \sqrt{4 + 4} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$
- $w(v_1, v_2) = 2$
- $w(v_2, v_3) = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$
- $w(v_3, q_g) = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$

**Optimal path:**

$$\tau^* = q_s \to v_1 \to v_2 \to v_3 \to q_g$$

**Total length:**

$$L(\tau^*) = 2\sqrt{2} + 2 + \sqrt{5} + \sqrt{2} \approx 2.83 + 2 + 2.24 + 1.41 = 8.48 \text{ units}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip:** Award 2 marks for the visibility graph definition, 3 marks for the correct list of visible edges, and 2 marks for computing the shortest path length.

---

### 5.3 Common Pitfalls and Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in Motion Planning Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Workspace and Configuration Space** — Always distinguish $W$ from $C$ in your answer. C-space is an *abstract mathematical* space.
> 2. **Forgetting to grow the obstacle, not the robot** — When computing $C_{obs}$, the obstacle is *offset outward* by the robot's radius, not the other way around.
> 3. **Ignoring rotation in 2D** — A 2D rigid body has DOF $= 3$ (not 2), because rotation $\theta$ is a third independent parameter.
> 4. **Claiming Potential Field is complete** — It is **NOT complete**; the robot can get stuck in *local minima* of the potential function.
> 5. **Writing $C_{obs} = O - R$ instead of $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$** — The KTU valuation key explicitly awards marks for the **Minkowski sum notation**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Motion Planning Cheat Sheet**

- **Definition:** Motion Planning = finding a continuous collision-free path $\tau$ in $C_{free}$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
- **5-tuple:** $\Pi_{MP} = (C, C_{obs}, C_{free}, q_{start}, q_{goal})$.
- **Workspace vs. C-space:** $W$ is physical; $C$ is abstract mathematical pose-space.
- **Minkowski Sum (KEY FORMULA):** $C_{obs} = O \oplus (-R)$.
- **DOF:** 2D rigid body → 3; 3D rigid body → 6; $N$-link arm with revolute joints → $N$.
- **Three classical approaches:** Roadmap, Cell Decomposition, Potential Field.
- **Completeness:** Roadmap and Cell Decomposition are complete; Potential Field is **not** (local minima).
- **Optimality:** Visibility Graph gives shortest path in 2D; RRT\* is asymptotically optimal in high dimensions.
- **Sampling methods:** PRM (multi-query), RRT (single-query), RRT\* (optimal).
- **Clearance $\delta$:** Minimum distance from path to $C_{obs}$; critical for safety in medical and autonomous-vehicle applications.
- **Piano Mover's Problem:** Pure geometric, complete-information, PSPACE-hard in general.
- **Algorithms to memorize:** Visibility Graph ($O(n^{3})$), Voronoi Diagram ($O(n \log n)$), A* in grid, RRT/PRM.
- **Real-world apps:** Self-driving cars, surgical robots, Mars rovers, warehouse automation, gaming NPCs.
- **Examiner's focus areas:** Definition, C-space transformation via Minkowski sum, comparison of planning paradigms, working out a visibility graph problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Search Algorithms - Breadth-First Search (BFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Search Algorithms - Breadth-First Search (BFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is a uniform-cost, uninformed (blind) graph traversal and search strategy that systematically explores a search space by expanding all nodes at the present depth level before proceeding to nodes at the next depth level. Formally, BFS expands the **shallowest unexpanded node** first, where the depth is measured as the number of edges along the path from the root (initial state) to the node.

In the KTU 2024 taxonomy of AI search algorithms, BFS is classified as:
- **Search Class:** Uninformed / Blind Search (no heuristic guidance, i.e., $h(n) = 0$)
- **Strategy Class:** Graph-search with a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) frontier (open list)**
- **Completeness:** Guaranteed (on finite graphs and finite branching factor)
- **Optimality:** Guaranteed only when all step costs are identical (uniform-cost graph)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examiner-Friendly):**
> *"Breadth-First Search is a blind search algorithm that uses a FIFO queue as its frontier. It expands nodes level-by-level from the root, guaranteeing that any goal node reached is via the shortest path in terms of number of steps when all edges have equal cost."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a **circular pond** and a stone has fallen somewhere at the bottom. Instead of diving to one specific spot, you walk around the pond in **expanding concentric circles**:

- **Ring 1 (depth 0):** You check the spot directly where you stand.
- **Ring 2 (depth 1):** You check every location exactly one step away.
- **Ring 3 (depth 2):** Then every location two steps away, and so on.

You keep widening the circle until you spot the stone. This is **exactly how BFS works** on a search graph — it never rushes down a single path; instead, it fans out **uniformly in all directions**, layer by layer.

Another real-world analogy: **Ripples on water surface after a pebble is dropped.** The wavefront expands outward uniformly, touching everything at distance 1 first, then distance 2, and so on.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Metric | Standard Notation | Value for BFS |
|---|---|---|
| Branching Factor | $b$ | Maximum number of successors of any node |
| Depth of Shallowest Goal | $d$ | Edge-count from root to nearest goal |
| Maximum Search Depth | $D$ | Hard upper limit (if defined) |
| Step Cost (uniform) | $c$ | Constant per edge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BFS Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> BFS is the foundational "baseline" algorithm against which every other search strategy (DFS, UCS, A*, Greedy, IDS, Bidirectional) is benchmarked. Mastering BFS properties — particularly its completeness, optimality, and $O(b^d)$ complexity — is **mandatory** for Module 4 questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Structured Step-by-Step

BFS operates on the **general tree-search / graph-search framework**, with one critical structural choice: the **frontier is a FIFO queue** (not a stack, not a priority queue).

### The BFS Operational Pipeline

1. **Initialization:**
   - Create an empty FIFO queue called `Frontier`.
   - Insert the initial state $S_0$ into the Frontier.
   - Initialize an empty `Explored` (closed) set (graph search variant only).

2. **Loop Termination Check:**
   - If Frontier is **empty** → return **FAILURE** (no goal reachable).

3. **Node Selection (FIFO Discipline):**
   - Dequeue the **oldest** node $n$ from the Frontier. This guarantees the shallowest unexpanded node is chosen.

4. **Goal Test:**
   - If $n$ satisfies the `GOAL-TEST`, then return $n$ (with the solution path reconstructed via parent pointers).

5. **Expansion:**
   - For every successor $n'$ of $n$ that is **not already in the Frontier or Explored set**:
     - Compute the path-cost $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$.
     - Set the parent pointer of $n'$ to $n$.
     - Enqueue $n'$ at the **rear** of the Frontier.

6. **Repeat** from step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind FIFO:** A queue enforces *insertion-order retrieval*. Since newly generated nodes are always at depth $= \text{depth}(n) + 1$, and we always retrieve the oldest (shallowest) node first, BFS naturally explores all depth-$k$ nodes before any depth-$(k+1)$ node.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready)

| # | Property | Formula / Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time Complexity** | $T(n) = O(b^d)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal |
| 2 | **Space Complexity** | $S(n) = O(b^d)$ | Frontier + Explored set together |
| 3 | **Completeness** | $\text{Yes}$ (if $b$ is finite) | Guaranteed to find a solution if one exists |
| 4 | **Optimality** | $\text{Yes}$ (only if step costs are uniform) | Finds minimum-depth / minimum-cost path |
| 5 | **Nodes Expanded** | $1 + b + b^2 + b^3 + \dots + b^d = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Geometric series of frontier size |
| 6 | **Path Cost to Goal** | $g(\text{goal}) = d \cdot c$ | For uniform step cost $c$ |
| 7 | **Frontier Size at Depth $k$** | $\vert F \vert = b^{k}$ | After completing exploration of depth $(k-1)$ |
| 8 | **Explored Set Size (Worst Case)** | $\vert E \vert = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | All nodes up to depth $d$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $O(b^d)$ for BFS space complexity. While correct, the **full space** includes the Explored set. The complete expression is $O(b^d)$ because the Explored set is asymptotically bounded by the same geometric series. Do **not** write $O(b^{d-1})$ — that is a frequent valuation deduction.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of BFS

- **Shortest-Path Routing:** Finding the minimum-hop path in communication networks (OSPF protocol uses a Dijkstra-like variant).
- **Social Network Analysis:** Computing the "degree of separation" between two users (e.g., LinkedIn, Facebook).
- **Web Crawlers:** Discovering all hyperlinks reachable within $k$ clicks from a seed URL.
- **Garbage Collection (Cheney's Algorithm):** BFS over object-reference graphs in programming language runtimes.
- **Broadcast in Networks:** Spanning-tree construction for packet flooding.
- **GPS / Map Services:** Finding the minimum-number-of-roads route.
- **AI Planners & Puzzle Solvers:** Solving the 8-puzzle, Rubik's cube layer-by-layer, river-crossing problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive BFS Trace — Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

### Problem Setup

Consider the following **unweighted, undirected graph** $G = (V, E)$ with start node **S** and goal node **G**.

**Adjacency Representation:**
$$S = \{A, B\}, \quad A = \{S, C, D\}, \quad B = \{S, E\}, \quad C = \{A, D, G\}, \quad D = \{A, C\}, \quad E = \{B, G\}, \quad G = \{C, E\}$$

**Objective:** Trace BFS from S to find goal G. Show the frontier, explored set, and discovery tree at each step.

### Step 0 — Initialization

$$\text{Frontier} = [S], \quad \text{Explored} = \{\}, \quad \text{Step} = 0$$

### Step 1 — Dequeue S, Goal Test, Expand

- Dequeue **S**. $S \neq G$, so expand.
- Successors of $S$: $\{A, B\}$.
- Neither $A$ nor $B$ is in Frontier or Explored. Enqueue both.
- Parent: $\text{parent}(A) = S$, $\text{parent}(B) = S$.

$$\text{Frontier} = [A, B], \quad \text{Explored} = \{S\}$$

**Path cost so far:** $g(A) = 1$, $g(B) = 1$.

### Step 2 — Dequeue A, Expand

- Dequeue **A** (oldest in queue = shallowest). $A \neq G$.
- Successors of $A$: $\{S, C, D\}$.
- $S$ is in Explored → skip. $C$ and $D$ are new → enqueue.

$$\text{Frontier} = [B, C, D], \quad \text{Explored} = \{S, A\}$$

**Path cost:** $g(C) = 2$, $g(D) = 2$.

### Step 3 — Dequeue B, Expand

- Dequeue **B**. $B \neq G$.
- Successors of $B$: $\{S, E\}$. $S$ in Explored → skip. $E$ is new → enqueue.

$$\text{Frontier} = [C, D, E], \quad \text{Explored} = \{S, A, B\}$$

**Path cost:** $g(E) = 2$.

### Step 4 — Dequeue C, Expand

- Dequeue **C**. $C \neq G$.
- Successors of $C$: $\{A, D, G\}$. $A$ in Explored; $D$ already in Frontier; **$G$ is new** → enqueue.

$$\text{Frontier} = [D, E, G], \quad \text{Explored} = \{S, A, B, C\}$$

**Path cost:** $g(G) = 3$, $\text{parent}(G) = C$.

### Step 5 — Dequeue D, Goal Test (Optional)

- Dequeue **D**. $D \neq G$. Expand successors — all already known. No new enqueues.

$$\text{Frontier} = [E, G], \quad \text{Explored} = \{S, A, B, C, D\}$$

### Step 6 — Dequeue E, Expand

- Dequeue **E**. $E \neq G$.
- Successors of $E$: $\{B, G\}$. $B$ in Explored; $G$ **already in Frontier** → skip (graph search).

$$\text{Frontier} = [G], \quad \text{Explored} = \{S, A, B, C, D, E\}$$

### Step 7 — Dequeue G, GOAL TEST

- Dequeue **G**. $G$ satisfies the goal test → **SUCCESS**.

### 3.2 Reconstructing the Solution Path

Tracing parent pointers backward: $G \leftarrow C \leftarrow A \leftarrow S$.

$$\boxed{\text{Solution Path: } S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G, \quad g = 3}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Dequeued | Frontier (after) | Explored (after) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | [S] | {} | Initialize |
| 1 | S | [A, B] | {S} | Enqueue successors |
| 2 | A | [B, C, D] | {S, A} | Enqueue C, D |
| 3 | B | [C, D, E] | {S, A, B} | Enqueue E |
| 4 | C | [D, E, G] | {S, A, B, C} | Enqueue G |
| 5 | D | [E, G] | {S, A, B, C, D} | No new nodes |
| 6 | E | [G] | {S, A, B, C, D, E} | G already in frontier |
| 7 | G | [] | — | **GOAL REACHED** |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Geometric-Series Node Count

The total number of nodes generated by BFS up to depth $d$ follows a geometric series. We derive it explicitly:

$$N_{\text{total}} = b^0 + b^1 + b^2 + \cdots + b^d = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k$$

Using the closed-form formula for a finite geometric series with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = b$:

$$N_{\text{total}} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{(for } b \neq 1\text{)}$$

For large $d$, the dominant term is $b^d$, which yields the asymptotic bound:

$$N_{\text{total}} = O(b^d)$$

This confirms both the **time** and **space** complexity bounds in the formula sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The geometric-series derivation is a frequent 2-3 mark sub-question. Memorize both the closed form and the asymptotic $O(b^d)$ bound.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Input Graph Used in the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS((S)) --- nodeA((A))
    nodeS((S)) --- nodeB((B))
    nodeA((A)) --- nodeC((C))
    nodeA((A)) --- nodeD((D))
    nodeC((C)) --- nodeD((D))
    nodeC((C)) --- nodeG((G))
    nodeB((B)) --- nodeE((E))
    nodeE((E)) --- nodeG((G))
```

## 4.2 BFS Discovery Tree (Layered Expansion)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootS["S depth 0"] --> aA["A depth 1"]
    rootS --> aB["B depth 1"]
    aA --> aC["C depth 2"]
    aA --> aD["D depth 2"]
    aB --> aE["E depth 2"]
    aC --> aG["G depth 3 GOAL"]
```

## 4.3 BFS Functional Architecture Flow (Algorithmic Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start] --> A1[Initialize FIFO Frontier with S]
    A1 --> A2{Is Frontier empty?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Return FAILURE]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Dequeue oldest node n]
    A4 --> A5{Is n the GOAL?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Return SUCCESS with path]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Generate successors of n]
    A7 --> A8{For each successor n prime}
    A8 --> A9{n prime in Frontier or Explored?}
    A9 -- Yes --> A8
    A9 -- No --> A10[Set parent and path cost g of n prime]
    A10 --> A11[Enqueue n prime at REAR of Frontier]
    A11 --> A8
    A8 -- All successors processed --> A2
```

## 4.4 FIFO Queue State Transitions (From the Worked Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q0["Step 0: S"]
    q1["Step 1: A,B"]
    q2["Step 2: B,C,D"]
    q3["Step 3: C,D,E"]
    q4["Step 4: D,E,G"]
    q5["Step 5: E,G"]
    q6["Step 6: G"]
    q7["Step 7: GOAL"]
    q0 --> q1 --> q2 --> q3 --> q4 --> q5 --> q6 --> q7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

### Q1. Define Breadth-First Search. State its completeness and optimality conditions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
BFS is an uninformed search algorithm that uses a FIFO queue to expand the **shallowest unexpanded node** at each step, traversing the search space level-by-level from the root. **Completeness:** BFS is complete — it is guaranteed to find a goal state if one exists, provided the branching factor $b$ is finite. **Optimality:** BFS is optimal (finds the minimum-depth solution) only when all step costs are uniform / identical. In weighted graphs where edge costs differ, BFS may return a suboptimal (deeper but cheaper) path — Uniform-Cost Search is required instead. **(3 marks)**

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

### Q2. Differentiate BFS and DFS with respect to data structure used and memory requirement.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Frontier Data Structure | **FIFO Queue** | **LIFO Stack** |
| Memory (Space) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ where $m$ is max depth |
| Optimal? | Yes (uniform cost) | No |
| Complete? | Yes (finite $b$) | No (can loop on cycles) |

BFS stores the entire explored frontier (an exponentially growing level), while DFS stores only the path from root to current node plus siblings. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *[CHOICE 1]*

**(a)** Explain the Breadth-First Search algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss its completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply BFS on the following graph. Start node = **S**, Goal node = **G**. Show the frontier (queue), explored set, and the final solution path at every step. The graph is given below: **(7 Marks)**

**Adjacency:**
$$S = \{A, D\},\ A = \{S, B\},\ B = \{A, C, E\},\ C = \{B, G\},\ D = \{S, E\},\ E = \{B, D, G\},\ G = \{C, E\}$$

---

### ✅ Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) Algorithm + Properties (7 Marks)

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
```
BFS-Search(problem):
    node ← NODE(problem.INITIAL)
    if problem.GOAL-TEST(node.state): return node
    Frontier ← FIFO-queue with node
    Explored ← empty set
    loop:
        if Frontier is empty: return FAILURE
        node ← Frontier.pop()                   // dequeue oldest
        Explored.add(node)
        for each action in problem.ACTIONS(node.state):
            child ← CHILD-NODE(problem, node, action)
            if child.state NOT IN Explored AND NOT IN Frontier:
                if problem.GOAL-TEST(child.state): return child
                Frontier.push(child)             // enqueue at rear
```

**Properties Table:**

| Property | BFS Value |
|---|---|
| Complete? | **Yes** (finite $b$) |
| Optimal? | **Yes** (uniform step cost) |
| Time | $O(b^d)$ |
| Space | $O(b^d)$ |

**Flowchart:** Refer to **Section 4.3** above. **(7 marks distributed as: Algorithm 3, Properties 2, Flowchart 2)**

#### (b) BFS Trace (7 Marks)

| Step | Dequeue | Frontier (after) | Explored (after) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | [S] | {} |
| 1 | S | [A, D] | {S} |
| 2 | A | [D, B] | {S, A} |
| 3 | D | [B, E] | {S, A, D} |
| 4 | B | [E, C] | {S, A, D, B} |
| 5 | E | [C, G] | {S, A, D, B, E} |
| 6 | C | [G, — ] | {S, A, D, B, E, C} |
| 7 | G | — | **GOAL** |

**Solution Path (via parent pointers):** $G \leftarrow C \leftarrow B \leftarrow A \leftarrow S$
$$\boxed{\text{Path: } S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G, \quad g = 4}$$
**(7 marks distributed as: Table 5, Path reconstruction 2)**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *[CHOICE 2]*

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the working of BFS algorithm. Mention the data structure used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robot is in a 3×3 grid (cells numbered 1-9). Cell 1 is the start, cell 9 is the goal. The robot can move Up/Down/Left/Right. Apply BFS to find the shortest path from cell 1 to cell 9. Show the search tree. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Question B — Model Solution Outline

#### (a) Working + Data Structure (7 Marks)
- **Definition + FIFO Queue explanation (2 marks)**
- **Worked example using a small tree/graph with step-by-step queue state transitions (4 marks)**
- **Conclusion: BFS finds shallowest goal first (1 mark)**

#### (b) Grid BFS (7 Marks)
- 3×3 grid adjacency construction (1 mark)
- BFS levels: Level 0 = {1}; Level 1 = {2, 4}; Level 2 = {3, 5, 7}; Level 3 = {6, 8, 9} (3 marks)
- Shortest path reconstructed: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 9$ (or $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 9$ or similar) (2 marks)
- Search tree diagram (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on BFS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing BFS Optimality in Weighted Graphs:** Students write "BFS is always optimal" — **DEDUCT 1 MARK.** BFS is optimal *only* with uniform step costs.
> 2. **Writing Space Complexity as $O(b^d)$ without justification:** Examiners expect you to mention *both* the frontier (queue) and the explored (closed) set. Vague answers lose 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to reconstruct the solution path:** When tracing BFS, you MUST show the parent-pointer chain. A trace that only states "G is found at step 7" without showing the path $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Using DFS-like stack notation:** Some students mistakenly write "pop the most recent node" — this is DFS, not BFS. The word **FIFO / Queue** is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Skipping the goal test in the trace:** Always check the goal test *before* expanding a node.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ BFS = **Shallowest-first expansion** using a **FIFO queue**.
- ⭐ **Complete:** Yes if branching factor $b$ is finite.
- ⭐ **Optimal:** Yes **only** for uniform step costs.
- ⭐ **Time Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ where $d$ = depth of shallowest goal.
- ⭐ **Space Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ — this is BFS's biggest weakness (memory-intensive).
- ⭐ **Frontier size at depth $k$** is $b^k$ (geometric growth).
- ⭐ **Total nodes generated** $= \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$.
- ⭐ **Graph search** (with Explored set) prevents redundant visits; **Tree search** does not.
- ⭐ **Goal test** is applied when a node is *dequeued*, not when it is *generated* (graph search variant).
- ⭐ BFS finds **shortest path in number of edges**, not in weighted cost — that is UCS.
- ⭐ Applications: shortest path (unweighted), level-order traversal, connected components, bipartiteness check, web crawling.
- ⭐ **Comparison hook for exam:** BFS uses more memory than DFS but guarantees shortest path; DFS uses less memory but is neither complete nor optimal.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A* Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** A* Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**A* Search** is a best-first, informed (heuristic) graph search algorithm that finds the least-cost path from a given initial/start node to a goal node in a weighted graph. It evaluates nodes by combining the actual cost incurred to reach the node from the start with an estimated cost to reach the goal from that node, using the evaluation function:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = the cost of the path from the start node to node $n$ (actual, known cost).
- $h(n)$ = the heuristic estimated cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal (estimated cost, problem-specific).
- $f(n)$ = the total estimated cost of the cheapest solution passing through node $n$.

A* maintains two sets of nodes: the **OPEN list** (frontier — nodes discovered but not yet expanded, ordered by $f$-value) and the **CLOSED list** (explored — nodes already expanded). At each iteration, the node with the lowest $f(n)$ in OPEN is selected, expanded, and its successors are inserted (or updated) in OPEN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A* is treated as the canonical example of an *informed search* strategy in the **Search Strategies** module. Expect direct questions on admissibility, consistency, optimality, and completeness in Part A (3 marks) and trace-based/algorithm-based questions in Part B (14 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi (start)** to **Trivandrum (goal)**. Your GPS navigation system continuously estimates:
- $g(n)$ = the kilometers you have *already* driven so far (actual).
- $h(n)$ = the straight-line ("as-the-crow-flies") remaining distance to Trivandrum (estimated).
- $f(n)$ = the GPS's *best guess* of the total journey length if you continue on the current road through your current location.

The GPS always picks the next junction with the **smallest estimated total trip length** ($f$-value) to investigate first. If the straight-line estimate $h(n)$ is **never an overestimate** (i.e., roads are never shorter than the bird's-eye view — which is geometrically true), then the GPS is *guaranteed* to suggest a route no longer than the truly optimal one. That is the heart of A* search: **"search smarter, not harder, by being honest about the optimism of your heuristic."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Memorize:**
> - **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS):** uses $f(n) = g(n)$ — ignores the goal direction. Optimal but slow.
> - **Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS):** uses $f(n) = h(n)$ — ignores the past. Fast but not optimal.
> - **A\* Search:** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — *balances the past and the future*. Optimal **and** efficient (provided $h$ is admissible).

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Heuristic function $h(n)$** | Estimated cost from $n$ to a goal | Problem-specific "rule of thumb" |
| **Admissible heuristic** | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$ (never overestimates) | Guarantees A\* optimality |
| **Consistent (monotonic) heuristic** | $h(n) \le c(n, n', n'') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$ | Stronger — guarantees $f$-values are non-decreasing along any path |
| **Dominated heuristic** | $h_2(n) \ge h_1(n)$ for all $n$ with $h_2$ still admissible | $h_2$ is *more informed* → expands fewer nodes |
| **OPEN list** | Priority queue of frontier nodes keyed on $f(n)$ | Implementation: min-heap or priority queue |
| **CLOSED list** | Set of already-expanded nodes | Prevents re-expansion of revisited nodes |
| **Pathmax** | Tie-breaking rule: $f(\text{successor}) \ge f(\text{parent})$ | Used in graphs with non-monotonic heuristics |
| **Pruning** | Discarding nodes that cannot lie on an optimal path | Reduces search space drastically |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** A* is *optimally efficient* — no other optimal algorithm using the same heuristic can expand fewer nodes than A*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Step-by-Step Logic

A* is essentially a controlled marriage of **Dijkstra's algorithm** (which expands in order of $g(n)$) and **Greedy Best-First Search** (which expands in order of $h(n)$). The following bullet-by-bullet breakdown is the precise sequence examiners expect you to write in KTU board answers:

1. **Initialization**
   - Set $g(\text{start}) = 0$.
   - Compute $f(\text{start}) = g(\text{start}) + h(\text{start}) = h(\text{start})$.
   - Insert start into **OPEN**. Initialize **CLOSED** as empty.

2. **Termination Check**
   - If OPEN is empty → **failure** (no path exists).
   - If the node selected is a goal → **success**; reconstruct path via parent pointers.

3. **Node Selection**
   - Remove from OPEN the node $n$ with the **lowest $f(n)$** (ties broken arbitrarily, or by lower $h$, or by higher $g$ — your tie-breaking rule must be declared).
   - Add $n$ to CLOSED.

4. **Goal Test (early vs. late)**
   - *Early goal test:* check when a node is *generated* (before insertion into OPEN).
   - *Late goal test:* check when a node is *selected* from OPEN for expansion.
   - Late goal test is generally preferred with consistent heuristics.

5. **Expansion of $n$**
   - For every successor $n'$ of $n$ via action $a$ with step cost $c(n, a, n')$:
     - Compute $g_{\text{new}} = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$.
     - Compute $f_{\text{new}} = g_{\text{new}} + h(n')$.

6. **Membership & Update Logic**
   - If $n'$ is already in CLOSED with a cost $\le g_{\text{new}}$ → **discard** the new path.
   - If $n'$ is in OPEN with a cost $\le g_{\text{new}}$ → **discard** the new path.
   - Otherwise: set $g(n') = g_{\text{new}}$, set parent, compute $f(n')$, and insert/replace $n'$ in OPEN.

7. **Loop back to Step 2.**

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why priority queue on $f(n)$?** It guarantees that the first time a goal is *dequeued* with consistent heuristics, the path cost equals the optimal path cost — no later path can beat it.
- **Why track CLOSED separately?** Without it, the same node could be re-expanded multiple times via different paths, violating optimality in graphs with cycles.
- **Why prefer consistent heuristics?** They make A* *equivalent to running Dijkstra on a graph where edge costs include the heuristic*. Hence $f$-values are non-decreasing along any path, and the algorithm never needs to re-open a CLOSED node.
- **Why does A* avoid re-expansion with consistent $h$?** The triangular inequality of the heuristic ensures $f(n) \le f(\text{successor})$, so once a node is expanded (removed from OPEN), no shorter path to it can later emerge.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Meaning | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|--------------------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost via $n$ | ★★★ |
| 2 | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ (Admissibility) | Heuristic never overestimates true cost to goal | ★★★ |
| 3 | $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ (Consistency) | Triangle inequality on heuristic | ★★★ |
| 4 | $f(\text{goal}) = C^*$ (Optimality) | First goal dequeued has optimal cost $C^*$ | ★★ |
| 5 | Branching factor effective: $b^*$ | A\* expands nodes with $f(n) < C^*$ | ★★ |
| 6 | $h_{\text{Euclidean}}(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | Straight-line distance heuristic (8-puzzle grids: $h = $ Manhattan distance) | ★★ |
| 7 | Tie-breaking: prefer node with higher $g(n)$ (lower $h$) | Among equal $f$, expand the one closest to goal | ★ |
| 8 | $f(n) \le f(n')$ along any path (for consistent $h$) | $f$-cost is non-decreasing — enables CLOSED pruning | ★★ |
| 9 | Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*): $f$-bounded DFS | Memory-efficient variant | ★ |
| 10 | Simplified Memory-Bounded A\* (SMA\*): drops worst leaf when memory full | Bounded-memory variant | ★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** You will almost always be asked to *trace* A* on a small graph (typically 5–8 nodes) with a *given heuristic table*. Memorize the $f$-value arithmetic and parent-pointer updates — they form the core of the 7-mark sub-question in Part B.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS & Route Planning:** Google Maps, OpenStreetMap routers, autonomous vehicle path planning.
- **Game AI:** Pathfinding for non-player characters (NPCs) on tile-based maps (e.g., StarCraft, Civilization).
- **Robotics:** Motion planning in ROS (MoveIt) using A* over occupancy grids.
- **Network Routing & Puzzles:** Rubik's Cube solvers, 8-puzzle, 15-puzzle, sliding-tile problems.
- **Bioinformatics & Logistics:** Protein-ligand docking route planning, warehouse robot navigation, supply-chain path optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Admissibility Guarantees Optimality (Sketch Proof)

We will prove: *If $h(n)$ is admissible, A* (with a consistent tie-break) returns an optimal solution when one exists.*

**Claim 1.** Suppose A* has selected a goal node $G_2$ (a non-optimal one) for expansion. There exists some optimal goal $G_1$ on OPEN with $f(G_1) = g(G_1) + h(G_1) = C^* + 0 = C^*$, since $h(G_1) = 0$ for a goal and $g(G_1) = C^*$ by optimality.

**Claim 2.** Because $G_2$ was selected before $G_1$, we must have $f(G_2) \le f(G_1)$, i.e., $g(G_2) + h(G_2) \le C^*$. But $h(G_2) = 0$ (goal), so $g(G_2) \le C^*$, meaning $G_2$ is also optimal — contradiction.

**Claim 3.** The contradiction establishes that A* cannot select a non-optimal goal before an optimal one, provided all edges have non-negative cost and $h$ is admissible.

### 3.2 Worked Example: A* Trace on a 5-Node Graph

**Graph Data:**

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| S → A | 1 |
| S → B | 4 |
| A → B | 1 |
| A → C | 3 |
| B → C | 2 |
| B → D | 5 |
| C → D | 1 |
| C → G | 6 |
| D → G | 2 |

**Heuristic Table (straight-line estimates to G):**

| Node | $h$ |
|------|-----|
| S | 7 |
| A | 6 |
| B | 4 |
| C | 2 |
| D | 1 |
| G | 0 |

**Goal:** Find shortest path S → G.

**Initialization:**
- OPEN = $\{(S, f=0+7=7)\}$, CLOSED = $\{\}$, parent(S) = None.

**Iteration 1:** Select S ($f=7$). Expand S.
- Successor A: $g = 0+1 = 1$, $f = 1+6 = 7$. Not in OPEN/CLOSED → insert.
- Successor B: $g = 0+4 = 4$, $f = 4+4 = 8$. Insert.
- OPEN = $\{(A,7), (B,8)\}$, CLOSED = $\{S\}$.

**Iteration 2:** Select A ($f=7$). Expand A.
- Successor B (via A): $g = 1+1 = 2$, $f = 2+4 = 6$. B already in OPEN with $f=8$. **Update B** to $f=6$, parent = A.
- Successor C (via A): $g = 1+3 = 4$, $f = 4+2 = 6$. Insert C.
- OPEN = $\{(B,6), (C,6), (B,8)\text{ (stale)}\}$ → after dedup, $\{(B,6), (C,6)\}$. CLOSED = $\{S, A\}$.

**Iteration 3:** Tie between B and C. Use tie-break: higher $g$ (closer to goal). B has $g=2$, C has $g=4$. **Select C** ($f=6$, higher $g$). Expand C.
- Successor D (via C): $g = 4+1 = 5$, $f = 5+1 = 6$. Insert D.
- Successor G (via C): $g = 4+6 = 10$, $f = 10+0 = 10$. Insert G.
- OPEN = $\{(B,6), (D,6), (G,10)\}$. CLOSED = $\{S, A, C\}$.

**Iteration 4:** Tie B vs D. B has $g=2$, D has $g=5$. **Select D** ($f=6$, higher $g$). Expand D.
- Successor G (via D): $g = 5+2 = 7$, $f = 7+0 = 7$. G already in OPEN with $f=10$. **Update G** to $f=7$, parent = D.
- OPEN = $\{(B,6), (G,7)\}$. CLOSED = $\{S, A, C, D\}$.

**Iteration 5:** Select B ($f=6$). Expand B.
- Successor C (via B): $g = 2+2 = 4$, $f = 4+2 = 6$. C already CLOSED with $g=4$ via A. Equal — no update.
- Successor D (via B): $g = 2+5 = 7$, $f = 7+1 = 8$. D already CLOSED with $g=5$ via C. Discard (worse).
- OPEN = $\{(G,7)\}$. CLOSED = $\{S, A, C, D, B\}$.

**Iteration 6:** Select G ($f=7$). **GOAL TEST → success!**

**Result:** Path reconstructed via parents: G ← D ← C ← A ← S, total cost = $1+3+1+2 = 7$.

Let us verify the shortest path by brute force:
- S→A→B→C→G = $1+1+2+6 = 10$
- S→A→C→G = $1+3+6 = 10$
- S→A→C→D→G = $1+3+1+2 = 7$ ✓
- S→B→C→D→G = $4+2+1+2 = 9$
- S→A→B→D→G = $1+1+5+2 = 9$

A* correctly returned the optimal path S → A → C → D → G with total cost **7**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties of A\* and Variants

| Algorithm | $f(n)$ | Optimality | Completeness | Time | Space | Use Case |
|-----------|--------|------------|--------------|------|-------|----------|
| Uniform-Cost (UCS) | $g(n)$ | Yes | Yes* | Exponential | Exponential | Uninformed, edge-cost focused |
| Greedy Best-First | $h(n)$ | No | No* | Exponential | Exponential | Fast, goal-directed |
| A\* | $g(n)+h(n)$ | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | Yes* | Exponential | Exponential | Balanced, optimal |
| Weighted A\* ($W > 1$) | $g(n)+W\cdot h(n)$ | $\epsilon$-optimal | Yes* | Faster | Same | Time-bounded, suboptimal solutions |
| Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*) | Iterative $f$-threshold DFS | Yes (if $h$ admissible) | Yes* | Polynomial-ish | $O(bd)$ linear | Memory-constrained |
| Simplified Memory-Bounded A\* (SMA\*) | $g(n)+h(n)$ with memory cap | Yes (within memory) | Yes (within memory) | Bounded by memory | User-specified bound | Long paths in limited RAM |
| Beam Search | $g(n)+h(n)$ with width $k$ | No (approximate) | No | Reduced | $O(kd)$ | NLP, large state spaces |

(*Complete only if edge costs $\ge \varepsilon > 0$ and branching factor is finite.)

### 3.4 Comparison of Common Heuristics (8-Puzzle / Grid Domains)

| Domain | Heuristic | Admissible? | Consistent? | Notes |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| 8-puzzle | $h_1$ = # misplaced tiles | Yes | Yes | Simplest, less informed |
| 8-puzzle | $h_2$ = Manhattan distance | Yes | Yes | Dominates $h_1$ → fewer expansions |
| 8-puzzle | $h_3$ = Linear conflict | Yes | Yes | Strictly dominates $h_2$ |
| Maps | Euclidean distance | Yes (if $\ge$ true cost) | Yes (if metric) | Used in 2D grids |
| Maps | Manhattan distance | Yes (if 4-connected) | Yes | Used in city-block routing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic Dominance Lemma:** If $h_1(n) \le h_2(n)$ for all $n$ and both are admissible, then $h_2$ *dominates* $h_1$ and A* with $h_2$ will expand **no more nodes** than A* with $h_1$. This is a favourite 3-mark "compare and justify" question in KTU boards.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of A\* Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start Node S]) --> init[Initialize OPEN and CLOSED]
    init --> pushStart["Push S with f = h S into OPEN"]
    pushStart --> openEmptyCheck{OPEN empty?}
    openEmptyCheck -- Yes --> fail([Return FAILURE no path])
    openEmptyCheck -- No --> popBest[Pop node n with minimum f from OPEN]
    popBest --> goalCheck{Is n a goal node?}
    goalCheck -- Yes --> success([Return path and cost])
    goalCheck -- No --> addClosed[Add n to CLOSED]
    addClosed --> expandGen[Generate successors n prime of n]
    expandGen --> loopStart{For each successor n prime}
    loopStart --> gCalc["Compute g_new = g of n plus c of n to n prime"]
    gCalc --> fCalc["Compute f_new = g_new plus h of n prime"]
    fCalc --> skipCheck{Is n prime in CLOSED with lower or equal g?}
    skipCheck -- Yes --> loopStart
    skipCheck -- No --> openCheck{Is n prime in OPEN with lower or equal g?}
    openCheck -- Yes --> loopStart
    openCheck -- No --> updateInsert["Set parent and g of n prime, insert or update in OPEN"]
    updateInsert --> loopStart
    loopStart -- Done all successors --> openEmptyCheck
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Admissibility vs. Consistency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hDef["Heuristic h of n"] --> q1{Does h of n always underestimate true cost h star of n?}
    q1 -- Yes --> admissible["Admissible h"]
    q1 -- No --> notAdmissible["Not Admissible - A star NOT guaranteed optimal"]
    admissible --> q2{Does h satisfy triangle inequality h of n less or equal c plus h of n prime?}
    q2 -- Yes --> consistent["Consistent Monotonic h"]
    q2 -- No --> weaklyAdmissible["Admissible but NOT consistent - need Pathmax or reopen nodes"]
    consistent --> a1["A star optimal, complete, and avoids re-opening CLOSED nodes"]
    weaklyAdmissible --> a2["A star optimal, but may need to re-open CLOSED nodes"]
```

### 4.3 Worked Trace: Visualisation of Section 3.2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((S h=7)) -- "1" --> A((A h=6))
    S -- "4" --> B((B h=4))
    A -- "1" --> B
    A -- "3" --> C((C h=2))
    B -- "2" --> C
    B -- "5" --> D((D h=1))
    C -- "1" --> D
    C -- "6" --> goalNode((G h=0))
    D -- "2" --> goalNode
    style S fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333
    style goalNode fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FF9999,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading guide:** Orange nodes are *expanded* in the optimal A* path; green is the *goal*; red is *expanded but not on optimal path* (a classic demonstration that A\* still expands a few non-optimal nodes if the heuristic is weakly informed).

### 4.4 Search Tree Showing $f$, $g$, $h$ Annotations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n0["S  f=7  g=0  h=7"]
    n1["A  f=7  g=1  h=6"]
    n2["B  f=8  g=4  h=4"]
    n3["C  f=6  g=4  h=2"]
    n4["B  f=6  g=2  h=4"]
    n5["D  f=6  g=5  h=1"]
    n6["G  f=7  g=7  h=0"]
    n7["G  f=10  g=10  h=0"]
    n0 --> n1
    n0 --> n2
    n1 --> n4
    n1 --> n3
    n3 --> n5
    n3 --> n7
    n5 --> n6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define an admissible heuristic. Why is admissibility important for the optimality of A\* search?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if, for every node $n$, $h(n) \le h^*(n)$, where $h^*(n)$ is the *true* minimum cost from $n$ to a goal.
- (1 mark) **Why:** If $h$ is admissible, then for any optimal goal $G$ on OPEN, $f(G) = g(G) + h(G) = C^* + 0 = C^*$. A* always expands the lowest-$f$ node, so the first goal it dequeued must be optimal.
- (1 mark) **Corollary:** Admissibility is a sufficient condition for A* to return an optimal solution on finite graphs with non-negative edge costs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Differentiate between Uniform-Cost Search and A\* Search. When does A\* degenerate into UCS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **UCS** uses $f(n) = g(n)$ — purely backward-looking, ignores the goal direction.
- (1 mark) **A\*** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — combines actual cost with a heuristic forward estimate.
- (1 mark) **Degeneration:** A\* behaves identically to UCS when $h(n) = 0$ for all nodes (i.e., the heuristic provides no information). It also degenerates when the heuristic is uninformative (e.g., a constant) on infinite-uniform-cost branches.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Forgetting to **update parent pointers** when a cheaper $g$ is found → lose 2–3 marks.
> - Confusing *admissibility* with *consistency* in theoretical sub-parts → lose 1–2 marks.
> - Failing to declare **tie-breaking** rules during trace → lose 1 mark (board style penalty).
> - Not stating **termination condition** (OPEN empty / goal reached) → lose 1 mark.
> - Skipping the *re-construction* of the final path from parent pointers → lose 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]

Consider the following graph. Each edge has a step cost as labelled. Heuristic values are given in the table. Apply **A\* search** starting from S, with goal G. Use the tie-breaking rule: prefer higher $g(n)$, then lower $h(n)$.

**Graph Edges:**
- S→A: 1, S→B: 5, A→B: 2, A→C: 4, B→C: 1, B→D: 6, C→D: 2, C→G: 7, D→G: 3.

**Heuristic Table:**

| Node | S | A | B | C | D | G |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h$ | 8 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 1 | 0 |

**(a) Trace the complete A\* algorithm step-by-step, showing OPEN, CLOSED, and parent pointers at every iteration. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
1. **Init:** OPEN = $\{(S, f=8)\}$, CLOSED = $\{\}$, parent(S) = None. (½ mark)
2. **Iter 1:** Pop S ($f=8$). Expand. S→A: $g=1, f=1+6=7$. S→B: $g=5, f=5+4=9$. OPEN = $\{(A,7), (B,9)\}$, CLOSED = $\{S\}$. (1 mark)
3. **Iter 2:** Pop A ($f=7$). Expand. A→B: $g=1+2=3, f=3+4=7$ → update B (was 9, now 7, parent A). A→C: $g=1+4=5, f=5+2=7$ → insert C. OPEN = $\{(B,7), (C,7)\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A\}$. (1 mark)
4. **Iter 3:** Tie B vs C. B: $g=3$; C: $g=5$. **Select C** (higher $g$). Expand. C→D: $g=5+2=7, f=7+1=8$ → insert D. C→G: $g=5+7=12, f=12$ → insert G. OPEN = $\{(B,7), (D,8), (G,12)\}$. (1 mark)
5. **Iter 4:** Pop B ($f=7$). Expand. B→C: $g=3+1=4, f=4+2=6$. C is in CLOSED with $g=5$ — no improvement (since new $g=4 < 5$, but C is closed; with a **non-consistent** scenario we would reopen, but here $h$ is consistent so C's $g$ via B is worse along $f$). Actually with consistent $h$, re-check: $f(C\text{ via B}) = 6 < f(C\text{ via A}) = 7$ — C is already closed. **Skip** (consistent heuristic, no reopening). B→D: $g=3+6=9, f=9+1=10$. D in OPEN with $f=8$ → discard. OPEN = $\{(D,8), (G,12)\}$. (1 mark)
6. **Iter 5:** Pop D ($f=8$). Expand. D→G: $g=7+3=10, f=10+0=10$. G in OPEN with $f=12$ → update G ($f=10$, parent D). OPEN = $\{(G,10)\}$. (1 mark)
7. **Iter 6:** Pop G ($f=10$). **GOAL.** Path = G ← D ← C ← A ← S, cost = $1+4+2+3 = 10$. (1 mark)

**Final Path:** S → A → C → D → G, total cost = **10**.

**(b) Verify the admissibility and consistency of $h$. State the conditions under which A\* is guaranteed to be complete and optimal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) **Admissibility check:** Compute $h^*$ (true shortest distance to G) for each node.
  - $h^*(S) = 10 \ge 8 = h(S)$ ✓
  - $h^*(A) = 9 \ge 6 = h(A)$ ✓
  - $h^*(B) = 7 \ge 4 = h(B)$ ✓
  - $h^*(C) = 5 \ge 2 = h(C)$ ✓
  - $h^*(D) = 3 \ge 1 = h(D)$ ✓
  - $h^*(G) = 0 = h(G)$ ✓
  - Hence $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$ — **$h$ is admissible**.
- (2 marks) **Consistency check:** Verify $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every edge.
  - $h(S)=8 \le 1+h(A)=7$? $8 \le 7$? **No, fails.** Hence $h$ is **not consistent** for this graph (the heuristic is *too optimistic* at S, it overestimates with respect to going S→A).
  - Other checks: $h(A)=6 \le 2+h(B)=6$ ✓ (tight); $h(A)=6 \le 4+h(C)=6$ ✓; $h(B)=4 \le 1+h(C)=3$? $4 \le 3$? **Fails.** So $h$ is *admissible but not consistent*.
- (2 marks) **Completeness conditions:** A* is **complete** if the branching factor $b$ is finite and every edge cost $\ge \varepsilon > 0$ (positive constant). This prevents infinite loops on zero-cost edges.
- (1 mark) **Optimality conditions:** A* is **optimal** if $h$ is admissible (sufficient) **or** $h$ is consistent (which is stronger and implies admissibility). With a non-consistent $h$, A* may need to re-open CLOSED nodes; this still yields optimality but increases computation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]

**(a) Explain the concept of a heuristic function. With an example (8-puzzle), compare two admissible heuristics $h_1$ (misplaced tiles) and $h_2$ (Manhattan distance). Show that $h_2$ dominates $h_1$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) **Heuristic definition:** A heuristic $h(n)$ is a function that estimates the cost of the cheapest path from node $n$ to a goal. In a puzzle context, it is a relaxation of the original problem.
- (2 marks) **Example 8-puzzle:** Each tile moves orthogonally one cell at a time. Current state vs. goal state.
  - $h_1(n)$ = number of tiles *out of place* (excluding the blank).
  - $h_2(n)$ = sum over all tiles of the *Manhattan distance* (grid steps) between current and goal positions.
- (2 marks) **Admissibility argument:** Every misplaced tile must move at least one step, so $h_1 \le h^*$. Every tile must travel at least its Manhattan distance, and tiles move independently in the relaxation, so $h_2 \le h^*$. Both are admissible.
- (1 mark) **Domination:** For any non-goal state, every misplaced tile contributes $\ge 1$ to $h_2$ and exactly $1$ to $h_1$. So $h_2(n) \ge h_1(n)$ for all $n$. Hence A* with $h_2$ expands a subset of nodes expanded by A* with $h_1$ (i.e., $h_2$ dominates $h_1$).

**(b) Prove that A\* is optimally efficient among all algorithms using the same heuristic. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) **Definition of optimally efficient:** No other optimal algorithm can expand fewer nodes than A* on a given problem using the same heuristic.
- (2 marks) **Setup of proof:** Let $A$ be any optimal algorithm. Suppose A* expands a node $n$ that $A$ does not. Then A* must have done so because $f(n) < C^*$, where $C^*$ is the optimal solution cost.
- (1.5 marks) **Contradiction:** If $f(n) < C^*$, then any optimal solution passing through $n$ has cost $\ge C^*$, so $n$ lies on a non-optimal branch. Any algorithm that returns an optimal solution must eventually *discard* $n$ — either by generating it and pruning, or by checking it. The act of generating $n$ *is* expansion. Hence any optimal algorithm must expand at least as many nodes with $f < C^*$.
- (1.5 marks) **Conclusion:** A* expands *all* nodes with $f(n) < C^*$ and no others. Any optimal algorithm must expand at least these. Hence A* is optimally efficient.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — A* Search**

- **Core equation:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- **Admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ — heuristic is *optimistic*.
- **Consistency (monotonicity):** $h(n) \le c(n, n', n'') + h(n')$ — stronger than admissibility; guarantees $f$ is non-decreasing along paths.
- **Optimality:** A* is optimal if $h$ is admissible (with consistent tie-breaking).
- **Completeness:** A* is complete if $b$ is finite and edge costs are bounded below by a positive $\varepsilon$.
- **Optimally efficient:** No other optimal algorithm expands fewer nodes using the same heuristic.
- **Heuristic dominance:** If $h_2(n) \ge h_1(n)$ for all $n$ and both admissible, $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ and leads to fewer expansions.
- **Data structures:** OPEN = priority queue (min-heap) keyed on $f(n)$; CLOSED = hash set keyed on node identity.
- **Tie-breaking conventions:** Lower $f$ first; on equal $f$, prefer higher $g$ (closer to goal) or lower $h$.
- **Goal test timing:** Late goal test is preferred with consistent $h$ (avoids suboptimal goal claims).
- **Variants:** Weighted A* (suboptimal, faster), IDA* (linear memory), SMA* (memory-bounded).
- **Common heuristics:** Euclidean (2D), Manhattan (4-connected grids), Misplaced tiles, Linear conflict (8-puzzle).
- **Real-world domains:** GPS, robotics, game AI, network routing, puzzle solvers.
- **Common pitfalls:** (i) Forgetting parent-pointer updates, (ii) failing to check membership in both OPEN and CLOSED, (iii) not declaring tie-breaking, (iv) confusing admissible with consistent, (v) mis-computing $g_{\text{new}}$ as $g(n')$ instead of $g(n) + c(n, n')$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRAT404 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Programming (DP)** is a class of algorithms that systematically solve complex, sequential decision-making problems by breaking them down into simpler overlapping sub-problems, storing the solution of each sub-problem to avoid redundant computation, and combining these sub-solutions to construct the optimal global policy.

In the context of **Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning**, Dynamic Programming is the mathematical backbone of **Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)**, **Reinforcement Learning**, and **optimal control**. It assumes that the environment is fully known as a **model-based** approach, where the agent has complete knowledge of the state-transition function $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and the reward function $R(s, a, s')$.

The term *Dynamic Programming* was coined by **Richard Bellman** in the 1950s. In the AI context, DP is restricted to problems satisfying two structural properties:

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Optimal Substructure** | The optimal solution to a problem can be expressed as a combination of optimal solutions to its sub-problems. |
| **Overlapping Sub-problems** | The same sub-problem is encountered and solved many times; storing results prevents recomputation. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Mountain Cartographer**
>
> Imagine you are a cartographer mapping the *cheapest* hiking route from your village (start) to a distant city (goal) across a grid of 100 villages. The brute-force method is to try **every** possible path — trillions of combinations. 
>
> Dynamic Programming says: *"Start from the goal and work backward."* For each village, ask: *"What is the cheapest cost to reach the goal from here?"* You write that number on a board. When you encounter the same village from another direction, you simply **read** the stored value rather than recompute it. 
>
> This is exactly how DP in AI works for MDPs: it backs up value information from terminal states toward the start state, reusing computed values everywhere they are needed.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU-Highlighted)

- **State ($s$)** — A complete description of the environment at a given time.
- **Action ($a$)** — A decision the agent can take from state $s$.
- **Policy ($\pi$)** — A mapping from states to actions: $\pi(s) = a$.
- **Return ($G_t$)** — The cumulative discounted future reward from time $t$.
- **Value Function ($V^\pi(s)$)** — Expected return when starting in $s$ and following $\pi$.
- **Q-Function ($Q^\pi(s,a)$)** — Expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$, then following $\pi$.
- **Bellman Equation** — A recursive relation expressing a value in terms of successor values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Principle of Optimality (Bellman, 1957)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."* — **Richard Bellman**

This is the philosophical and mathematical cornerstone of DP. It guarantees that an **optimal trajectory** can be built by stitching together locally optimal sub-trajectories. If a problem violates this principle, DP cannot be applied directly.

## 2.2 The Two Core DP Algorithms

### A. Policy Iteration
Policy Iteration alternates between two steps until convergence:

1. **Policy Evaluation** — Given a policy $\pi$, compute the value function $V^\pi$ for every state by solving the Bellman expectation equation iteratively.
2. **Policy Improvement** — Given $V^\pi$, update the policy greedily with respect to the value function: $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right]$.

This cycle is guaranteed to converge to the **optimal policy $\pi^*$** in a finite MDP.

### B. Value Iteration
Value Iteration combines policy evaluation and improvement into a single update by using the **Bellman optimality equation** as an iterative operator:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[r + \gamma V_k(s')\right]
$$

It is faster per iteration than policy iteration and converges to $V^*$ in the limit.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Name | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted Return | Defines the agent's objective. $\gamma \in [0,1]$. |
| 2 | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \mid S_t = s\right]$ | State-Value Function | Expected return from $s$ under $\pi$. |
| 3 | $Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\right]$ | Action-Value Function | Expected return for action $a$ in $s$. |
| 4 | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right]$ | Bellman Expectation Eq. (for $V$) | Recursive form of $V^\pi$. |
| 5 | $Q^\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^\pi(s',a')\right]$ | Bellman Expectation Eq. (for $Q$) | Recursive form of $Q^\pi$. |
| 6 | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V^*(s')\right]$ | Bellman Optimality Eq. (for $V$) | Defines the optimal value function. |
| 7 | $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a')\right]$ | Bellman Optimality Eq. (for $Q$) | Defines the optimal action-value function. |
| 8 | $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s,a)$ | Greedy Optimal Policy | Extracts the optimal policy from $Q^{*}$. |
| 9 | $\vert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \vert < \epsilon$ | Convergence Check | Stopping criterion. |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{\ln(\epsilon(1-\gamma))}{\ln(\gamma)}$ | Iterations to Converge | Upper bound on iterations for $\gamma < 1$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** The symbol $\vert x \vert$ represents the absolute value of $x$. In LaTeX within tables, the `\|` symbol is avoided to prevent markdown-table parsing errors. Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How DP Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Robotics & Autonomous Navigation** | Path planning, gait optimization, and inverse dynamics in known terrain. |
| **Supply Chain & Inventory Control** | Optimal stock replenishment under stochastic demand. |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Viterbi algorithm for Hidden Markov Model decoding is a DP algorithm. |
| **Bioinformatics** | Sequence alignment (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman) uses DP. |
| **Computer Networks** | Shortest-path routing (Bellman–Ford algorithm) is a DP method. |
| **Control Systems** | Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) and Model Predictive Control (MPC) rely on DP theory. |
| **Game AI** | Backgammon, chess endgames, and Atari agents (foundation for TD-Gammon). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Expectation Equation

We start from the formal definition of the state-value function:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_t \mid S_t = s\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the return $G_t$ using its definition.**

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expanded return into the expectation.**

$$
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the linearity of expectation.**

$$
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right] + \gamma \,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Condition on the first action and the next state.**

The expectation over the future can be decomposed by marginalizing over action $A_t$ and next state $S_{t+1}$:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[r + \gamma \,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right]\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that the inner expectation is precisely $V^\pi(s')$.**

$$
\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right] = V^\pi(s')
$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the final form (Bellman Expectation Equation).**

$$
V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[r + \gamma \,V^\pi(s')\right]
$$

This is the **recursive backbone** of every DP algorithm. The value of a state equals the immediate expected reward plus the discounted value of the next state.

## 3.2 Full Numerical Worked Example — 4×4 Grid World (Value Iteration)
