Consider a **4×4 grid world** (states numbered 1 to 16). The agent starts at any cell, terminal states are $S_{16}$ (goal, reward $+1$) and $S_{1}$ (pit, reward $-1$). Other transitions give $r = -0.04$ (small step penalty to encourage speed). The discount factor is $\gamma = 0.9$. The action set is $\{\uparrow, \downarrow, \leftarrow, \rightarrow\}$; intended actions succeed with probability **0.8** and slip sideways with probability **0.1** each.

We want to compute $V^*(s)$ for a sample non-terminal state, say $s = 6$, by one value-iteration step, assuming current values $V_0(s) = 0$ for all non-terminal states and $V_0(1) = V_0(16) = 0$.

### Step-by-step application of the Bellman Optimality Update

For each candidate action $a$, compute the action-value:

$$
Q(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V(s')\right]
$$

$$
V_{1}(6) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q(6, a)
$$

Assume from $s = 6$ the four actions lead to neighbouring states with the following deterministic structure (for clarity, we omit stochastic slips in this sub-step):

- Action $\rightarrow$: goes to $s' = 7$, reward $r = -0.04$
- Action $\leftarrow$: goes to $s' = 5$, reward $r = -0.04$
- Action $\uparrow$: goes to $s' = 2$, reward $r = -0.04$
- Action $\downarrow$: goes to $s' = 10$, reward $r = -0.04$

Compute each Q-value using $V_0 = 0$ for all non-terminals:

$$
Q(6, \rightarrow) = 0.8 \cdot (-0.04 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.1 \cdot (-0.04 + 0.9 \cdot 0) + 0.1 \cdot (-0.04 + 0.9 \cdot 0)
$$

$$
Q(6, \rightarrow) = (0.8 + 0.1 + 0.1) \cdot (-0.04) = 1.0 \cdot (-0.04) = -0.04
$$

By symmetry of the grid at iteration 0, all four actions give:

$$
Q(6, \uparrow) = Q(6, \downarrow) = Q(6, \leftarrow) = -0.04
$$

Therefore, the greedy update yields:

$$
V_{1}(6) = \max\{-0.04, -0.04, -0.04, -0.04\} = -0.04
$$

**After many iterations**, the values propagate from the goal and the pit. For example, after convergence, the optimal value of a state two steps from the goal becomes:

$$
V^{*}(s) = r + \gamma \, V^{*}(\text{next}) = -0.04 + 0.9 \cdot 1.0 = 0.86
$$

This example demonstrates how a single scalar update — using only **local information** — propagates the optimal value across the entire state space.

## 3.3 Policy Improvement Theorem — Step-by-Step Proof

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem.** If for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$:
> $$Q^\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^\pi(s)$$
> then the new policy $\pi'$ is at least as good as $\pi$: $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^\pi(s)$ for all $s$.

**Step 1 — Expand the Q-value of $\pi'$ using the Bellman expectation equation.**

$$
Q^\pi(s, \pi'(s)) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the inequality.**

$$
V^\pi(s) \leq \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V^\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Define a sequence $V_0, V_1, V_2, \dots$ where $V_0 = V^\pi$ and:**

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V_k(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = \pi'(s)\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Show by induction that $V_k(s) \leq V_{k+1}(s)$ for all $k$ and all $s$.**

Base case: true by assumption. Inductive step: if $V_k \leq V_{k+1}$, then by monotonicity of expectation, $V_{k+1} \leq V_{k+2}$.

**Step 5 — Recognize the limit of this sequence is $V^{\pi'}$.**

The recursion in Step 3 is precisely the Bellman expectation equation for $\pi'$. By the **Contraction Mapping Theorem**, the sequence converges to the unique fixed point $V^{\pi'}$.

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = V_0(s) \leq V_1(s) \leq V_2(s) \leq \dots \leq V^{\pi'}(s)
$$

Hence $V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for every state. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Policy Iteration | Value Iteration |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Rule** | Two nested steps: Evaluate $V^\pi$, then improve $\pi$ | Single Bellman-optimality backup per state |
| **Per-Iteration Cost** | High (full policy evaluation) | Low (one sweep over states) |
| **Number of Iterations** | Few (often < 10) | Many (depends on $\gamma$) |
| **Stores** | Both $V$ and $\pi$ | Only $V$ |
| **Convergence Guarantee** | Yes, finite MDP | Yes, asymptotic |
| **Best Used When** | State space is small | State space is large, $\gamma$ near 1 |
| **Optimality** | Reaches exact $\pi^*$ | Reaches $V^*$, then extract $\pi^*$ by one greedy pass |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Policy Iteration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Arbitrary Policy pi_0] --> B[Policy Evaluation: Compute V for pi_k]
    B --> C[Policy Improvement: For each state pick greedy action]
    C --> D{Is Policy Stable?}
    D -- No --> E[Set pi_k plus 1 equal to new policy] --> B
    D -- Yes --> F[Output Optimal Policy pi star and Optimal Value V star]
    styleA[Initialize Arbitrary Policy pi_0]
    styleB[Policy Evaluation: Compute V for pi_k]
    styleC[Policy Improvement: For each state pick greedy action]
    styleD[Is Policy Stable]
    styleE[Set pi_k plus 1 equal to new policy]
    styleF[Output Optimal Policy pi star and Optimal Value V star]
```

## 4.2 Value Iteration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize V0 s to 0 for all s] --> B[For k = 0 1 2 until convergence]
    B --> C[For every state s in S]
    C --> D[Compute Q s a for all actions a]
    D --> E[Set V k plus 1 s to max over a of Q s a]
    E --> F{Change less than epsilon everywhere}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Extract pi star s equals argmax over a of Q star s a]
    styleA[Initialize V0 s to 0 for all s]
    styleB[For k = 0 1 2 until convergence]
    styleC[For every state s in S]
    styleD[Compute Q s a for all actions a]
    styleE[Set V k plus 1 s to max over a of Q s a]
    styleF[Change less than epsilon everywhere]
    styleG[Extract pi star s equals argmax over a of Q star s a]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Dynamic Programming. State the two properties that a problem must satisfy for DP to be applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Dynamic Programming is a method for solving complex problems by breaking them into smaller overlapping sub-problems, solving each sub-problem once, and storing the results in a table to be reused, thereby avoiding redundant computation. In AI/ML, DP is used to compute optimal policies in Markov Decision Processes when the model of the environment is fully known.
>
> **Two Properties (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Optimal Substructure** — An optimal solution to the problem contains within it optimal solutions to its sub-problems.
> 2. **Overlapping Sub-problems** — The recursion involves solving the same sub-problem repeatedly; DP exploits this by memoization or tabulation.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the principle of optimality? Why is it essential for Dynamic Programming?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Optimality (2 marks):** Stated by Richard Bellman in 1957, the principle says: *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."*
>
> **Importance (1 mark):** It guarantees that an optimal solution can be built by composing optimal sub-solutions. Without this property, the recursive decomposition that DP relies on is invalid, and the algorithm cannot guarantee finding the global optimum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Formula marks (≈3 marks):** Students frequently lose marks by **not writing the discounted factor $\gamma$** in the Bellman backup. Always include it.
> - **Algorithm marks (≈3 marks):** When describing Policy Iteration, students often skip the **convergence check** step. Examiners specifically look for the statement: *"stop when the policy is stable (no change between $\pi_k$ and $\pi_{k+1}$)."*
> - **Diagram marks (≈2 marks):** A neat backup-diagram or state-transition graph earns easy marks. Skipping it is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> - **Notation discipline:** Do not confuse $V^\pi$ (value of state under policy $\pi$) with $V^*$ (optimal value). Mixing them is a 1-mark penalty.

---

### **Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4] (14 Marks)`**

**(a) Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^\pi(s)$. Clearly show every step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the definition of the state-value function:

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,G_t \,\mid\, S_t = s\,\right]
$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Expand the discounted return $G_t$:

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots = R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute into the expectation:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\,R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \,\mid\, S_t = s\,\right]
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply linearity of expectation:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right] \;+\; \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\right]
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Condition on action $A_t = a$ and next state $S_{t+1} = s'$:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma\,\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right]\,\right]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Recognize that the inner expectation equals $V^\pi(s')$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'\right] = V^\pi(s')
$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Final Bellman Expectation Equation:

$$
V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)\!\left[\,r + \gamma \,V^\pi(s')\,\right]
$$

This is the **recursive decomposition** that allows the value of any state to be expressed in terms of the values of its successors.

---

**(b) Explain the Policy Iteration algorithm with a neat block diagram. State its convergence property. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept (1 mark):** Policy Iteration is a DP algorithm that alternates between **policy evaluation** (computing the value of a given policy) and **policy improvement** (making the policy greedy with respect to that value), until the policy stops changing.

**Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):** Start with an arbitrary deterministic policy $\pi_0$ and a small threshold $\theta > 0$.

**Step 2 — Policy Evaluation (2 marks):** Given $\pi_k$, compute $V^{\pi_k}$ by repeated application of the Bellman expectation equation until the maximum change $\Delta = \max_s \lvert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \rvert$ falls below $\theta$:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi_k(a \mid s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V_k(s')\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Policy Improvement (2 marks):** For each state $s$, select the action that maximizes the Q-value under the current value function:

$$
\pi_{k+1}(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V^{\pi_k}(s')\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Check (0.5 mark):** If $\pi_{k+1} = \pi_k$ for all states, stop; else return to Step 2.

**Step 5 — Convergence Property (0.5 mark):** *Policy Iteration converges to the optimal policy $\pi^*$ in a finite number of iterations for any finite MDP.*

**Block Diagram (compensates for layout):** See the **Mermaid Policy Iteration diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. The cycle is: Initialize $\to$ Evaluate $V$ $\to$ Improve $\pi$ $\to$ Check stability $\to$ (loop or stop).

---

### **Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4] (14 Marks)` — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*(s)$ from first principles. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Begin with the definition of the optimal state-value function:

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Since $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) Q^{\pi}(s,a)$, the max over policies is achieved by always picking the action with the highest Q-value, i.e., the optimal policy is **deterministic and greedy**:

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} Q^{*}(s,a)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Recall the definition of the optimal action-value function:

$$
Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\right]
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute the expression for $Q^{*}$ back into the $V^{*}$ definition:

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}(s)} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\right]
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$**. It is a system of non-linear equations (one per state) because of the $\max$ operator. The **Value Iteration algorithm** solves it by treating it as an update rule:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V_k(s')\right]
$$

By the **Contraction Mapping Theorem**, the sequence $\{V_k\}$ converges to the unique fixed point $V^{*}$.

---

**(b) Explain the Value Iteration algorithm with a suitable example. State the stopping criterion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept (1 mark):** Value Iteration combines the two steps of Policy Iteration into a single Bellman-optimality backup. It directly computes the optimal value function $V^{*}$ without explicitly maintaining a policy.

**Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):** Set $V_0(s) = 0$ for all non-terminal states, $V_0(\text{terminal}) = 0$, and choose $\theta > 0$ (e.g., $\theta = 10^{-5}$).

**Step 2 — Iterative Update (2 marks):** For $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ do:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V_k(s')\right] \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Stopping Criterion (1 mark):** Stop when:

$$
\Delta = \max_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \lvert V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s) \rvert < \theta
$$

**Step 4 — Policy Extraction (1 mark):** After convergence, extract the optimal policy in a single greedy pass:

$$
\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\!\left[r + \gamma V^{*}(s')\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (1 mark):** *For the 4×4 grid world with $r = -0.04$, $\gamma = 0.9$, terminal $+1$ and $-1$:* after the first sweep, every non-terminal state takes the value $-0.04$. After many iterations, the values form a gradient pointing from the pit and toward the goal, and the greedy policy traces the shortest safe path. The number of iterations required to achieve $\epsilon$-accuracy is bounded by:

$$
T = \frac{\ln(\epsilon(1-\gamma))}{\ln(\gamma)}
$$

For $\epsilon = 0.001$ and $\gamma = 0.9$, this gives approximately $T \approx 65$ iterations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dynamic Programming**

- **DP** = method for **sequential decision-making** under a **known model**; built on **Bellman equations**.
- The two structural requirements are **optimal substructure** and **overlapping sub-problems**.
- The **principle of optimality** (Bellman, 1957) is the philosophical foundation; without it DP is invalid.
- The **Bellman Expectation Equation** gives $V^\pi$ and $Q^\pi$ under any fixed policy $\pi$.
- The **Bellman Optimality Equation** gives $V^*$ and $Q^*$ for the **optimal** policy, and contains a $\max$ operator.
- **Policy Iteration** = Policy Evaluation $\to$ Policy Improvement $\to$ check stability. Converges in **finite steps** for finite MDPs.
- **Value Iteration** = single Bellman-optimality backup. Converges asymptotically, governed by the **Contraction Mapping Theorem**.
- Always include the **discount factor $\gamma$** in backups — losing it costs marks.
- Convergence rate of value iteration scales as $\gamma^{k}$ — close to 1 means slow convergence.
- **Asynchronous DP** sweeps update states in-place and can dramatically speed up convergence.
- DP is the **theoretical foundation** of all model-based reinforcement learning; model-free methods (MC, TD) approximate it when the model is unknown.
- The **Viterbi algorithm**, **Bellman–Ford shortest path**, and **sequence alignment (Needleman–Wunsch)** are all special cases of DP.
- For KTU 2024 Scheme, **derivation of the Bellman equations** and **algorithm-level description of Policy and Value Iteration** are the **highest-weight** sub-topics (≈ 60% of Module 4 marks).
